name: Sacubitril/Valsartan
moa: Sacubitril inhibits neprilysin (↑ natriuretic peptides); valsartan blocks AT1 receptor (↓ vasoconstriction/RAAS)
indications: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF); PARADIGM-HF showed superiority to enalapril; CKD progression reduction
dosing: PO: 24/26 mg BID, titrate to target 97/103 mg BID; Renal/hepatic impairment: start 24/26 mg daily
renal: CrCl <30: start 24/26 mg daily; dialysis: caution; ARNI-induced hyperkalemia risk with CKD stage 4-5
hepatic: Moderate-severe: reduce initial dose; start 24/26 mg daily
ades: Hyperkalemia (monitor K+ and creatinine); hypotension; cough (less common than ACEi); angioedema (rare but serious); hyporenal azotemia in CKD
monitoring: K+ and SCr at baseline, 1 week, monthly × 3, then q3 months; BP; eGFR; NYHA class/symptoms
ddi: NSAIDs (↑ renal injury/hyperkalemia); ACEi/ARBs (contraindicated); potassium supplements/K+-sparing agents (↑ hyperkalemia); lithium (↑ levels)
pearls: PARADIGM-HF (2014): reduced mortality/hospitalization vs enalapril. Do NOT use with ACEi/ARB (↑ angioedema risk). K+ monitoring critical. Black box: pregnancy (teratogenic).
trials: PARADIGM-HF, DAPA-HF, EMPEROR-Reduced
pk: Sacubitril: prodrug → LBQ657 (active) by esterases; t½ sacubitril ~1.4 h, LBQ657 ~11.5 h. Valsartan: t½ ~10 h. Protein binding >94%. Excretion: sacubitril renal 52–68%; valsartan fecal 83%. No CYP interactions.
bbw: PREGNANCY: Can cause fetal harm/death. Discontinue immediately if pregnancy detected.
highyield:
• Requires 36-hour washout from ACEi before starting (angioedema risk)
• PARADIGM-HF: 20% RRR in CV death/HF hospitalization vs enalapril
• Only for HFrEF (EF ≤40%); NOT for HFpEF based on PARAGON-HF
• K⁺ and creatinine monitoring mandatory — similar to ACEi/ARB

name: Ivabradine
class: If-Channel Blocker (Hyperpolarization-Activated Cyclic Nucleotide-Gated Channel Inhibitor)
moa: Selective inhibitor of I(f) current in sinoatrial node; slows heart rate via diastolic depolarization (no negative inotropic effect)
indications: Chronic HFrEF with HR ≥70 bpm (EF ≤35%) in sinus rhythm; symptom reduction only; SHIFT trial primary outcome
dosing: PO: 5 mg BID, titrate to target 2.5-7.5 mg BID based on HR (target HR 50-60 bpm); Monitor HR; may need dose reduction if HR <50
renal: No dose adjustment needed; CrCl <15: avoid (limited data)
hepatic: Child-Pugh A: no change; B: caution, max 5 mg BID; C: contraindicated
ades: Phosphenes (visual symptoms ~25%, transient); bradycardia; atrial fibrillation (careful monitoring); conduction abnormalities
monitoring: HR q4 weeks during titration, then q3 months; ECG baseline and after dose changes; visual symptoms (usually resolve)
ddi: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, verapamil): max 2.5 mg BID; weak inhibitors: caution; no interaction with ACEi/beta-blockers
pearls: SHIFT (2010): HR reduction, no mortality benefit but improved HF hospitalizations. Phosphenes (light flashes) common but reversible. Only for SN rhythm (NOT AFib). Bradycardia risk.
trials: SHIFT, SIGNIFY
pk: t½ ~2 h (active metabolite ~11 h). Bioavailability ~40% (first-pass). Protein binding 70%. Metabolized by CYP3A4 (major). Excretion: biliary/fecal ~83%, renal ~4%.
highyield:
• ONLY for sinus rhythm patients — contraindicated in AFib/flutter
• SHIFT trial: reduced composite CV death/HF hospitalization (HR reduction benefit)
• Phosphenes (visual brightness) in ~25% — transient, reversible
• Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, verapamil) → cut dose by 50%

name: Spironolactone
moa: Competitively blocks aldosterone at mineralocorticoid receptor in collecting duct; potassium-sparing diuretic
indications: HFrEF (mortality/hospitalization reduction); primary hyperaldosteronism; resistant hypertension; cirrhosis with ascites
dosing: PO: 12.5-25 mg daily, max 50 mg daily; titrate by K+ tolerance; RALES trial used 25 mg daily
renal: eGFR 30-60: use cautiously, monitor K+ closely; eGFR <30: generally contraindicated; hyperkalemia risk increases exponentially
hepatic: Cirrhosis/advanced disease: caution, ↑ risk hyperkalemia; dose adjustment case-by-case
ades: Hyperkalemia (most serious; esp. with ACEi/ARB/CKD); gynecomastia (20-50% in men, reversible); menstrual irregularities; sexual dysfunction; metabolic acidosis
monitoring: K+ and SCr at baseline, day 3, 1 week, 4 weeks, then monthly × 3, then q3 months; BP; eGFR; baseline ECG if K+ >5.5
ddi: ACEi/ARBs/ARNI/NSAIDs/trimethoprim/tacrolimus (↑ hyperkalemia risk); monitor closely; avoid potassium supplements
pearls: RALES (1999): ↓ mortality 30% in HFrEF. Gynecomastia common but reversible after d/c. K+ monitoring is CRITICAL (life-threatening if >6.5). Avoid triple whammy (ACEI/ARB + MRA + NSAID).
trials: RALES, EMPHASIS-HF
pk: Prodrug → active metabolites (canrenone, 7α-thiomethyl spironolactone). t½ ~1.4 h (canrenone ~16.5 h). Protein binding 91%. Extensively metabolized (CYP not major). Excretion: renal (metabolites).
bbw: CARCINOGENESIS: Tumorigenic in rats at high chronic doses. Use at lowest effective dose for shortest duration.
highyield:
• RALES trial: 30% mortality reduction in severe HFrEF (EF ≤35%)
• Gynecomastia in ~10% men — switch to eplerenone if problematic
• Monitor K⁺ and creatinine at 1 week, 1 month, quarterly
• Hold/reduce if K⁺ >5.5 mEq/L or SCr >2.5 mg/dL

name: Eplerenone
moa: Selective mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (11β-HSD2 binding minimizes androgen antagonism); potassium-sparing
indications: Acute MI with systolic LV dysfunction (EF ≤40%); HFrEF (EMPHASIS-HF); resistant hypertension (adjunctive)
dosing: PO: 25 mg daily, titrate to 50 mg daily (target, post-MI at day 3-14); K+ and SCr dependent; max 50 mg daily
renal: eGFR 30-60: start 25 mg every other day; eGFR <30: contraindicated; hyperkalemia risk
hepatic: No dose adjustment; severe disease: caution
ades: Hyperkalemia (less common than spironolactone due to selectivity); gynecomastia (rare <1%); diarrhea; headache; dizziness
monitoring: K+ and SCr baseline, 3 days, 1 week, 4 weeks, 8 weeks, then periodically; BP; eGFR q1-2 weeks × 2 if dose >50 mg
ddi: ACEi/ARBs/NSAIDs/potassium supplements (↑ hyperkalemia); CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, verapamil, diltiazem): avoid or monitor closely
pearls: EMPHASIS-HF (2011): selective advantage ↓ gynecomastia vs spironolactone (1% vs 15-20%). Post-MI: start within 2 weeks, shown mortality ↓. Expensive (generic available). K+ monitoring still essential.
trials: EMPHASIS-HF, EMPHASIS-HF-Chronic, RALES
pk: t½ ~4–6 h. Bioavailability ~69%. Protein binding 49%. Metabolized by CYP3A4. Excretion: renal 67%, fecal 32%.
highyield:
• EPHESUS (post-MI, EF ≤40%): 15% RRR in CV mortality; EMPHASIS-HF (HFrEF): 37% RRR in HF hospitalization
• Fewer hormonal side effects than spironolactone (no gynecomastia)
• Contraindicated with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, itraconazole)
• Avoid if K⁺ >5.0 mEq/L, SCr >2.0 (males) or >1.8 (females), or eGFR <30

name: Carvedilol
class: Non-Selective Beta-Blocker with Alpha-1 Blocking Activity
moa: Non-selective β1/β2 antagonist + alpha-1 blockade; reduces vasoconstriction and sympathetic tone; reduces cardiac workload
indications: HFrEF (mortality ↓ COPERNICUS/CIBIS/MERIT-HF); post-MI; hypertension; angina; cardiomyopathy
dosing: PO: 3.125 mg BID, titrate q2 weeks to 6.25, 12.5, 25 mg BID (target); extended-release: 10-80 mg daily
renal: No dose adjustment; caution in severe renal disease
hepatic: Severe cirrhosis: caution, reduce dose; avoid if decompensated
ades: Bradycardia/AV block; hypotension; dizziness (esp. with alpha blockade); fatigue; bronchospasm (non-selective); cold extremities
monitoring: HR, BP q2 weeks during titration; EF at baseline, 3 months; signs of decompensation; SCr/BUN (baseline, periodic)
ddi: Non-dihydropyridine CCB (diltiazem/verapamil): ↑ bradycardia/AV block; clonidine (abrupt d/c ↑ rebound HTN); NSAIDs (↓ efficacy); digoxin (↑ levels)
pearls: COPERNICUS (2001): improved EF, ↓ mortality 35% even in advanced HF. Slow titration essential (angioedema/syncope risk if too rapid). Alpha-1 block helps systemic vasodilation. Non-selective: contraindicated in asthma/COPD.
trials: COPERNICUS, CIBIS, MERIT-HF
pk: t½ ~7–10 h. Bioavailability ~25–35% (first-pass, food ↑ absorption). Protein binding ~98%. Metabolized by CYP2D6 and CYP2C9. Excretion: fecal ~60%, renal <2%.
highyield:
• Non-selective β-blocker + α1-blocker → more vasodilation than metoprolol
• Take with food to slow absorption and reduce orthostatic hypotension
• COPERNICUS, MERIT-HF: proven mortality benefit in HFrEF
• One of only 3 β-blockers proven in HFrEF: carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol

name: Metoprolol Succinate
moa: β1-selective adrenergic antagonist at therapeutic doses; reduces HR, contractility, BP; extended-release (ER) for sustained β-blockade
indications: HFrEF (mortality ↓ MERIT-HF); post-MI; hypertension; angina; tachycardia; atrial fibrillation rate control
dosing: PO ER: 25-50 mg daily (for HF), titrate q2 weeks to 190 mg daily (MERIT-HF target); IR (immediate-release): BID dosing available
renal: No dose adjustment; caution CrCl <30
hepatic: Severe disease: caution, reduce dose
ades: Bradycardia; hypotension; fatigue; sexual dysfunction; bronchospasm (less common than non-selective); depression
monitoring: HR, BP q2 weeks during titration; EF; signs of HF decompensation; SCr/BUN periodic
ddi: Diltiazem/verapamil (↑ AV block); clonidine (↑ rebound HTN on d/c); NSAIDs (↓ antihypertensive effect); digoxin (monitor levels)
pearls: MERIT-HF (1999): ↓ mortality 34% in HFrEF. USE SUCCINATE (IR/ER), NOT TARTRATE (immediate-release, less efficacy in HF). Extended-release preferred for HF (better compliance). Cardioselective better tolerated in pulmonary disease vs non-selective.
trials: MERIT-HF, COPERNICUS
pk: Extended-release. t½ ~3–7 h. Bioavailability ~50% (first-pass). Protein binding ~12%. Metabolized by CYP2D6 (primary). Excretion: renal ~95% (metabolites).
highyield:
• MERIT-HF: 34% RRR in all-cause mortality vs placebo in HFrEF
• Cardioselective β1 blocker — preferred over carvedilol in reactive airway disease
• Do NOT abruptly discontinue — risk of rebound angina/MI
• CYP2D6 poor metabolizers: higher blood levels; drugs like fluoxetine ↑ levels

name: Digoxin
class: Cardiac Glycoside (Inotropic Agent + AV Node Blocker)
moa: Na+/K+-ATPase inhibition → ↑ intracellular Ca2+ → ↑ contractility; also vagomimetic (↑ AV block/bradycardia via vagal stimulation)
indications: HFrEF + AFib with RVR (dual benefit: inotropic + rate control); HF (symptom relief only, no mortality ↓); SVT (particularly adenosine-refractory)
dosing: Load: 8-12 mcg/kg divided q6h × 2-3 doses (PO) or 4-6 mcg/kg IV divided doses; Maint: 0.125-0.25 mg daily PO; AUC-guided dosing for CKD
renal: Critically important: CrCl <60: reduce dose significantly; CrCl <30: 50% of normal; CrCl <10: 25% of normal; dialysis: no HD removal; post-dialysis supplement
hepatic: Cirrhosis/HF: reduced clearance, caution; start low, titrate slowly
ades: Narrow therapeutic index (0.5-2 ng/mL); toxicity: arrhythmias (PACs, PVCs, bigeminy, complete heart block), nausea/vomiting/anorexia, visual disturbances (yellow vision), confusion; hyperkalemia worsens toxicity
monitoring: Digoxin level (trough, 6-8h after dose): therapeutic 0.5-2.0 ng/mL (HF) or 1.0-2.0 ng/mL (AFib rate); K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, SCr/CrCl baseline and periodically; ECG baseline (watch PR/QT); Symptom assessment
ddi: Amiodarone/verapamil/diltiazem (↑ digoxin levels 20-50%); thiazide/loop diuretics (↓ K+, ↑ toxicity risk); NSAIDs (↑ levels); amphotericin B (↓ K+); quinidine (↑ digoxin levels 100%)
pearls: DIG trial (1997): no mortality ↓, but ↓ hospitalizations/symptoms in HF. NARROW THERAPEUTIC INDEX = HIGH TOXICITY RISK. K+ hypokalemia critical risk factor. Toxicity mimics arrhythmias/hyperkalemia. Old drug but still useful in HF+AFib. Monitor levels religiously.
trials: DIG Trial, Digitalis Investigation Group
pk: t½ ~36–48 h (renal-dependent). Bioavailability ~70–80% (tablets). Protein binding ~25%. Not metabolized by CYP. Excretion: renal ~60–80% unchanged. Volume of distribution: large (~7 L/kg).
highyield:
• Narrow therapeutic index: target serum level 0.5–0.9 ng/mL for HF (>2 ng/mL = toxicity)
• Toxicity signs: GI (N/V, anorexia), bradyarrhythmias, visual (yellow-green halos), bidirectional VT
• Hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, and hypothyroidism all increase toxicity risk
• Not first-line — add-on for rate control in AFib or persistent HF symptoms
• Quinidine, amiodarone, verapamil, spironolactone: all ↑ digoxin levels

name: Amiodarone
class: Class III Antiarrhythmic (Potassium Channel Blocker) + Multi-Channel Effects
moa: K+ channel blockade (Class III: prolongs action potential/refractory period); also Na+ blockade (Class I), β-blockade (Class II), non-selective Ca2+ blockade (Class IV)
indications: Life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias (VT/VF); AFib (rate/rhythm control); refractory SVT; cardiomyopathy + arrhythmias; ICD shock reduction
dosing: Load: 150-600 mg IV over 20 min - 2h (VT/VF), repeated q5min PRN; PO: 800-1600 mg daily × 1-3 weeks, then 600-800 daily × 1 month, then 200-400 mg daily (maint)
renal: Minimal renal clearance; dose adjustment minimal; hepatic metabolism (CYP3A4/CYP2C8) dominant
hepatic: Hepatic disease: caution, accumulates; cirrhosis: reduce dose by 25-50%
ades: Pulmonary toxicity (1-5%: pneumonitis, fibrosis, acute dyspnea); hypothyroidism (20-30%); hyperthyroidism (2%); hepatotoxicity; ophthalmic (corneal microdeposits, optic neuritis); photosensitivity; blue-gray skin discoloration; QT prolongation; bradycardia/AV block; peripheral neuropathy
monitoring: Baseline: CXR, PFTs, TFTs, LFTs, ECG; then TFTs q6 months, LFTs q6 months, PFTs annually (if symptoms: immediately); Sx of pulmonary toxicity; signs of hypothyroidism; ophthalmic exams (if symptoms); SCr/K+; QTc interval
ddi: Warfarin (↑ INR 30-50%); digoxin (↑ levels 100%, ↓ dose); beta-blockers (↑ bradycardia); diltiazem/verapamil (↑ AV block); CYP3A4 substrates (simvastatin, tacrolimus, clarithromycin: ↑ levels); QT-prolonging drugs (↑ Torsades risk)
pearls: Most effective antiarrhythmic but HIGHEST toxicity risk. DO NOT use for asymptomatic arrhythmias (Flecainide, Sotalol preferred). Pulmonary/thyroid toxicity serious and irreversible. Class III pure: prolongs refractoriness (multi-channel makes it unique). IV for acute VT/VF. Complex PK (very long half-life ~40 days: steady state 4-6 weeks). Drug interactions MAJOR.
trials: ARREST, ALIVE, ACLS
pk: t½ extremely long: 40–55 days (range 26–107 days). Bioavailability ~50% (variable). Protein binding >96%. Metabolized by CYP3A4/CYP2C8 → active desethylamiodarone. Excretion: biliary/fecal. Lipophilic — accumulates in tissue.
bbw: PULMONARY TOXICITY (may be fatal). HEPATOTOXICITY (liver failure reported). PROARRHYTHMIA (ventricular arrhythmias). Intended only for life-threatening arrhythmias when alternatives inadequate.
highyield:
• Most potent antiarrhythmic — Vaughan-Williams Class III (also I, II, IV properties)
• Thyroid monitoring mandatory: causes both hypo- and hyperthyroidism (iodine-rich)
• LFTs, PFTs, TFTs, eye exams at baseline and annually
• Drug interactions extensive: warfarin (↑ INR ~35–50%), digoxin (↑ levels ~70%), statins
• Loading phase required due to huge volume of distribution; full effect takes weeks

name: Dronedarone
moa: Multi-channel blockade: K+ (Class III), Na+ (Class I), non-dihydropyridine Ca2+ blocker (Class IV), non-selective beta antagonism (Class II); benzofuran backbone (no iodine)
indications: Paroxysmal/persistent AFib (rhythm/rate control, ↓ hospitalization); HFrEF + AFib (reduced hospitalizations); post-cardioversion maintenance
dosing: PO: 400 mg BID with meals; reduce to 600 mg daily if HFrEF (SHIFT-AF); max 800 mg daily
renal: CrCl <15: avoid (no data); mild-moderate: no adjustment
hepatic: Moderate-severe: contraindicated; Child-Pugh B-C: avoid
ades: Bradycardia; QT prolongation (Torsades risk ↑ with electrolyte abnormalities); GI upset; asthenia; hepatotoxicity (rare but serious); photosensitivity; neuropathy (less common than amiodarone)
monitoring: Baseline: LFTs, SCr, K+, Mg2+, ECG; then LFTs q6 months; K+/Mg2+ baseline/periodic; HR/BP; QTc interval; symptoms of HF
ddi: CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, verapamil): ↑ levels 2-3×; avoid strong inhibitors; diltiazem/verapamil (↑ bradycardia); warfarin (↑ INR); beta-blockers (↑ AV block); potassium-raising agents (↑ Torsades)
pearls: PAFAF (2008), PERMANENT (2011): ↓ AFib recurrence, ↓ hospitalization (not mortality). NO iodine (unlike amiodarone) = no thyroid toxicity or PFT decline = better tolerability. NOT for HFrEF with EF <30 (SHIFT-AF mortality ↑, though SHIFT-AF limited). QT monitoring important. Better safety profile than amiodarone; less toxicity.
trials: PAFAF, PERMANENT, ARISTOTLE
pk: t½ ~13–19 h. Bioavailability ~15% fasted, ~84% with food. Protein binding >98%. Metabolized by CYP3A4. Excretion: fecal ~84%, renal ~6%.
bbw: CONTRAINDICATED in NYHA Class IV or decompensated HF (doubled mortality in ANDROMEDA). CONTRAINDICATED in permanent AFib (increased CV mortality, stroke, HF hospitalization in PALLAS).
highyield:
• Non-iodinated analog of amiodarone — fewer thyroid/pulmonary side effects
• ATHENA: reduced AFib hospitalization, but NO mortality benefit
• Causes SCr elevation (~0.1 mg/dL) via tubular secretion inhibition — not true renal decline
• Contraindicated with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors and Class I/III antiarrhythmics
• Do NOT use in permanent AFib — PALLAS trial stopped early for harm

name: Sotalol
moa: K+ channel blockade (Class III: prolongs action potential duration) + non-selective beta-1/2 blockade (Class II); primarily Class III effect at therapeutic doses
indications: Documented life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias (VT/VF post-MI); AFib (rate control + rhythm maintenance); SVT
dosing: PO: 80 mg BID, titrate by QTc/response to 80-160 mg BID (max 320-480 mg daily in divided doses); IV (Sotylize): not widely used
renal: CRITICAL: CrCl >60: normal dosing; CrCl 40-60: q12h; CrCl <40: q24h or contraindicated; dialysis: consider 40 mg post-HD
hepatic: No specific dose adjustment; use caution
ades: QT prolongation (Torsades de Pointes risk ↑ with hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia); bradycardia; hypotension; fatigue; sexual dysfunction; bronchospasm (non-selective beta-blockade, esp. in COPD/asthma)
monitoring: Baseline ECG (QTc), K+, Mg2+, SCr, BUN; post-dose q3 days (QTc <500 ms, ideally <480); HR <50-55 BPM: caution; K+ >5.5 or <3.5: risk factor for Torsades; EF (baseline, periodic); symptoms of arrhythmia
ddi: QT-prolonging drugs (macrolides, antipsychotics, fluoroquinolones, domperidone: ↑ Torsades); hypokalemia-inducing agents (loop diuretics, thiazide, amphotericin: ↑ Torsades); NSAIDs (↓ efficacy); anti-arrhythmics (additive effects, caution)
pearls: Non-selective beta-blocker (bronchospasm risk in COPD). SEVERE Torsades risk if QTc >500 ms or K+ deranged. Renal clearance dependent = MAJOR DOSE ADJUSTMENT in CKD. More selective than amiodarone (lower toxicity). CrCl <40: generally contraindicated. Do NOT use if baseline QTc >450 ms (male) or >460 ms (female).
trials: ESVEM, SWORD
pk: t½ ~12 h. Bioavailability ~90–100%. Protein binding minimal (<10%). NOT metabolized by liver/CYP. Excretion: renal ~80% unchanged (adjust for CrCl).
bbw: PROARRHYTHMIA: Life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias (TdP). Initiation/dose increases require in-hospital monitoring (QTc ≥500 ms = stop).
highyield:
• Class III antiarrhythmic + non-selective β-blocker (Class II)
• Dose-dependent QT prolongation — monitor QTc and electrolytes
• Contraindicated if CrCl <40 mL/min (dose reduces to q24–36h if CrCl 40–60)
• Hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia dramatically increase TdP risk — correct before use

name: Ticagrelor
moa: Reversible binding to P2Y12 ADP receptor on platelets; active metabolite contributes; faster onset (1 h) and offset (3-5 days) vs clopidogrel
indications: Acute coronary syndrome (ACS: STEMI/NSTEMI/UA); post-PCI stent (12 months); stable CAD (PLATO-based); PLATO showed superiority to clopidogrel
dosing: PO: 180 mg loading (if ACS), then 60 mg BID maintenance (standard) or 90 mg BID (post-MI if >60y or <60kg); DAPT × 12 months typical
renal: No dose adjustment; severe renal impairment (eGFR <30): caution (increased bleeding risk)
hepatic: Avoid if severe hepatic disease (no data)
ades: Bleeding (↑ vs clopidogrel in PLATO, but offset by ↓ CV deaths); bradycardia (2-3%, vagal, often asymptomatic); dyspnea (10%, dose-dependent, reversible); bradyarrhythmias (AV block rare); gout (hyperuricemia)
monitoring: Baseline CBC, INR (if on warfarin); assess for signs of bleeding; HR (bradycardia); ECG if bradycardic symptoms; renal function; liver function; symptoms of dyspnea
ddi: Warfarin/anticoagulants (↑ bleeding, though used together in some trials); aspirin >100 mg/day (↑ bleeding, use ≤100 mg/day); NSAIDs (↑ bleeding); macrolides/antifungals/protease inhibitors (CYP3A4 inhibition: ↑ levels, but not major dose adjustment recommended)
pearls: PLATO (2009): ↓ CV death/MI vs clopidogrel in ACS. Reversible (advantage over prasugrel/clopidogrel). Bradycardia common but usually asymptomatic. Dyspnea (10%): tolerable, reversible. Faster onset/offset than clopidogrel. NO genetic testing needed (vs clopidogrel CYP2C19). Bleeding risk ↑ vs clopidogrel but offset by clinical benefit.
trials: PLATO, PEGASUS-TIMI 54
pk: t½ ~7–8.5 h (active metabolite ~8.5 h). Bioavailability ~36%. Protein binding >99%. Metabolized by CYP3A4 (major). Excretion: fecal ~58%, renal ~26%.
bbw: BLEEDING RISK: Can cause significant and fatal bleeding. Avoid in patients with active bleeding, prior ICH. Maintain aspirin dose ≤100 mg/day (higher doses reduce effectiveness).
highyield:
• Does NOT require hepatic activation (unlike clopidogrel/prasugrel) — faster, more consistent effect
• PLATO trial: superior to clopidogrel in ACS (reduced CV death, MI, stroke)
• Dyspnea in ~15% (adenosine-mediated) — usually transient, not bronchospasm
• Hold 5 days before CABG; reverse with platelet transfusion if needed
• Aspirin dose >100 mg/day reduces ticagrelor efficacy — keep aspirin ≤81 mg

name: Prasugrel
moa: Irreversible P2Y12 ADP receptor antagonist (thienopyridine); active metabolite is potent/fast (peak 30 min); stronger antiplatelet effect vs clopidogrel
indications: ACS (STEMI/NSTEMI) with PCI planned; post-PCI stent placement; TRITON-TIMI 38 showed ↓ stent thrombosis vs clopidogrel (but ↑ bleeding)
dosing: PO: 60 mg loading (age <75y, weight ≥60 kg), then 5 mg daily maint; Age ≥75 or weight <60 kg: 5 mg loading, 5 mg daily; DAPT × 12 months (or shorter per ACS/stent type)
renal: No dose adjustment; end-stage renal disease (eGFR <15): limited data, caution
hepatic: Moderate hepatic disease: use caution (reduced active metabolite formation); severe: contraindicated
ades: Bleeding (↑ vs clopidogrel, especially if age ≥75 or weight <60 kg, TRITON showed ↑ major bleed); bradycardia (rare); rash; thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP: extremely rare)
monitoring: CBC baseline (platelet count); assess for signs of bleeding; renal function; liver function; symptoms of arrhythmia; consider HAS-BLED score
ddi: Anticoagulants/warfarin (↑ bleeding); aspirin >100 mg daily (↑ bleeding); NSAIDs (↑ bleeding); opioids (may ↓ prasugrel absorption/efficacy, mechanism unclear)
pearls: TRITON-TIMI 38 (2007): ↓ stent thrombosis vs clopidogrel, but ↑ major bleeding (esp. fatal bleeding and bleeding needing transfusion). Irreversible (7-10 day recovery once d/c). Stronger platelet inhibition = ↑ efficacy but ↑ bleeding. Age ≥75 or weight <60 kg: higher bleeding risk (use 5 mg). Opioid paradox: may reduce absorption (use before PCI or post-PCI post-opioid). NO genetic testing (vs clopidogrel CYP2C19).
trials: TRITON-TIMI 38, CHAMPION-PCI
pk: Prodrug → active metabolite by esterases + CYP3A4/CYP2B6. t½ active metabolite ~7 h. Bioavailability ~79% (prodrug). Protein binding active metabolite ~98%. Excretion: urine ~68%, fecal ~27%.
bbw: BLEEDING RISK: Can cause significant and fatal bleeding. CONTRAINDICATED in patients with prior TIA or stroke. Generally not recommended in age ≥75 or weight <60 kg.
highyield:
• TRITON-TIMI 38: superior to clopidogrel in STEMI/ACS-PCI; but ↑ major bleeding
• Irreversible P2Y12 inhibitor — hold 7 days before CABG
• Absolute contraindication: prior stroke/TIA (net harm shown in TRITON-TIMI 38)
• Preferred over clopidogrel in STEMI (faster activation, no non-responders)
• No dose adjustment needed for renal impairment

name: Clopidogrel
moa: Irreversible P2Y12 ADP receptor antagonist; PRODRUG (requires hepatic CYP2C19 activation); peak antiplatelet effect 3-7 days
indications: Acute coronary syndrome (NSTEMI, STEMI with thrombolysis); post-PCI stent (BMS: 1 month; DES: 12 months); stable CAD; secondary prevention post-MI/stroke; PAD
dosing: PO: 300-600 mg loading (ACS/PCI), then 75 mg daily maintenance; DAPT: 12 months typical post-ACS (with aspirin 81-100 mg daily)
renal: No dose adjustment; ESRD: caution
hepatic: Hepatic disease: reduced activation; severe: avoid or careful monitoring
ades: Bleeding (↑ risk esp. if PPI/H2-blocker use, GI bleeding); rash; neutropenia (rare, 0.04%); TTP (rare, 1-2 per million); hypersensitivity; Stevens-Johnson; toxic epidermal necrolysis (very rare)
monitoring: CBC baseline (if symptoms: fever/jaundice assess for TTP); assess for bleeding signs; check CYP2C19 genotype (if available/useful); PPI use assess (omeprazole/esomeprazole ↓ efficacy); symptoms of arrhythmia
ddi: PPIs (omeprazole, esomeprazole: strong CYP2C19 inhibitors, ↓ active metabolite 25-50%); H2-blockers (ranitidine: ↓ efficacy); atazanavir/ritonavir (↑ levels); macrolides (moderate interaction); NSAIDs (↑ GI bleed risk); anticoagulants (↑ bleeding)
pearls: Decades-old standard for ACS/stents. PRODRUG: CYP2C19 dependent (genetic polymorphisms affect response; poor metabolizers respond minimally). PPI interactions: omeprazole/esomeprazole problematic (↓ activation); pantoprazole preferred. Irreversible (7-10 day recovery). Slower onset than prasugrel/ticagrelor (peak 3-7 days). TTP/hematologic toxicity rare but serious (fever/jaundice: urgent CBC). Alternative: switch to ticagrelor/prasugrel if poor response.
trials: CURE, COMMIT, CAPRIE
pk: Prodrug → active metabolite by CYP2C19 (major) + CYP3A4. t½ active metabolite ~30 min. Parent t½ ~6 h. Protein binding ~98%. Excretion: urine ~50%, fecal ~46%.
bbw: DIMINISHED EFFICACY IN CYP2C19 POOR METABOLIZERS: Reduced platelet inhibition. Consider alternative for high-risk patients.
highyield:
• CYP2C19 poor metabolizers (~14% population) have reduced efficacy — consider ticagrelor/prasugrel
• PPIs (especially omeprazole) reduce active metabolite via CYP2C19 inhibition
• CAPRIE: superior to aspirin for atherosclerotic events; CURE: added to aspirin in NSTE-ACS
• Irreversible — hold 5 days before CABG
• Genetic testing (ABCB1, CYP2C19) can guide P2Y12 selection post-PCI

name: Rivaroxaban
moa: Selective direct Factor Xa inhibition (both free and prothrombinase-bound); does not require antithrombin; ↓ prothrombin generation
indications: DVT/PE (acute & chronic prophylaxis); AFib stroke prevention (ROCKET-AF: non-inferiority vs warfarin); ACS (secondary prevention with aspirin: COMPASS); post-op thromboprophylaxis; CAD/PAD (COMPASS)
dosing: AF stroke prevention: 20 mg daily with food (15 mg if CrCl 15-30 or ≥80y + low weight); DVT/PE treatment: 15 mg BID × 3 weeks, then 20 mg daily × 3-12 months; ACS secondary: 2.5 mg BID + aspirin
renal: CrCl 15-30: 15 mg daily for AF; CrCl <15: avoid; significant renal impairment ↑ AUC
hepatic: Child-Pugh A: no change; B-C: avoid or caution (limited data)
ades: Bleeding (major: 2-3.6% annually in AF, comparable to warfarin); GI bleed (↑ risk); intracranial hemorrhage; hematuria; epistaxis; dyspepsia; rash
monitoring: Baseline: CBC, PT/INR (not monitored on DOAC typically), renal function, LFTs; periodic renal function (yearly or if AKI risk); assess bleeding signs; adherence (no monitoring levels required)
ddi: Strong CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitors (ketoconazole, itraconazole, ritonavir, lopinavir: ↑ rivaroxaban levels, use with caution or avoid); strong inducers (rifampin, carbamazepine: ↓ efficacy); NSAIDs/aspirin (↑ bleeding); anticoagulants (↑ bleeding, contraindicated); antiplatelet agents (↑ bleeding risk)
pearls: ROCKET-AF (2011): non-inferior to warfarin for AF stroke prevention. Once-daily (with food) = convenience. Hepatic metabolism (CYP3A4, P-gp substrate). No reversal agent (apixaban: andexanet alfa approved; rivaroxaban: andexanet alfa less effective). GI absorption food-dependent: MUST take with food (↓ absorption fasting). Peak levels 2-4h. Half-life 5-13h. Direct Xa inhibition (fast, predictable). Anticoagulation monitoring NOT required.
trials: ROCKET-AF, EINSTEIN, COMPASS
pk: t½ ~5–9 h (young), ~11–13 h (elderly). Bioavailability: 15 mg/20 mg tablets 80% with food (take WITH FOOD for ≥15 mg). Protein binding 92–95%. Metabolized by CYP3A4/CYP2J2 + P-gp substrate. Excretion: renal ~33%, fecal ~28%.
bbw: PREMATURE DISCONTINUATION INCREASES STROKE RISK (AFib): Do not stop without adequate anticoagulation. SPINAL/EPIDURAL HEMATOMA: Risk with neuraxial procedures.
highyield:
• 15 mg and 20 mg doses MUST be taken with the evening meal (absorption-dependent)
• ROCKET-AF: non-inferior to warfarin for stroke prevention in AFib
• No reversal agent issue: andexanet alfa (Andexxa) is the specific antidote
• Avoid if CrCl <15 mL/min; dose reduce to 15 mg/day if CrCl 15–50 (AFib)
• Dual CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitors (ketoconazole) and inducers (rifampin) significantly alter levels

name: Apixaban
moa: Selective direct Factor Xa inhibition (free and prothrombinase-bound Xa); ↓ thrombin generation; twice-daily dosing
indications: AFib stroke prevention (ARISTOTLE: superior to warfarin, ↓ mortality); DVT/PE (acute & prophylaxis); post-surgical thromboprophylaxis; ACS secondary (with aspirin: APPRAISE)
dosing: AF: 5 mg BID (standard), or 2.5 mg BID if ≥2 of: age ≥60, weight ≤60 kg, Cr ≥1.5 mg/dL; DVT/PE: 10 mg BID × 7 days, then 5 mg BID; post-op: 2.5-5 mg BID × 10-35 days
renal: CrCl 15-30: use 2.5 mg BID for AF; CrCl <15: limited data (avoid per labeling); minimal renal excretion (25-27%)
hepatic: Child-Pugh A: no change; B: increase dose-doubling risk (use caution); C: contraindicated
ades: Bleeding (2.1% major annually, ↓ vs warfarin/rivaroxaban); GI bleed (less common than rivaroxaban); intracranial hemorrhage; hematuria; epistaxis; minor bleeding
monitoring: Baseline: CBC, renal function, LFTs; periodic renal function (yearly); assess for bleeding signs; drug interactions (CYP3A4/P-gp); medication adherence
ddi: Strong CYP3A4 + P-gp inhibitors (ketoconazole, itraconazole, ritonavir: ↑ apixaban, avoid or monitor); strong inducers (rifampin, carbamazepine: ↓ efficacy, avoid); NSAIDs/aspirin (↑ bleeding); anticoagulants (↑ bleeding, contraindicated)
pearls: ARISTOTLE (2011): SUPERIOR to warfarin (↓ stroke, ↓ major bleed, ↓ all-cause mortality 11%) for AFib. BID dosing (inconvenience vs rivaroxaban once-daily). No food interaction (unlike rivaroxaban). Hepatic metabolism (CYP3A4/P-gp). Andexanet alfa (Factor Xa inhibitor reversal agent) EFFECTIVE for apixaban (better reversal vs rivaroxaban). Peak levels 3-4h. Half-life 12h. Lower bleeding risk vs warfarin/rivaroxaban. Renal clearance minimal = better CKD tolerability.
trials: ARISTOTLE, APPRAISE, AMPLIFY
pk: t½ ~12 h. Bioavailability ~50% (not food-dependent for ≤10 mg). Protein binding 87%. Metabolized by CYP3A4/P-gp. Excretion: fecal ~56%, renal ~27%.
bbw: PREMATURE DISCONTINUATION INCREASES STROKE RISK (AFib). SPINAL/EPIDURAL HEMATOMA: Risk with neuraxial anesthesia/spinal puncture.
highyield:
• ARISTOTLE: reduced stroke, major bleeding, and all-cause mortality vs warfarin in AFib
• Dose reduction criteria (use 2.5 mg BID): ≥2 of: age ≥80, weight ≤60 kg, SCr ≥1.5 mg/dL
• Andexanet alfa (Andexxa) is the specific reversal agent
• Can give with or without food (unlike rivaroxaban at high doses)
• AMPLIFY: superior efficacy and less bleeding vs LMWH/warfarin for DVT/PE treatment

name: Edoxaban
moa: Selective direct Factor Xa inhibition (prothrombinase-bound Xa preferentially); once-daily dosing; rapid absorption
indications: AFib stroke prevention (ENGAGE AF-TIMI 48: non-inferiority vs warfarin); DVT/PE (HOKUSAI-VTE); post-op thromboprophylaxis
dosing: AF: 60 mg daily (standard), or 30 mg daily if CrCl 15-50, weight ≤60 kg, or concurrent strong P-gp inhibitor; DVT/PE: 60 mg daily (10 mg daily × 5-10 days if acute)
renal: CrCl 15-50: 30 mg daily for AF; CrCl <15: avoid; significant renal clearance (50%) means CKD adjustment important
hepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic impairment; Child-Pugh B: caution
ades: Bleeding (major: 2.7% annually for AF, similar to warfarin); GI bleed; intracranial hemorrhage; hematuria; dyspepsia; rash
monitoring: Baseline: CBC, renal function, LFTs; periodic renal function (yearly if CKD); bleeding assessment; drug interactions (P-gp); adherence (once-daily = easier)
ddi: Strong P-gp inhibitors (verapamil, quinidine, erythromycin: ↑ edoxaban, reduce dose); P-gp inducers (rifampin, St. John's Wort: ↓ efficacy); NSAIDs/aspirin (↑ bleeding); anticoagulants (↑ bleeding); antiplatelet agents (↑ bleeding)
pearls: ENGAGE AF-TIMI 48 (2013): non-inferiority to warfarin for AFib. Once-daily dosing (convenience). High renal clearance (50%) = dose adjustment in CKD (30 mg daily if CrCl 15-50). P-gp substrate (food/drug interactions). NO reversal agent approved (unlike apixaban andexanet alfa). Lower prevalence among prescribers vs apixaban/rivaroxaban. Peak levels 1-2h. Half-life 10-14h. Fast absorption. Less bleeding vs warfarin in ENGAGE AF-TIMI 48.
trials: ENGAGE AF-TIMI 48, HOKUSAI-VTE
pk: t½ ~10–14 h. Bioavailability ~62% (not significantly food-affected). Protein binding 55%. Minimal CYP metabolism; P-gp substrate. Excretion: renal ~50%, biliary ~50%.
bbw: REDUCED EFFICACY AT HIGHER CrCl: Avoid in non-valvular AFib if CrCl >95 mL/min (increased stroke risk demonstrated in ENGAGE AF). PREMATURE DISCONTINUATION: ↑ stroke risk.
highyield:
• Unique: reduce dose to 30 mg daily if CrCl 15–50, weight ≤60 kg, OR P-gp inhibitor use
• ENGAGE AF-TIMI 48: non-inferior to warfarin; ↓ bleeding and CV mortality
• Must transition from parenteral anticoagulation after 5–10 days for VTE treatment
• Andexanet alfa reversal (FDA-approved); 4-factor PCC as alternative
• Avoid if CrCl >95 mL/min in AFib (inferior stroke prevention in ENGAGE AF)

name: Dabigatran
moa: Selective direct thrombin (Factor II) inhibition; reversibly binds to thrombin active site; does not require antithrombin
indications: AFib stroke prevention (RE-LY: superior to warfarin); DVT/PE (acute & secondary prevention); post-op thromboprophylaxis; ACS with stent (DAPT continuation)
dosing: AF: 150 mg BID (standard), or 75 mg BID if CrCl 15-30, age ≥80, or concurrent P-gp inhibitor; DVT/PE: 150 mg BID after 5-10 days parenteral anticoagulation
renal: CrCl 15-30: 75 mg BID for AF; CrCl <15: avoid (or use with extreme caution); significant renal clearance (80%); dialyzable
hepatic: Child-Pugh A: no change; B-C: caution/avoid
ades: Bleeding (1.6% major annually for AF, ↓ vs warfarin); GI bleed (dyspepsia 15%); intracranial hemorrhage; hematuria; epistaxis; gastritis
monitoring: Baseline: CBC, renal function, LFTs; renal function q3-12 months (esp. age ≥75 or CKD); assess bleeding signs; drug interactions (P-gp); GI symptoms (dyspepsia)
ddi: P-gp inhibitors (verapamil, dronedarone, clarithromycin, ritonavir: ↑ dabigatran, caution); P-gp inducers (rifampin, St. John's Wort: ↓ efficacy, avoid); NSAIDs/aspirin (↑ bleeding); anticoagulants (↑ bleeding); weak acid reducers (H2-blockers, PPIs: may ↓ absorption slightly)
pearls: RE-LY (2009): SUPERIOR to warfarin (↓ stroke 35% with 150 mg BID, ↓ bleeding vs warfarin). DTI (not Xa inhibitor). BID dosing (inconvenience). High renal clearance (80%) = CKD dose adjustments critical. Capsules contain tartaric acid (pellets inside, not tablets) = swallow whole, do NOT open (↑ bleeding). GI bleeding risk ↑ vs warfarin but offset by ↓ hemorrhagic stroke/mortality. Idarucizumab (reversal agent) available, monoclonal antibody (effective reversal). Peak levels 1-3h. Half-life 12-17h.
trials: RE-LY, RE-COVER, RE-MEDY
pk: Prodrug (dabigatran etexilate) → active dabigatran via esterases. t½ ~12–17 h. Bioavailability ~6–7% (low but consistent). Protein binding 35%. NOT a CYP substrate. P-gp substrate. Excretion: renal ~80% unchanged.
bbw: PREMATURE DISCONTINUATION INCREASES STROKE RISK (AFib). SPINAL/EPIDURAL HEMATOMA risk with neuraxial procedures.
highyield:
• Only DOAC with specific reversal agent idarucizumab (Praxbind) — dabigatran-specific antidote
• Take with glass of water; do NOT crush/chew capsules (pellets irritate GI); GI upset common — take with food
• RE-LY trial: non-inferior (110 mg) or superior (150 mg) to warfarin in AFib
• Renal: avoid if CrCl <15; reduce to 75 mg BID if CrCl 15–30; avoid with P-gp inhibitors if CrCl <50
• Dronedarone and ketoconazole (P-gp inhibitors): ↑ dabigatran levels significantly

name: Warfarin
moa: Inhibits vitamin K-dependent carboxylation (Factors II, VII, IX, X, proteins C & S); prevents γ-carboxylation in liver; requires 3-5 days for full effect (delayed until vitamin K-dependent factors eliminated)
indications: AFib (stroke prevention); VTE (DVT/PE acute & secondary prevention); mechanical heart valves (indefinite); thrombophilia; post-MI with LV thrombus; recurrent thrombotic events
dosing: PO: 5-10 mg loading (day 1), then 2-10 mg daily (adjusted by INR); target INR 2-3 (most indications), 2.5-3.5 (mechanical valves); requires frequent INR monitoring
renal: No dose adjustment; prolonged INR if severe renal impairment
hepatic: Liver disease: ↑ INR risk, reduce dose; avoid in decompensated cirrhosis
ades: Bleeding (major: 1-3% annually); INR supratherapeutic (overanticoagulation); warfarin necrosis (day 3-5, protein C deficiency: rare, skin necrosis); purple toe syndrome (atheroemboli); HIT (rare); hepatic necrosis (very rare)
monitoring: INR baseline, 3-5 days post-initiation, then q2-4 weeks until stable, then q4-12 weeks; PT/INR therapeutic monitoring MANDATORY; dietary vitamin K (dietary consistency important: affects INR); signs of bleeding; hepatic/renal function baseline
ddi: NSAIDs/aspirin (↑ bleeding); phenytoin (↓ warfarin levels); rifampin (↑ metabolism, ↓ INR); antibiotics (macrolides, fluoroquinolones, TMP-SMX: ↑ INR); antifungals (azoles: ↑ INR); amiodarone (↑ INR significantly); SSRIs (↑ INR); statins (↑ INR); alcohol (chronic: ↓ INR); vitamin K supplements (↓ INR)
pearls: Decades-old standard, still used for mechanical valves & select cases. NARROW THERAPEUTIC INDEX: INR monitoring MANDATORY (requires regular blood draws, patient education on vitamin K diet). Slow onset (3-5 days, bridge with LMWH or UFH acutely). Many drug interactions. Pregnancy contraindicated (teratogenic). Supratherapeutic INR: fresh frozen plasma (FFP) or vitamin K (slow reversal, phytonadione/vitamin K1). Dietary vitamin K affects INR (patient education critical). DOACs preferred over warfarin for most AFib, but warfarin still indicated for mechanical valves.
trials: AFFIRM, CHEST Guidelines
pk: Racemic mixture: S-warfarin (3–4× more potent, CYP2C9) + R-warfarin (CYP1A2, CYP3A4). t½ ~36–42 h. Bioavailability ~93–100%. Protein binding ~99%. Excretion: renal ~92% (metabolites).
bbw: BLEEDING RISK: May cause major or fatal bleeding. Risk factors: high INR, age ≥65, variable INR, prior GI bleed, hypertension, cerebrovascular disease, anemia. Regular INR monitoring required.
highyield:
• Vitamin K antagonist — inhibits factors II, VII, IX, X and proteins C, S (onset 2–3 days)
• Reversal: Vitamin K (slow), FFP (faster), 4-factor PCC (fastest for emergent reversal)
• Drug interactions: CYP2C9 inhibitors ↑ INR (fluconazole, amiodarone, metronidazole); inducers ↓ INR (rifampin, carbamazepine)
• Pharmacogenomics: CYP2C9 and VKORC1 polymorphisms affect dose requirements significantly
• Target INR 2–3 for most indications; 2.5–3.5 for mechanical mitral valve

name: Alteplase (tPA)
moa: Recombinant tissue plasminogen activator; converts plasminogen to plasmin; plasmin cleaves fibrin (fibrinolysis); restores thrombus perfusion
indications: Acute ischemic stroke (within 4.5h onset, or 3h if no prior thrombolysis); acute STEMI (within 12h, PCI not available or delayed); PE (massive, hemodynamically unstable)
dosing: Acute stroke: 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg), 10% as bolus over 1 min, remainder over 60 min IV; STEMI: 15 mg bolus, 0.75 mg/kg over 30 min (max 50 mg), then 0.5 mg/kg over 60 min (max 35 mg)
renal: No dose adjustment
hepatic: No specific adjustment; severe disease: caution (↑ bleeding risk)
ades: Intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH: 6% stroke, 0.4% STEMI); major bleeding (GI, pulmonary, retroperitoneal); reperfusion arrhythmias (STEMI, bradycardia, asystole); myocardial rupture (post-MI, rare); fever; nausea
monitoring: Baseline NIHSS score (stroke), ECG (STEMI), CT/MRI (exclude intracranial hemorrhage); post-thrombolysis: neurovital signs q15 min × 2h, then q30 min × 6h, then q1h × 16h; monitor for signs of bleeding; coagulation labs post-treatment
ddi: Anticoagulants (heparin, DOACs: start after 24h if possible, ↑ bleeding); antiplatelet agents (aspirin, P2Y12i: use with caution, can start 24h post-thrombolysis); fibrinolytic agents (avoid concurrent use)
pearls: Time-sensitive: "Time is brain" (stroke window 4.5h or 3h, STEMI 12h). NINDS rt-PA Study (1995): improved stroke outcomes 30% with tPA (though ↑ hemorrhage risk). Contraindications: prior ICH, active bleeding, recent surgery, BP >185/110. Reperfusion arrhythmias common in STEMI (prepare for ACLS). ICH risk highest if age >80, weight <70 kg, on anticoagulants. PCI preferred over fibrinolysis if available (better outcomes, lower bleeding).
trials: NINDS rt-PA, ASSENT-3, GUSTO
pk: t½ ~4–5 min (plasma clearance rapid). Bioavailability: IV only. Hepatic clearance. Fibrin-specific tPA; binds fibrin-bound plasminogen selectively.
highyield:
• Ischemic stroke: 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg) within 4.5 h of symptom onset; give 10% as bolus, rest over 60 min
• STEMI: accelerated infusion (front-loaded) when PCI not available within 120 min
• Absolute contraindications for stroke: prior ICH, ischemic stroke within 3 months, severe head trauma, active bleeding, uncontrolled HTN >185/110
• Monitor for angioedema (1–5%) — hold ACEi before tPA or monitor closely
• BP must be <185/110 before and ≤180/105 during/after tPA infusion

name: Atorvastatin
moa: Reversible inhibition of HMG-CoA reductase (rate-limiting step in cholesterol synthesis); ↓ LDL-C 30-50%+; ↑ HDL-C; ↓ TG
indications: Primary prevention (age 40-75 with CVD risk factors, LDL ≥70, or ASCVD 10yr >7.5%); secondary prevention (CVD, ACS, post-MI, post-stent); stroke prevention; familial hypercholesterolemia
dosing: PO: 10-20 mg daily (moderate intensity, ↓ LDL 30-49%), or 40-80 mg daily (high-intensity, ↓ LDL ≥50%); titrate by lipid response q4-12 weeks; max 80 mg daily
renal: No dose adjustment; severe renal disease: minimal metabolites
hepatic: Baseline LFTs; moderate-severe: avoid or caution (↑ hepatotoxicity risk); active liver disease: contraindicated
ades: Myalgia/muscle pain (5-10%); statin-associated muscle symptoms (SAMS, myopathy, rhabdomyolysis rare <0.1%); liver injury (1/10,000 LFT elevation, rarely symptomatic); new-onset diabetes (slight risk); cognitive effects (rare, reversible)
monitoring: Baseline lipid panel (TC, LDL, HDL, TG), LFTs, CK (if symptomatic); repeat lipids q4-12 weeks to goal; annual LFTs (baseline critical, repeat only if symptoms); muscle symptoms/pain assessment; glucose (diabetes risk screening)
ddi: CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, erythromycin, clarithromycin, ritonavir: ↑ atorvastatin 2-3×, risk myopathy; reduce atorvastatin); fibrates (gemfibrozil: ↑ myopathy risk significantly); niacin (↑ myopathy); cyclosporine (↑ myopathy); warfarin (monitor INR)
pearls: High-intensity statin (40-80 mg daily). WOSCOPS, ASCOT, JUPITER, HOPE-3: ↓ CV events/mortality. LDL goal varies (typically <70 mg/dL post-ACS, <100 mg/dL primary prevention). Statin intolerance: switch to lower-dose, every-other-day, or alternative statin (pravastatin less CYP3A4). SAMS: not all muscle pain is statin-related (context important). Reduce dose if combining with CYP3A4 inhibitors.
trials: WOSCOPS, ASCOT, JUPITER, HOPE-3
pk: t½ ~14 h (active metabolites ~20–30 h). Bioavailability ~14% (first-pass). Protein binding ≥98%. Metabolized by CYP3A4. Excretion: biliary/fecal ~98%.
highyield:
• High-intensity statin: 40–80 mg/day; LDL lowering ~50% at 80 mg dose
• ASCOT-LLA, CARDS, TNT trials support benefit across cardiovascular risk groups
• CYP3A4 inhibitors (diltiazem, amlodipine, clarithromycin) ↑ levels → myopathy risk
• Myopathy risk increases with dose; check CK if muscle symptoms arise
• Rhabdomyolysis risk elevated with gemfibrozil (avoid combination); fenofibrate preferred

name: Rosuvastatin
moa: Potent reversible HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor; ↓ LDL-C 40-55%+ (most potent statin); ↑ HDL-C; ↓ TG; minimal hepatic metabolism
indications: Primary/secondary ASCVD prevention (high-intensity LDL lowering); familial hypercholesterolemia; severe hypertriglyceridemia reduction
dosing: PO: 5-10 mg daily (moderate, ↓ LDL 30-49%), or 20-40 mg daily (high-intensity, ↓ LDL ≥50%); Asian ancestry: start 5 mg (increased statin exposure); max 40 mg daily
renal: Moderate-severe CKD: caution, possible ↑ statin levels (minimal renal clearance but active metabolite); hemodialysis: no adjustment
hepatic: Active liver disease/cirrhosis: contraindicated; moderate: caution
ades: Myalgia/myopathy (↑ vs atorvastatin in some reports); new-onset diabetes (slight risk); LFT elevation (3-5%); rhabdomyolysis (rare); myositis (very rare); congestive heart failure (rare, reversible)
monitoring: Baseline lipid panel, LFTs, CK (baseline if symptoms); repeat lipids q4-12 weeks to goal; LFTs baseline (repeat if symptoms only); muscle symptoms assessment; glucose screening
ddi: CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, clarithromycin: ↑ rosuvastatin 2-3×, reduce dose to 5-10 mg); gemfibrozil (↑ rosuvastatin AUC 2×, avoid or reduce rosuvastatin); cyclosporine (↑ myopathy risk, limit to 5 mg rosuvastatin); warfarin (monitor INR)
pearls: Most potent statin (highest LDL ↓ 40-55% standard dosing). Asian ancestry: ↑ statin exposure (start 5 mg). Minimal hepatic metabolism = fewer CYP interactions vs atorvastatin. JUPITER (2008): ↓ CV events even in low-baseline LDL. More myalgia reports vs atorvastatin (may be more potent effect). Avoid in patients on gemfibrozil/cyclosporine.
trials: JUPITER, STELLAR, MOON
pk: t½ ~19 h. Bioavailability ~20%. Protein binding ~88%. NOT significantly metabolized by CYP3A4 (minor CYP2C9). P-gp/OATP1B1 substrate. Excretion: fecal ~90%.
highyield:
• High-intensity statin: 20–40 mg/day; LDL lowering ~55–63% at 40 mg
• JUPITER trial: reduced CV events in patients with normal LDL but elevated hsCRP
• Fewer CYP3A4 interactions than atorvastatin — preferred with CYP3A4-heavy regimens
• Asian patients: start at 5 mg (higher plasma levels); max 20 mg if on certain drugs
• Dose reduce with certain HIV/transplant drugs (OATP1B1 inhibitors: cyclosporine → max 5 mg)

name: Evolocumab
class: PCSK9 Inhibitor (Monoclonal Antibody; IgG2 Fully Human)
moa: Monoclonal antibody binds PCSK9 (proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9); prevents PCSK9-mediated LDL receptor degradation; ↑ LDL receptor recycling → ↓ LDL-C additional 50-60% on statin
indications: Heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (FHC); clinical ASCVD (post-MI, post-stent, stable CAD, stroke) requiring additional LDL lowering; homozygous FHC (PCSK9 BB); statin intolerance with LDL goal unmet
dosing: SC: 140 mg Q2 weeks OR 420 mg Q4 weeks (equivalent); repeat dosing every 2 or 4 weeks for ongoing LDL reduction; can combine with statin ± ezetimibe
renal: No dose adjustment
hepatic: No specific adjustment
ades: Local injection site reactions (erythema, swelling, pain: 2-3%); muscle symptoms (PCSK9i myopathy rare, <1%); upper respiratory infections; fatigue; LDL-C <25 mg/dL (very low, long-term CV benefit unclear but no harm observed); anti-evolocumab antibodies (rare, may reduce efficacy)
monitoring: Baseline lipid panel (especially LDL-C); repeat lipids 4-12 weeks post-initiation to confirm LDL lowering; annual lipids; injection site assessment; muscle symptoms (baseline if SAMS history); cardiac event history
ddi: Minimal drug interactions (monoclonal antibody, no CYP metabolism); combine with statin ± ezetimibe for additive effect; additive benefits with other lipid-lowering agents
pearls: FOURIER (2017): ↓ CV events (MI/stroke/CV death) 15% with evolocumab + statin. Expensive (~$14,000/year before insurance). Can achieve LDL <30-50 mg/dL (very low, optimal in post-ACS). Inject SC Q2 or Q4 weeks (home-injectable). Monoclonal antibody = minimal drug interactions. Anti-PCSK9 antibodies develop in ~5% (may reduce efficacy). CMS/insurance coverage typically requires statin failure/intolerance or FHC.
trials: FOURIER, DESCARTES, MENDEL
pk: t½ ~11–17 days. Bioavailability ~72% (SC injection). Protein binding: binds PCSK9 (endogenous target). Eliminated by proteolytic degradation (not renal/hepatic CYP).
highyield:
• Monoclonal antibody binding PCSK9 → prevents LDL-R degradation → ↑ LDL clearance
• FOURIER trial: 59% LDL reduction; 15% RRR in CV events on top of statin therapy
• FDA-approved: heterozygous/homozygous FH, ASCVD with inadequate statin response
• Injection every 2 weeks (140 mg) or monthly (420 mg) — subcutaneous
• Most common ADEs: injection site reactions, nasopharyngitis, flu-like symptoms

name: Ezetimibe
moa: Inhibits Niemann-Pick C1-like 1 (NPC1L1) protein in intestinal brush border; blocks cholesterol/phytosterol absorption in small intestine; ↓ LDL-C 15-20% monotherapy
indications: Mild-moderate hypercholesterolemia (adjunct to statin or monotherapy if statin intolerant); familial hypercholesterolemia; combined hyperlipidemia
dosing: PO: 10 mg daily (taken with or without food); combine with statin for additive effect (10-20 mg LDL ↓); can combine with PCSK9i
renal: No dose adjustment; severe renal impairment: minimal data (likely safe)
hepatic: Moderate-severe cirrhosis: avoid (↑ ezetimibe levels)
ades: Myalgia/myopathy (when combined with statin); GI upset; headache; arthralgia; cholecystitis (rare, association uncertain); angioedema (rare)
monitoring: Baseline lipid panel; repeat lipids 4-12 weeks; LFTs if combined with statin; muscle symptoms if statin + ezetimibe
ddi: Bile acid sequestrants (cholestyramine, colestipol: ↓ ezetimibe absorption by 55-60%, separate dosing by 2h minimum); fibrates (monitor myopathy risk); statins (additive effect, monitor SAMS); cyclosporine (↑ ezetimibe levels)
pearls: IMPROVE-IT (2015): ezetimibe + simvastatin ↓ CV events vs simvastatin alone (additional 2% LDL ↓ = modest additional CV benefit). Not as potent as PCSK9i but cheaper. Additive with statin (modest ↓ LDL 15-20%). GI side effects minimal. No direct CV mortality reduction trials. Useful adjunct for statin-intolerant patients or maximizing LDL ↓.
trials: IMPROVE-IT, SANDS
pk: t½ ~22 h. Bioavailability ~35–65%. Protein binding ezetimibe ~99%, ezetimibe-glucuronide ~88%. Metabolized by glucuronidation (not CYP). Excretion: fecal ~78%, renal ~11%.
highyield:
• Inhibits NPC1L1 at intestinal brush border — reduces cholesterol absorption ~55%
• IMPROVE-IT: ezetimibe + simvastatin vs simvastatin alone → 6.4% RRR in CV events post-ACS
• Additive LDL-lowering ~15–20% on top of statin (combination achieves ~65% LDL reduction)
• No significant CYP interactions — minimal drug-drug interaction profile
• Not effective if no gallbladder (biliary recycling) — less effective after cholecystectomy

name: Hydralazine/Isosorbide Dinitrate
moa: Hydralazine: arteriolar vasodilator (↓ afterload, reflex tachycardia); Isosorbide dinitrate: nitrate (venodilator, ↓ preload); combined: ↑ nitric oxide bioavailability, ↓ sympathetic tone
indications: HFrEF self-identified Black patients (FDA-approved race-specific indication, A-HeFT trial); HF with reduced EF refractory to ACEi/ARB/beta-blockers; nitrate tolerance mitigation
dosing: PO: Hydralazine 10-25 mg TID-QID, Isosorbide dinitrate 20-40 mg TID (with 10-14h nitrate-free interval); titrate to max 300 mg hydralazine + 120 mg isosorbide daily
renal: CrCl 10-50: reduce hydralazine dose; CrCl <10: caution, nitrate accumulation risk
hepatic: Cirrhosis: caution (nitrates metabolized hepatically)
ades: Reflex tachycardia (hydralazine, 20%); hypotension; headache (nitrate); tachyphylaxis (nitrate tolerance, mitigated by nitrate-free interval); dizziness; palpitations; drug-induced lupus (hydralazine, rare); hypothyroidism (hydralazine, rare)
monitoring: Baseline ECG, vital signs; periodic HR/BP (reflex tachycardia risk); ANA (hydralazine lupus screen if symptoms); symptoms of decompensation
ddi: Phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil: profound hypotension, CONTRAINDICATED); other nitrates (↑ nitrate effect, spacing required); NSAIDs (↓ vasodilator effect); sympathomimetics (counteract vasodilation)
pearls: A-HeFT (2005): Black HF patients on hydralazine/isosorbide dinitrate + standard HF therapy (ACEi/ARB, beta-blocker, diuretic) showed ↓ mortality 43% vs placebo. Race-specific indication (FDA approval). Reflex tachycardia → add beta-blocker. Nitrate tolerance: nitrate-free interval (10-14h daily, typically overnight). Used when ACEi/ARB contraindicated or intolerant. BiDil formulation (fixed-dose) = compliance advantage.
trials: A-HeFT
pk: Hydralazine: t½ ~1–4 h; bioavailability varies (acetylation polymorphism — fast vs slow acetylators). ISDN: t½ ~1–4 h; bioavailability ~20–25%. Both hepatically metabolized.
highyield:
• A-HeFT trial: 43% mortality reduction in self-identified Black patients with HFrEF on top of standard therapy
• BiDil is fixed-dose combination — labeled specifically for Black patients (racial subgroup evidence)
• Lupus-like syndrome with hydralazine in fast acetylators (higher doses)
• Hydralazine: direct arterial vasodilator (↓ afterload); ISDN: venodilator (↓ preload)
• Alternative to ACEi/ARB when both are truly intolerable (renal, angioedema)

name: Mavacamten
moa: Direct allosteric inhibition of cardiac myosin motor protein; reduces cardiac myosin-actin force generation; improves LV obstruction and diastolic function in HCM
indications: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) with left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) obstruction; symptomatic HCM to reduce outflow obstruction and improve exercise capacity (EXPLORER-HCM)
dosing: PO: 5 mg daily, titrate based on ejection fraction (EF): decrease dose if EF drops <50% or >10% decline; may increase to 10 mg daily if tolerated (EF ≥50%); monitor EF weekly × 4 weeks, then monthly
renal: No dose adjustment; severe renal disease: limited data
hepatic: Avoid if moderate-severe hepatic impairment
ades: HF exacerbation/reduced EF (↓ contractility, ↑ HFrEF risk if EF already reduced); hypotension; dizziness; fatigue; bradycardia; syncope (from reduced contractility); back pain
monitoring: Baseline EF (echo/cardiac imaging), vital signs, symptoms; echocardiogram weekly × 4 weeks, then monthly, then q3 months; assess for HF symptoms (dyspnea, orthopnea, edema); HR/BP; exercise tolerance; LVOT gradient reduction
ddi: Negative inotropes (beta-blockers, CCBs: additive effect, use with caution); NSAIDs (↓ efficacy); anticoagulants if AFib develops (HCM+AFib ↑ stroke risk); CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole: may ↑ mavacamten, caution)
pearls: EXPLORER-HCM (2022): ↓ LVOT obstruction, ↓ symptoms, ↑ exercise capacity. FIRST-IN-CLASS cardiac myosin inhibitor. Mechanism: directly reduce contractility (opposite to inotropes) → ↓ LVOT obstruction. Risk of HFrEF if EF drops (↑ monitoring required). EF monitoring MANDATORY (weekly initial, then monthly). Contraindicated if baseline EF <50% or HFrEF. Can combine with existing HCM medications (beta-blockers, CCB, disopyramide) but monitor HF symptoms closely. Novel mechanism, newer data.
trials: EXPLORER-HCM, VALOR-HCM
pk: t½ ~7–9 days. Bioavailability ~77%. Protein binding 97–98%. Metabolized primarily by CYP2C19 (major), CYP3A4, CYP2C9. Excretion: fecal ~85%, renal ~11%.
bbw: HEART FAILURE RISK: Can cause symptomatic heart failure due to systolic dysfunction. Hold if LVEF <50% on echocardiogram. Available only through REMS program (Camzyos REMS).
highyield:
• First-in-class cardiac myosin inhibitor — reduces hypercontractility in obstructive HCM
• EXPLORER-HCM: improved LVOT gradient, functional class, and symptoms
• REMS program required — echocardiography every 12 weeks to monitor LVEF
• CYP2C19 poor metabolizers: use lower doses (genetic testing considered)
• Contraindicated with strong CYP2C19 or CYP3A4 inhibitors; moderate inhibitors require dose adjustment
• Hold if LVEF drops below 50% or clinical heart failure develops

name: Vancomycin
moa: Inhibits cell wall synthesis by binding D-Ala-D-Ala terminus of peptidoglycan precursors, blocking transglycosylation/transpeptidation. Bactericidal against gram-positives (time-independent; AUC/MIC-driven).
indications: MRSA infections (bacteremia, endocarditis, pneumonia, skin/soft tissue, osteomyelitis); C. diff colitis (PO only); VRE (last resort); coagulase-negative Staph.
dosing: IV: 15–20 mg/kg q8–12h (based on AUC/MIC target 400–600 mg·h/L per ASHP/IDSA 2020 guidelines). PO: 125 mg QID x 10d (C.diff). Loading dose 25–30 mg/kg if critically ill. Infuse over ≥60 min (1g); max rate 10 mg/min to prevent Red Man Syndrome.
renal: Primarily renally eliminated; CrCl <50 requires interval extension. HD: redose post-dialysis (500 mg–1g depending on session).
hepatic: No dose adjustment needed.
ades: Nephrotoxicity (esp. with concomitant piperacillin-tazobactam or aminoglycosides), ototoxicity (high troughs/prolonged therapy), Red Man Syndrome (rate-related, not true allergy — flushing/pruritus of face/neck), thrombophlebitis, DRESS.
monitoring: AUC/MIC monitoring (Bayesian pharmacokinetic software preferred); obtain 2 levels (pre and post 4th dose or per PK program). Older trough-based: 15–20 mg/L (MRSA bacteremia/endocarditis). SCr, CBC (neutropenia), LFTs. Audiometry if prolonged.
ddi: Synergistic nephrotoxicity: aminoglycosides, NSAIDs, loop diuretics, contrast. Additive nephrotoxicity with piperacillin-tazobactam (controversial but monitor closely).
pearls: AUC/MIC-guided dosing is now standard of care (2020 consensus guidelines). Red Man Syndrome prevented by slowing infusion or pre-medicating with diphenhydramine. Dose by actual body weight; use adjusted body weight if obese. Not effective for gram-negatives. Oral vancomycin NOT absorbed systemically (used only for GI indications).
trials: ASHP/IDSA/SIDP Vancomycin Consensus 2020, Rybak et al. AJHP 2020
pk: t½ ~4–8 h (renally dependent). Bioavailability: IV only (oral not absorbed — used for C. diff). Protein binding 55%. Not metabolized. Excretion: renal 80–90% unchanged (GFR-dependent).
bbw: OTOTOXICITY and NEPHROTOXICITY: Risk increased with concurrent nephrotoxic or ototoxic drugs. Monitor serum concentrations and renal function.
highyield:
• AUC/MIC-guided dosing preferred over trough-only: target AUC₂₄ 400–600 mg·h/L
• Red man syndrome (vancomycin flushing syndrome): not allergy — slow infusion rate (≥60 min), premedicate with antihistamine
• Narrow therapeutic index: nephrotoxicity (especially with piperacillin-tazobactam, aminoglycosides)
• MRSA coverage requires IV (oral = C. diff only)
• Loading dose 25–30 mg/kg IV for serious/bacteremic infections

name: Piperacillin-Tazobactam
moa: Piperacillin inhibits cell wall synthesis (binds PBPs). Tazobactam is a beta-lactamase inhibitor (irreversible suicide inhibitor of class A/C enzymes). Time-dependent killing; optimize by extended infusion.
indications: Intra-abdominal infections, nosocomial pneumonia (HAP/VAP), complicated UTI, skin/soft tissue, febrile neutropenia, Pseudomonas coverage.
dosing: 3.375 g IV q6h (standard); 4.5 g IV q6h; Extended infusion: 3.375 g infused over 4h q8h (preferred for Pseudomonas MIC 16–32 mcg/mL — maximizes % time above MIC). Renal dosing required.
renal: CrCl 20–40: 2.25 g q6h. CrCl <20: 2.25 g q8h. ESRD/HD: 2.25 g q12h + 0.75 g after HD sessions.
hepatic: No adjustment unless concurrent severe renal impairment.
ades: GI upset, diarrhea, electrolyte disturbances (hyponatremia — high Na content 2.35 mEq/g), hepatotoxicity (rare), thrombocytopenia, seizures (high doses), nephrotoxicity (debated synergy with vancomycin).
monitoring: SCr/BUN, CBC, electrolytes. Culture-directed therapy when possible. Drug fever — suspect if no other cause.
ddi: Vancomycin (AKI risk debate), methotrexate (decreased clearance), anticoagulants (hypoprothrombinemia risk), aminoglycosides (inactivation if mixed in same IV bag — separate administration).
pearls: Extended infusion improves PK/PD target attainment for MIC-creeping organisms (Pseudomonas). Does NOT cover MRSA, VRE, Stenotrophomonas, or Acinetobacter reliably. Penicillin allergy — assess cross-reactivity risk; aztreonam may substitute for gram-negative coverage. MERINO trial: pip-tazo inferior to meropenem for ESBL bacteremia (carbapenem preferred for ESBL).
trials: MERINO trial (Harris et al. JAMA 2018), Lodise extended infusion studies
pk: t½ piperacillin/tazobactam ~1 h. Bioavailability: IV only. Protein binding ~30%. Minimal hepatic metabolism. Excretion: renal ~68% (piperacillin), ~80% (tazobactam) unchanged.
highyield:
• Extended-spectrum penicillin + β-lactamase inhibitor; covers Pseudomonas, Enterobacteriaceae, anaerobes
• AKIKI trial: combination with vancomycin ↑ AKI risk vs vancomycin alone — separate or use alternative
• Time-dependent killing — optimize with extended infusions (3–4 hours)
• Does NOT cover MRSA, VRE, or ESBL-producing organisms reliably
• Dose adjust for CrCl <40 mL/min; dialysis supplement required

name: Meropenem
moa: Broad-spectrum beta-lactam; binds PBPs 2 and 3, inhibiting cell wall synthesis. Stable to most beta-lactamases including ESBLs and AmpC. Time-dependent killing.
indications: Severe gram-negative infections (ESBL, Pseudomonas, Acinetobacter); polymicrobial intra-abdominal; meningitis; febrile neutropenia; HAP/VAP. Drug of choice for ESBL-producing organisms (MERINO trial).
dosing: 1 g IV q8h (standard); 2 g IV q8h (Pseudomonas, CNS infections). Extended infusion: 2 g over 3h q8h for highly resistant organisms. Meningitis: 2 g q8h.
renal: CrCl 26–50: 1 g q12h. CrCl 10–25: 0.5 g q12h. CrCl <10: 0.5 g q24h. HD: dose after dialysis.
hepatic: No adjustment needed.
ades: GI (N/V/D), seizures (lower threshold than imipenem — preferred for CNS), hypersensitivity (cross-reactivity ~1% with penicillin allergy), hepatotoxicity (transient LFT elevation), thrombocytopenia.
monitoring: Culture/susceptibility, SCr, CBC. Watch for Clostridioides difficile with prolonged use. Seizure threshold monitoring.
ddi: Valproic acid — dramatic reduction in valproate levels (do not use together; use alternative anticonvulsant or antibiotic). Probenecid (increases levels).
pearls: Preferred carbapenem for CNS infections (less epileptogenic than imipenem). MERINO trial confirmed superiority over piperacillin-tazobactam for ESBL/AmpC bacteremia. Carbapenem-resistant organisms (CRE) require combination therapy (ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam, polymyxins). Meropenem-vaborbactam (Vabomere) covers KPC-producing CRE.
trials: MERINO (JAMA 2018), CARBAVAR trial, TANGO II (meropenem-vaborbactam)
pk: t½ ~1 h. Bioavailability: IV only. Protein binding ~2%. Minimal hepatic metabolism (hydrolysis). Excretion: renal ~70% unchanged.
highyield:
• Broadest β-lactam spectrum: covers ESBL, AmpC, Pseudomonas (variable KPC)
• Extended infusion (3–4 h) improves PK/PD target attainment for resistant organisms
• Lower seizure threshold than other carbapenems (less than imipenem-cilastatin)
• Valproic acid: carbapenems dramatically reduce valproate levels (↓ 60–100%) — avoid combination
• Dose reduce if CrCl <50; no hepatic adjustment

name: Daptomycin
moa: Binds to bacterial cell membrane (calcium-dependent), forming pores that depolarize the membrane potential → disrupts protein, DNA, and RNA synthesis. Rapidly bactericidal. Concentration-dependent.
indications: MRSA/MSSA bacteremia, right-sided endocarditis; cSSSI (4 mg/kg/d); MRSA bacteremia/endocarditis (6 mg/kg/d). NOT for pneumonia (inactivated by pulmonary surfactant).
dosing: cSSSI: 4 mg/kg IV q24h. Bacteremia/endocarditis: 6 mg/kg q24h (some guidelines recommend 8–10 mg/kg for high inoculum MRSA). All doses once daily.
renal: CrCl <30: extend interval to q48h. HD: dose after dialysis (supplemental dose 6–8 mg/kg post-HD 3x/week).
hepatic: No adjustment for mild-moderate hepatic impairment.
ades: Myopathy/rhabdomyolysis (CK monitoring essential — hold statins; check weekly), eosinophilic pneumonia (rare, new pulmonary infiltrates = stop drug), peripheral neuropathy, GI upset.
monitoring: CK weekly (or more if elevated/symptoms); discontinue if CK >5x ULN with symptoms or >10x ULN regardless. Hold HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors. SCr, CBC. MIC susceptibility (MIC breakpoint: susceptible ≤1 mcg/mL).
ddi: Statins — additive myopathy risk (hold for duration of daptomycin therapy). Tobramycin may increase daptomycin concentrations.
pearls: Cannot use for pneumonia — lung surfactant inactivates it (clinical trial failure). MRSA bacteremia with device: requires source control (remove device if possible) + daptomycin. Tolerance/resistance can emerge (MIC creep) especially with prolonged therapy or prior vancomycin exposure. High-dose (8–12 mg/kg) used off-label for MRSA with high MICs and VRE.
trials: Fowler et al. NEJM 2006 (bacteremia/endocarditis), Shorr et al. (cSSSI)
pk: t½ ~8–9 h. Bioavailability: IV only. Protein binding ~90–93%. Minimal metabolism. Excretion: renal ~78% (unchanged).
highyield:
• Lipopeptide — inactivated by pulmonary surfactant: CANNOT use for pneumonia
• Coverage: MRSA, VRE (enterococcal), Staph bacteremia/endocarditis
• Monitor CPK weekly (or more frequently) — myopathy/rhabdomyolysis risk
• DAPTOMYCIN + PULMONARY INFECTIONS = wrong choice (always tested on BCPS)
• Statins ↑ myopathy risk — consider holding statins during daptomycin therapy

name: Linezolid
moa: Binds to 23S ribosomal RNA of 50S subunit, preventing formation of 70S initiation complex → bacteriostatic against most organisms (bactericidal vs. streptococci). Unique ribosomal binding site; no cross-resistance with other protein synthesis inhibitors.
indications: VRE infections; MRSA pneumonia (superior lung penetration vs. vancomycin); MRSA skin/soft tissue; drug-resistant TB (off-label). Alternative for patients with vancomycin toxicity or treatment failure.
dosing: 600 mg IV/PO q12h (excellent oral bioavailability ~100% — oral = IV). Duration: 7–14d (SSTI), 14–21d (pneumonia), 14–28d (bacteremia).
renal: No adjustment required (metabolized hepatically). Use with caution in ESRD — metabolites accumulate.
hepatic: No dose adjustment for mild-moderate hepatic impairment.
ades: Myelosuppression (thrombocytopenia most common, anemia, neutropenia — especially >2 weeks), serotonin syndrome (MAO inhibition — do not combine with serotonergic agents), peripheral/optic neuropathy (prolonged use >28d), lactic acidosis (mitochondrial toxicity), hepatotoxicity.
monitoring: CBC weekly (esp. platelets — thrombocytopenia most dose-limiting). LFTs. Serotonin syndrome symptoms. Lactate if prolonged. Vision changes (optic neuropathy — discontinue if visual disturbances).
ddi: MAO inhibition — SSRIs, SNRIs, TCAs, meperidine, tramadol, dextromethorphan (serotonin syndrome risk). Tyramine-rich foods (hypertensive crisis risk — counsel patients to avoid aged cheese, wine, processed meats). Adrenergics.
pearls: Oral bioavailability 100% — ideal step-down therapy from IV when GI tract functional. Superior to vancomycin for MRSA VAP in some studies (ATTAIN trial). Myelosuppression risk increases after 14 days — limit duration. Unlike daptomycin, works in lungs. Check concurrent serotonergic medications BEFORE prescribing.
trials: ATTAIN trial (Wunderink CLIN INF DIS 2012 — MRSA VAP), ZEPHyR trial
pk: t½ ~5–7 h. Bioavailability ~100% (PO = IV). Protein binding ~31%. Metabolized by oxidation (not CYP). Excretion: renal ~80% (metabolites ~65%, parent ~30%).
highyield:
• Oxazolidinone — covers MRSA, VRE (only reliable oral VRE option)
• Serotonin syndrome risk with SSRIs, SNRIs, MAOIs, tramadol — monitor closely
• Myelosuppression (thrombocytopenia most common) with use >2 weeks — CBC weekly
• 100% oral bioavailability — no dose change oral-to-IV
• Weak MAO inhibitor — tyramine-rich foods (aged cheese, wine) can cause hypertensive crisis

name: Cefepime
moa: Binds PBPs, inhibits cell wall synthesis. Broad gram-negative coverage including Pseudomonas; stable to many AmpC beta-lactamases. Time-dependent killing. Zwitterionic charge allows rapid outer membrane penetration.
indications: Febrile neutropenia (monotherapy), HAP/VAP, complicated UTI, intra-abdominal (with metronidazole), Pseudomonas infections, empiric gram-negative coverage.
dosing: Standard: 2 g IV q8h (serious infections, Pseudomonas). UTI: 0.5–1 g q12h. Febrile neutropenia: 2 g q8h. Extended infusion: 2 g over 3h q8h for resistant organisms.
renal: CrCl 30–60: 2 g q12h. CrCl 11–29: 2 g q24h. CrCl <11: 1 g q24h. HD: dose after HD.
hepatic: No adjustment needed.
ades: Neurotoxicity (encephalopathy, myoclonus, seizures — esp. with renal impairment and incorrect dosing; cefepime-induced neurotoxicity), GI upset, hypersensitivity, elevated LFTs, positive Coombs.
monitoring: SCr (dose adjust), neurologic status (encephalopathy/myoclonus = red flag for cefepime neurotoxicity), culture/susceptibilities.
ddi: Aminoglycosides (additive nephrotoxicity), probenecid (increased cefepime levels). Incompatible with metronidazole, aminoglycosides in same IV line.
pearls: Cefepime neurotoxicity: underrecognized — encephalopathy, non-convulsive status epilepticus in renally impaired patients receiving standard doses. EEG shows triphasic waves. Reverses on drug discontinuation. Does NOT cover MRSA, VRE, or carbapenem-resistant organisms. Appropriate dose adjustment for renal function is critical.
trials: Freifeld AK et al. febrile neutropenia guidelines (IDSA 2011)
pk: t½ ~2–2.5 h. Bioavailability: IV/IM. Protein binding ~20%. Minimal hepatic metabolism. Excretion: renal ~85% unchanged.
highyield:
• 4th-generation cephalosporin: covers Pseudomonas + AmpC-producing organisms
• Neurotoxicity (encephalopathy, myoclonus, seizures) with dose excess/renal impairment
• IDEAL target: fT>MIC optimized with extended infusions or higher frequency dosing
• Does NOT cover MRSA, VRE, anaerobes, or ESBL reliably
• Dose reduction required for CrCl <60 mL/min; seizure risk ↑ if not renally adjusted

name: Metronidazole
moa: Reduced by microbial nitroreductases to reactive intermediates that damage DNA (strand breakage) → bactericidal/cidAL against anaerobes and select protozoa. Only active under anaerobic conditions.
indications: Anaerobic/mixed infections (intra-abdominal with cephalosporin/fluoroquinolone), C. difficile (mild-moderate — now second-line behind fidaxomicin/oral vancomycin), Trichomonas, Giardia, bacterial vaginosis, H. pylori (triple therapy), amoebic dysentery.
dosing: IV: 500 mg q8h or 1 g q12h. PO: 500 mg TID or 250 mg QID. C. diff mild: 500 mg PO TID x 10–14d (alternative; fidaxomicin/vancomycin preferred per IDSA 2021). H. pylori: 500 mg BID x 10–14d.
renal: No adjustment for renal impairment (metabolites accumulate in ESRD — dose reduction after 3 days in ESRD).
hepatic: Severe hepatic impairment: reduce dose by 50% (extensively hepatically metabolized, active metabolites).
ades: GI (N/V, metallic taste, abdominal cramps), peripheral neuropathy (prolonged use), CNS (headache, dizziness, seizures, encephalopathy), disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol, urine darkening.
monitoring: CBC with prolonged use, neurologic symptoms. LFTs in hepatic impairment. Avoid alcohol during and 48h after therapy.
ddi: Disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol (acetaldehyde accumulation — severe N/V). Warfarin (inhibits CYP2C9 — significant INR increase; reduce warfarin dose ~50%, monitor closely). Lithium toxicity. Disulfiram: psychosis/confusion. CYP inhibitor (2C9, 3A4).
pearls: Metallic taste is common — counsel patients. Peripheral neuropathy is cumulative — limit cumulative dose/duration. No longer first-line for C. diff (inferior recurrence rate vs. fidaxomicin/oral vancomycin per IDSA 2021 guidelines). Excellent oral bioavailability (>90%) — IV not superior to PO when gut works. CNS penetration excellent.
trials: IDSA C.diff Guidelines 2021 (downgraded metronidazole to alternative therapy)
pk: t½ ~6–10 h. Bioavailability ~>90% (oral). Protein binding <20%. Metabolized hepatically (CYP3A4 minor, oxidation primary). Excretion: renal ~60–80%.
bbw: CARCINOGENESIS: Metronidazole is carcinogenic in rodents. Avoid unnecessary use. Reserve for conditions for which it is indicated.
highyield:
• Disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol — avoid alcohol during therapy and 48 h after
• Drug of choice for anaerobic infections (intra-abdominal, C. diff — oral or IV)
• Warfarin interaction: inhibits CYP2C9 — may double INR; monitor closely
• CNS penetration excellent — drug of choice for brain abscess (anaerobic component)
• Peripheral neuropathy with prolonged use (>4 weeks); monitor for tingling/numbness

name: Voriconazole
moa: Inhibits fungal cytochrome P450 14-alpha-demethylase (CYP51) → blocks ergosterol synthesis → cell membrane disruption. Fungicidal vs. molds (Aspergillus), fungistatic vs. yeasts (Candida).
indications: Invasive aspergillosis (first-line), CNS/disseminated fungal infections, Fusarium, Scedosporium, voriconazole-susceptible Candida. Not for mucormycosis (use amphotericin B or isavuconazole).
dosing: IV: 6 mg/kg q12h x 2 doses (loading), then 4 mg/kg q12h. PO: 400 mg q12h x 1d (loading), then 200–300 mg q12h. Oral bioavailability ~96%. IMPORTANT: IV formulation contains sulfobutylether beta-cyclodextrin vehicle — accumulates in renal failure (use PO if CrCl <50).
renal: Switch to oral formulation if CrCl <50 mL/min (avoid IV cyclodextrin accumulation).
hepatic: Child-Pugh A/B: standard loading dose, reduce maintenance by 50%. Child-Pugh C: use only if benefit outweighs risk; monitor closely. Significant hepatotoxicity risk.
ades: Visual disturbances (photopsia, color changes, blurred vision — common, transient, reversible, dose-dependent), hallucinations/CNS toxicity (encephalopathy, psychosis — esp. supratherapeutic levels), hepatotoxicity (LFT elevation, cholestatic), phototoxicity/squamous cell carcinoma (prolonged use/sun exposure), QTc prolongation, periostitis (prolonged use — bone pain, fluorosis-like picture).
monitoring: Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM): trough target 1–5.5 mcg/mL (efficacy and toxicity-guided). Supratherapeutic (>5.5) increases hallucinations/encephalopathy risk. LFTs weekly x 1 month then monthly. ECG (QTc). Ophthalmology if visual changes persist.
ddi: Major CYP2C19, 3A4, 2C9 inhibitor — numerous interactions. Rifampin (major inducer — contraindicacted, ↓voriconazole ~90%). Carbamazepine, phenytoin, rifabutin — contraindicated. Tacrolimus/cyclosporine/sirolimus levels dramatically increased. Warfarin (↑INR). Statins (increased statin toxicity). Sirolimus contraindicated.
pearls: CYP2C19 polymorphism: poor metabolizers (Asians ~15–20% prevalence) have much higher drug levels — TDM essential. Visual disturbances are common but transient — warn patients before driving. Periostitis is unique long-term toxicity (>6 months use) — fluoride-like bone lesions. PREFERRED over amphotericin for invasive aspergillosis (Walsh et al. NEJM 2002 study).
trials: Walsh TJ NEJM 2002 (voriconazole vs. amphotericin for invasive aspergillosis)
pk: t½ ~6 h (saturable — non-linear PK at higher doses). Bioavailability ~96% (PO). Protein binding ~58%. Metabolized by CYP2C19 (major), CYP2C9, CYP3A4. Excretion: renal <2% unchanged.
bbw: HEPATOTOXICITY: Monitor LFTs during therapy; discontinue if significant elevation. PHOTOTOXICITY and SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA with long-term use.
highyield:
• Drug of choice for invasive aspergillosis (IDSA guidelines)
• Non-linear (saturable) PK → therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) mandatory; target trough 1–5.5 mcg/mL
• Visual disturbances (photopsia, color changes) in ~30% — transient, usually not serious
• Major CYP2C19 substrate — poor metabolizers (Asians ~15%) have 4× higher levels
• Extensive DDIs: contraindicated with rifampin, carbamazepine, long-acting barbiturates

name: Micafungin
moa: Non-competitive inhibition of 1,3-beta-D-glucan synthase → prevents glucan polymer synthesis → weakens fungal cell wall (absent in humans — favorable safety profile). Fungicidal vs. Candida, fungistatic vs. Aspergillus.
indications: Invasive candidiasis (preferred first-line per IDSA 2016); esophageal candidiasis; prophylaxis in HSCT. Preferred in ICU/hemodynamically unstable patients (vs. fluconazole). No oral formulation.
dosing: Invasive candidiasis: 100 mg IV q24h. Esophageal candidiasis: 150 mg IV q24h. HSCT prophylaxis: 50 mg IV q24h. No loading dose required.
renal: No dose adjustment (minimal renal elimination).
hepatic: No dose adjustment for mild-moderate impairment. Minimal data for severe.
ades: Generally very well tolerated. Histamine-mediated infusion reactions (rare), hepatotoxicity (LFT elevation), hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, nausea.
monitoring: LFTs, electrolytes (K+, Mg2+), CBC. Culture results/blood cultures q48–72h for candidemia.
ddi: Sirolimus (increased sirolimus levels by ~21% — monitor), nifedipine (increased nifedipine AUC). Minimal CYP interactions (not a CYP inhibitor/inducer).
pearls: Echinocandins are first-line for invasive candidiasis per IDSA 2016 (class includes micafungin, caspofungin, anidulafungin). Activity against most Candida species including C. glabrata, C. krusei. NOT active against Cryptococcus, Fusarium, Mucormycetes, Trichosporon. C. parapsilosis intrinsic reduced susceptibility (MIC higher — use azoles if susceptible). Consider step-down to fluconazole if patient stable + susceptible + blood cultures negative.
trials: Pappas PG et al. IDSA Candidiasis Guidelines 2016, Kuse ER crit care micafungin vs caspofungin
pk: t½ ~14–17 h. Bioavailability: IV only. Protein binding >99%. Metabolized hepatically (CYP3A4 minor). Excretion: fecal ~70%, renal ~15%.
highyield:
• Echinocandin — inhibits β-1,3-glucan synthase (fungal cell wall)
• Coverage: Candida spp. (including fluconazole-resistant), Aspergillus (static only)
• Does NOT cover Cryptococcus or Zygomycetes (Mucor)
• Minimal drug interactions vs azoles — preferred in transplant/drug-heavy patients
• No dose adjustment for renal impairment — hepatic adjustment for Child-Pugh C only

name: Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole
moa: Sequential blockade of folate synthesis: sulfamethoxazole inhibits dihydropteroate synthase (DHPS); trimethoprim inhibits dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR) → synergistic inhibition of tetrahydrofolate production → bactericidal.
indications: PCP prophylaxis and treatment (drug of choice); UTI (uncomplicated, MRSA UTI); MRSA skin/soft tissue; Toxoplasma prophylaxis/treatment; Nocardia; Stenotrophomonas maltophilia (drug of choice); Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (high-dose for treatment).
