dosing: PCP prophylaxis: 1 SS tab daily or 1 DS tab 3x/week. PCP treatment: 15–20 mg/kg/day (TMP component) IV/PO divided q6–8h x 21d. MRSA SSTI: 1–2 DS tabs BID x 5–10d. UTI: 1 DS tab BID x 3d (uncomplicated). Stenotrophomonas: 1 DS tab q8h.
renal: CrCl 15–30: 50% dose reduction. CrCl <15: avoid (hyperkalemia risk, SCr elevation). Increased SCr from TMP blocking creatinine secretion (not true GFR reduction — distinguish from real nephrotoxicity).
hepatic: Use with caution in severe hepatic impairment (hepatic metabolism).
ades: Hyperkalemia (TMP blocks ENaC sodium channels like potassium-sparing diuretic — dose-dependent), SCr elevation (tubular secretion blockade — spurious increase not true renal impairment), hematologic (megaloblastic anemia, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia — folate deficiency, add leucovorin for PCP treatment), Steven-Johnson syndrome/TEN (sulfonamide allergy), photosensitivity, nephrotoxicity (tubular injury with high doses), drug fever.
monitoring: SCr/BUN (distinguish TMP-induced SCr rise from true AKI), potassium (hyperkalemia risk esp. with ACEi/ARBs/aldosterone antagonists), CBC with differential, LFTs.
ddi: Warfarin (inhibits CYP2C9 — significant INR increase). ACEi/ARBs/spironolactone (additive hyperkalemia). Methotrexate (folate antagonism — myelosuppression). Phenytoin (increased phenytoin levels). Repaglinide (hypoglycemia).
pearls: TMP causes spurious SCr elevation by blocking tubular secretion (cimetidine does same) — does NOT reflect GFR reduction; check cystatin C if concerned. Hyperkalemia risk is significant — check K+ before and during therapy, especially in elderly or with RAAS medications. Drug of choice for Stenotrophomonas maltophilia and Pneumocystis. For PCP treatment, add prednisone if PaO2 <70 mmHg (improves survival).
trials: NIH ACTG 021 (PCP treatment), Masur et al. PCP guidelines
pk: TMP t½ ~8–10 h; SMX t½ ~9–11 h. Bioavailability ~100% (oral). Protein binding: TMP ~44%, SMX ~70%. TMP: renal elimination; SMX: acetylation + renal. Fixed 1:5 ratio (TMP:SMX).
highyield:
• PCP prophylaxis and treatment (drug of choice for moderate-severe PCP + adjunct steroids)
• Hyperkalemia via ENaC inhibition (resembles K+-sparing diuretic) — monitor K⁺ with ACEi/ARBs
• SCr elevation without true GFR decline (blocks tubular creatinine secretion)
• Hemolysis in G6PD-deficient patients — screen when possible
• Warfarin: inhibits CYP2C9 → ↑ INR; methotrexate: ↑ toxicity (folate antagonism)

name: Norepinephrine
moa: Potent alpha-1 and alpha-2 agonist (peripheral vasoconstriction, ↑SVR, ↑MAP); modest beta-1 agonist (↑HR minimally). Net effect: ↑MAP, ↑CO (via afterload normalization), ↑ perfusion pressure. Minimal beta-2 activity.
indications: Septic shock (first-line vasopressor per Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021); other distributive shock states; cardiogenic shock (adjunct when MAP insufficient). TARGET MAP ≥65 mmHg.
dosing: 0.01–3 mcg/kg/min IV infusion; titrate to MAP ≥65 mmHg. Typical dose: 0.1–0.5 mcg/kg/min for septic shock. Central line preferred (can use peripheral catheter for short duration per SCCM guidance, large-bore IV).
renal: No renal dose adjustment (hepatically metabolized, MAO/COMT).
hepatic: Hepatic failure → reduced clearance; titrate carefully by clinical response.
ades: Peripheral vasoconstriction (skin mottling, digital ischemia with prolonged high doses), reflex bradycardia, hypertension, arrhythmias (less than epinephrine), local tissue necrosis if extravasation (treat with phentolamine).
monitoring: Continuous hemodynamic monitoring (MAP, HR, BP), cardiac output (if PA catheter or CO monitoring), urine output (perfusion marker), lactate clearance, tissue perfusion markers (mottling score). Assess extravasation site regularly.
ddi: MAO inhibitors (severe hypertension), tricyclic antidepressants (potentiated vasopressor response), volatile anesthetic agents (arrhythmia risk), halogenated hydrocarbons.
pearls: First-line vasopressor for septic shock (SOAP II — norepinephrine superior to dopamine with fewer arrhythmias). Vasopressin can be added as second agent (0.03 units/min) to spare norepinephrine dose and reduce dosing requirements. Extravasation management: phentolamine 5–10 mg in 10–15 mL NS injected into tissue. CATS trial: comparable to dopamine but safer. Phenylephrine: pure alpha agonist alternative if tachyarrhythmia is concern.
trials: SOAP II (De Backer NEJM 2010 — norepinephrine vs. dopamine in shock)
pk: t½ ~2 min. IV continuous infusion only. Metabolized by COMT and MAO. Not renally excreted intact. Immediate onset.
bbw: EXTRAVASATION NECROSIS: Tissue necrosis from local ischemia. Use large vein; monitor IV site. Phentolamine antidote for extravasation.
highyield:
• First-line vasopressor for septic shock (Surviving Sepsis Campaign)
• Predominantly α1-adrenergic agonist + moderate β1 → vasoconstriction + mild inotropy
• SOAP II: superior to dopamine (fewer arrhythmias, lower mortality in cardiogenic/septic shock)
• Central line preferred (extravasation → tissue necrosis); phentolamine for extravasation
• Dose range 0.01–3 mcg/kg/min; titrate to MAP ≥65 mmHg

name: Vasopressin
moa: Binds V1 receptors (vascular smooth muscle) → vasoconstriction via IP3/DAG pathway (independent of adrenergic receptors). V2 receptors (renal collecting duct) → water reabsorption (antidiuretic effect). Restores vasopressin deficiency common in septic shock (relative deficiency within 24h).
indications: Adjunctive vasopressor in septic/distributive shock (norepinephrine-sparing); vasodilatory shock; central diabetes insipidus (low dose IV/intranasal desmopressin alternative); GI variceal bleeding (terlipressin preferred in Europe).
dosing: Septic shock: fixed dose 0.03–0.04 units/min IV (NOT titrated in most protocols — add-on to norepinephrine). NOT weight-based. Diabetes insipidus: 2–4 units IM/SC q4–6h.
renal: No dose adjustment; assess fluid balance.
hepatic: No dose adjustment. Metabolized in liver/kidneys.
ades: Skin necrosis (digital ischemia, bowel ischemia at high doses), hyponatremia (SIADH-like with high doses), cardiac ischemia (coronary vasoconstriction), decreased CO if used without adequate filling pressures.
monitoring: Hemodynamics (MAP, CO), urine output/osmolality, sodium (hyponatremia risk), tissue perfusion, mesenteric ischemia signs (abdominal exam), cardiac biomarkers.
ddi: Catecholamines (additive vasopressor effect); indomethacin (reduces GFR — monitor renal function); lithium (antagonizes V2 antidiuretic effect).
pearls: VASST trial: vasopressin vs. norepinephrine alone — no mortality difference overall, but subset analysis suggests benefit in less severe shock (norepinephrine requirement <15 mcg/min). Fixed dose of 0.03 units/min is standard (higher doses associated with cardiac/mesenteric ischemia). Does not require dose titration. Vasopressin deficiency occurs within 24h in severe septic shock. VANCS trial (cardiac surgery): vasopressin reduced AF vs norepinephrine.
trials: VASST (Russell et al. NEJM 2008), VANCS trial, VANISH trial
pk: t½ ~10–20 min. IV infusion. Hepatic and renal metabolism. Fixed dose (0.03–0.04 units/min for septic shock — NOT titrated).
highyield:
• V1 receptor (vasoconstriction) + V2 receptor (ADH — water reabsorption)
• VASST trial: add-on to norepinephrine — norepinephrine-sparing but no mortality benefit
• Fixed dose 0.03–0.04 units/min in septic shock — do NOT titrate (risk of ischemia)
• Mesenteric/coronary ischemia risk at higher doses
• Also used for GI varices bleeding (0.2–0.4 units/min) and central DI (intranasal desmopressin preferred)

name: Propofol
moa: Enhances GABA-A receptor chloride conductance (positive allosteric modulator) → CNS depression, sedation, hypnosis, amnesia. May also modulate glycine receptors. Very rapid onset and offset (highly lipid soluble).
indications: ICU sedation (mechanically ventilated patients); procedural sedation; induction/maintenance of general anesthesia; status epilepticus (refractory). SCCM guidelines support light sedation targeting RASS -1 to 0.
dosing: ICU sedation: 5–50 mcg/kg/min (0.3–3 mg/kg/h) IV continuous infusion. Anesthesia induction: 1–2 mg/kg IV. Titrate to RASS/CAM-ICU. Do not exceed 4 mg/kg/h (67 mcg/kg/min) for >48h (propofol infusion syndrome risk).
renal: No dose adjustment needed; metabolized hepatically.
hepatic: Clearance reduced in hepatic failure — titrate to effect.
ades: Propofol infusion syndrome (PRIS): high doses >4 mg/kg/h and/or prolonged use — metabolic acidosis, rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalemia, renal failure, cardiac failure (potentially fatal); hypotension (vasodilation), pain on injection (use large vein/lidocaine pretreatment), hypertriglyceridemia (formulated in 10% soy oil emulsion — 1.1 kcal/mL), bradycardia, apnea (bolus dosing).
monitoring: RASS/sedation score q4–8h; triglycerides (baseline then q48–72h if high dose or prolonged); daily spontaneous awakening trials (SAT); acidosis/lactate/CK if PRIS suspected; caloric intake from propofol (subtract from total nutrition goal).
ddi: CNS depressants (additive), opioids (synergistic sedation + hypotension), beta-blockers/calcium channel blockers (additive bradycardia/hypotension), valproate (may increase propofol levels).
pearls: Propofol infusion syndrome (PRIS): rare but deadly — metabolic acidosis + rhabdomyolysis + cardiac failure + renal failure. Risk factors: high dose, prolonged infusion, low CHO/high fat diet, glucocorticoids, catecholamines. Stop propofol immediately if suspected. Propofol emulsion formulation supports bacterial growth — change IV tubing q12h; aseptic technique mandatory. Caloric density 1.1 kcal/mL must be counted in TPN/nutrition plans. Preferred ICU sedative in ABCDEF bundle (light sedation goal).
trials: ABCDEF bundle (Ely et al.), MENDS trial, SLEAP trial
pk: t½ initial ~2–8 min (redistribution); terminal ~4–7 h. IV only. Protein binding 97–99%. Metabolized by CYP2C9/CYP2B6 and hepatic/extrahepatic glucuronidation. Lipid emulsion formulation.
bbw: PROPOFOL INFUSION SYNDROME (PRIS): Rare but potentially fatal — metabolic acidosis, rhabdomyolysis, cardiac failure, hyperkalemia, renal failure. Risk ↑ with doses >4 mg/kg/h for >48 h.
highyield:
• Rapid onset/offset — preferred for short-term sedation and procedural sedation
• Contains 1.1 kcal/mL from lipid emulsion — count toward daily caloric/lipid goals
• Caution if egg or soy allergy (contains egg lecithin and soybean oil)
• PRIS risk at high doses (>4 mg/kg/h >48 h) — monitor triglycerides, CPK, lactate
• Propofol does NOT provide analgesia — pair with opioid or non-opioid analgesics

name: Cisatracurium
moa: Competitive antagonist at nicotinic acetylcholine receptors at neuromuscular junction → prevents ACh-mediated muscle contraction → skeletal muscle paralysis. Hoffmann elimination (pH/temperature-dependent, non-enzymatic) → independent of renal/hepatic function.
indications: ARDS (early severe ARDS: PaO2/FiO2 <150 — 48h infusion per ACURASYS); facilitate mechanical ventilation (refractory vent dyssynchrony); intubation (if rocuronium not available); status asthmaticus; ICP management.
dosing: Bolus: 0.15–0.2 mg/kg IV. Continuous infusion: 0.5–10 mcg/kg/min. Titrate by train-of-four (TOF) monitoring: target 1–2 twitches/4. Must have adequate sedation/analgesia BEFORE and DURING NMBA use (patient aware but paralyzed = torture).
renal: No dose adjustment required (Hoffmann elimination — organ-independent).
hepatic: No dose adjustment required.
ades: Prolonged weakness/paralysis (ICU-acquired weakness — limit duration), aware paralysis if inadequate sedation (PTSD), hypotension (rare — minimal histamine release, advantage over atracurium), laudanosine accumulation (CNS stimulation — theoretical concern, minimal with cisatracurium), respiratory failure if drug not reversed before extubation.
monitoring: Train-of-four (TOF) monitoring (peripheral nerve stimulator — ulnar nerve, facial nerve); RASS/sedation score BEFORE starting (ensure adequate sedation); daily assessment of need/weaning; ICU-acquired weakness assessment.
ddi: Aminoglycosides/clindamycin (potentiate blockade), magnesium (potentiate), succinylcholine (prior use may change response), volatile anesthetics (potentiate), corticosteroids + NMBAs = high risk ICU-acquired weakness.
pearls: ACURASYS trial (2010): 48h cisatracurium infusion in early severe ARDS (P/F <150) improved 90-day mortality. However ROSE trial (2019) failed to replicate — debate continues. Cisatracurium preferred over atracurium in ICU (less histamine release, no bronchospasm, Hoffmann elimination organ-independent). MANDATORY: ensure adequate sedation before initiating NMBA — paralyzed but aware patient experiences extreme distress. Sugammadex reverses rocuronium; neostigmine/glycopyrrolate reverses cisatracurium.
trials: ACURASYS (Papazian NEJM 2010), ROSE trial (NEJM 2019)
pk: t½ ~22–29 min. IV only. Undergoes Hofmann elimination (pH and temperature-dependent, spontaneous degradation) — not organ-dependent. Minor renal/hepatic excretion of metabolites.
highyield:
• Preferred NMBA in ICU — Hofmann elimination (organ-independent clearance)
• ACURASYS trial: 48-h early cisatracurium in moderate-severe ARDS improved outcomes
• No histamine release (unlike atracurium) — preferred in hemodynamically unstable patients
• Must provide analgesia and sedation concurrently — NMBAs provide no pain relief or sedation
• Train-of-four (TOF) monitoring: target 1–2 twitches of 4 for ICU paralysis

name: Hydrocortisone (Septic Shock)
moa: Binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors → nuclear transcription modulation → anti-inflammatory, immunosuppressive, and vasopressor-sensitizing effects. Restores catecholamine receptor downregulation in critical illness. Mineralocorticoid activity reduces sodium excretion.
indications: Septic shock refractory to fluids and vasopressors (adjunctive therapy to hasten shock resolution); adrenal insufficiency in critical illness; thyroid storm; acute exacerbation of adrenal insufficiency.
dosing: Septic shock: 200 mg/day IV as continuous infusion OR 50 mg IV q6h x 7 days (or until vasopressor weaned). ATHOS-3 (angiotensin II) and ADRENAL/APROCCHSS trials inform practice. Do NOT use ACTH stimulation test to guide therapy (current guidelines: treat empirically if refractory shock).
renal: No dose adjustment needed.
hepatic: No dose adjustment (altered if severe — monitor clinically).
ades: Hyperglycemia (frequent — increase insulin protocol), sodium retention/fluid overload, hypokalemia, muscle weakness, impaired wound healing, immunosuppression (secondary infections), delirium/psychiatric symptoms, GI bleeding (co-administer PPI/H2 blocker), adrenal suppression with prolonged use.
monitoring: Blood glucose q2–4h (tight glycemic control target 140–180 mg/dL per NICE-SUGAR), potassium, sodium, daily vasopressor requirements, infection surveillance, GI prophylaxis.
ddi: NSAIDs (additive GI risk), anticoagulants (variable effects), oral hypoglycemics (antagonism), live vaccines (avoid), CYP3A4 inducers/inhibitors alter metabolism.
pearls: APROCCHSS trial: hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone reduced 90-day mortality in septic shock. ADRENAL trial: no significant mortality benefit but faster shock resolution. Current SSC 2021: recommend 200 mg/day if not responding to fluids + vasopressors. Fludrocortisone (50 mcg/day via NGT) may be added (APROCCHSS used combination). Taper gradually rather than abrupt discontinuation to prevent rebound. Hyperglycemia is the most common and clinically significant complication.
trials: APROCCHSS (Annane NEJM 2018), ADRENAL (Venkatesh NEJM 2018), CORTICUS trial
pk: t½ ~1.5–2 h (plasma). IV/PO. Rapidly absorbed. Protein binding ~90%. Hepatic metabolism (CYP3A4). Excretion: renal (metabolites).
highyield:
• APROCCHSS/ADRENAL trials: corticosteroids shorten vasopressor duration in septic shock but no clear mortality benefit
• Dose: 200 mg/day (50 mg IV q6h or 200 mg/day continuous infusion)
• Use with vasopressor-refractory septic shock (MAP <65 despite adequate fluids + 2 vasopressors)
• Hyperglycemia requiring insulin most common ICU ADE — monitor glucose closely
• Taper over days — do not abruptly discontinue after prolonged use

name: Insulin Glargine
moa: Binds insulin receptors → promotes glucose uptake (GLUT4 translocation) in muscle/fat, suppresses hepatic gluconeogenesis. Glargine: modification at A21 (Asn→Gly) + 2 Arg added to B-chain → isoelectric point shift → precipitates at physiologic pH after SC injection → slow, peakless absorption (T1/2 ~24h). Provides consistent basal coverage.
indications: Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes mellitus — basal insulin component. AACE/ADA recommend as preferred basal insulin. Inpatient hyperglycemia management. Prevention of DKA in T1DM.
dosing: T1DM: 0.2–0.4 units/kg/day basal (50% of total daily dose). T2DM initiation: 10 units SC qHS or 0.1–0.2 units/kg qHS; titrate by 2 units q3d until FBG 80–130 mg/dL. Toujeo (U-300): 80% of Lantus dose equivalent. Once daily (qHS preferred for nocturnal coverage).
renal: Use with caution — insulin requirements may decrease with CKD (reduced insulin clearance and gluconeogenesis). Higher hypoglycemia risk; more frequent monitoring required.
hepatic: Decreased hepatic metabolism in cirrhosis → hypoglycemia risk; monitor closely and reduce doses.
ades: Hypoglycemia (lower risk than NPH — peakless profile; however still occurs), weight gain, lipodystrophy at injection sites (rotate sites), hypokalemia (insulin shifts K+ intracellularly), local injection site reactions.
monitoring: FBG daily (titration target 80–130 mg/dL), A1c q3 months, hypoglycemia episodes, weight, renal function. CGM increasingly standard. InPATIENT: pre-meal and bedtime BG checks.
ddi: Beta-blockers (mask hypoglycemia symptoms except sweating), corticosteroids (severe hyperglycemia — increase dose), fluoroquinolones/sulfonamides (hypoglycemia risk), alcohol (potentiates hypoglycemia), thiazide diuretics (worsen glycemic control).
pearls: Peakless profile reduces nocturnal hypoglycemia risk vs. NPH. CANNOT mix with other insulins (pH mismatch — will form precipitate → loss of long-acting property). Lantus vs. Toujeo: Toujeo (U-300) more concentrated — 3x/mL — different PK, less hypoglycemia risk. Basaglar is biosimilar to Lantus (not identical to Toujeo). In-hospital: use scheduled basal-bolus correction scale (never sliding scale alone — no evidence; causes reactive dosing).
trials: ORIGIN trial (glargine in pre-diabetes), DEVOTE trial (degludec vs. glargine)
pk: Onset ~2–4 h; duration ~20–24 h (no pronounced peak). Bioavailability SC ~60–80%. Protein binding ~5%. Metabolized to active metabolites M1 and M2. Excretion: renal clearance minimal.
highyield:
• Peakless basal insulin — do NOT mix with other insulins (pH change precipitates glargine)
• Toujeo (U-300) has longer duration (~36 h), less nocturnal hypoglycemia vs Lantus
• Basaglar (biosimilar), Semglee (interchangeable biosimilar) — verify interchangeability with prescriber
• FDA warning: medication errors from concentration mix-ups (U-100 vs U-300) — verify clearly
• Dose timing: any consistent time daily; no preference for bedtime

name: Semaglutide
moa: Mimics endogenous GLP-1 → binds GLP-1 receptors: (1) pancreatic beta cells → glucose-dependent insulin secretion + glucagon suppression; (2) CNS (hypothalamus) → appetite suppression + satiety; (3) GI → delayed gastric emptying; (4) liver → reduced hepatic glucose output. CV benefits via direct vascular anti-inflammatory effects.
indications: T2DM glycemic control (Ozempic); cardiovascular risk reduction in T2DM with established CVD (SUSTAIN-6); chronic weight management (Wegovy — BMI ≥30 or ≥27 with comorbidities); recently FDA-approved for CV mortality reduction in obesity regardless of diabetes (SELECT trial — Wegovy 2.4 mg/week).
dosing: Ozempic SC: start 0.25 mg weekly x 4 weeks → 0.5 mg weekly; max 2 mg/week. Rybelsus PO: 3 mg daily x 30d → 7 mg → 14 mg (take 30 min before food with 4 oz water only). Wegovy SC: escalation over 16–20 weeks to 2.4 mg weekly.
renal: No dose adjustment required. Use with caution in severe renal impairment (limited data; GI side effects may worsen dehydration → AKI).
hepatic: No dose adjustment required.
ades: GI dominant: N/V/D (very common, especially initial titration — improves with time), constipation, GERD; pancreatitis (rare but black box warning — monitor amylase/lipase with symptoms); thyroid C-cell tumors (rodent data — black box warning; contraindicated in MEN2/personal/family history of medullary thyroid cancer); gallbladder disease (cholelithiasis, cholecystitis), injection site reactions, acute kidney injury (dehydration from GI side effects).
monitoring: A1c q3 months, weight/BMI, renal function (if GI side effects severe), lipase/amylase if abdominal pain, thyroid function/nodules (surveillance), heart rate (mild increase).
ddi: Oral medications: delayed gastric emptying may reduce absorption rate of oral drugs (administer separately, time-sensitive medications). Insulin/sulfonylureas (additive hypoglycemia — reduce sulfonylurea dose by 50% when initiating).
pearls: Cardiovascular benefits BEYOND glycemic control: SUSTAIN-6 (semaglutide) and SELECT trial (semaglutide 2.4 mg) demonstrated MACE reduction. SELECT trial landmark: first GLP-1 RA to demonstrate CV mortality/MI/stroke reduction in patients without diabetes (obesity alone). Slow titration minimizes GI side effects. Take Rybelsus on empty stomach — significantly better absorption. NOT indicated for T1DM. GLP-1 receptor agonists preferred over other agents in T2DM with established CVD per 2024 ADA standards.
trials: SUSTAIN-6 (CV outcomes T2DM), SELECT trial (semaglutide 2024 — CV in obesity)
pk: t½ ~1 week (SC/PO). Bioavailability SC ~89%; PO ~0.4–1% (taken fasting with small water). Protein binding >99%. Proteolytic degradation. Not CYP-dependent.
bbw: THYROID C-CELL TUMORS: Medullary thyroid carcinoma in rodents. Contraindicated with personal/family history of MTC or MEN2. Not for routine obesity treatment where other options adequate.
highyield:
• SUSTAIN (SC) and PIONEER (oral) trials: superior HbA1c reduction and CV outcomes vs comparators
• SEMAGLUTIDE = SELECT trial: 20% CV event reduction in obese patients without diabetes
• GLP-1 RA: slows gastric emptying (↓ postprandial glucose), satiety, weight loss, pancreatic insulin release
• GI side effects (N/V/D) are dose-limiting — titrate slowly to improve tolerability
• Oral semaglutide must be taken 30 min before first food/drink with ≤4 oz water

name: Empagliflozin
moa: Inhibits sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 (SGLT2) in proximal convoluted tubule → blocks reabsorption of ~90% of filtered glucose → glucosuria (glucose-dependent hypoglycemia low risk). Osmotic diuresis, natriuresis, reduced preload/afterload → cardiorenal benefits (tubuloglomerular feedback restoration, visceral adiposity reduction, ketone body utilization, reduced sympathetic tone).
indications: T2DM glycemic control; HFrEF (reduced hospitalization/mortality regardless of diabetes — EMPEROR-Reduced); HFpEF (EMPEROR-Preserved); CKD with proteinuria (EMPA-KIDNEY — reduced CKD progression regardless of diabetes); CV mortality reduction in T2DM with established CVD (EMPA-REG OUTCOME).
dosing: T2DM/HF/CKD: 10 mg PO daily (may increase to 25 mg daily for additional glycemic effect). Take with or without food. HF indication requires eGFR ≥20 mL/min/1.73m2.
renal: eGFR ≥45: full dose for glycemic control. eGFR 20–44: do not use for glycemic control (insufficient glycosuria) BUT can continue for HF/CKD cardiorenal protection down to eGFR ≥20. Below eGFR 20: not recommended.
hepatic: No dose adjustment (minimal hepatic metabolism). Avoid in severe hepatic impairment (limited data).
ades: UTIs and genital mycotic infections (glucosuria promotes yeast/bacterial growth — counsel hygiene), DKA (euglycemic DKA — can occur at normal/near-normal glucose levels; perioperative hold required), volume depletion/orthostatic hypotension, Fournier's gangrene (rare — necrotizing fasciitis of perineum, black box warning), bone fractures (canagliflozin > others), lower limb amputations (canagliflozin signal), polyuria.
monitoring: eGFR/SCr, blood glucose (lower hypoglycemia risk vs. insulin/SU), blood pressure, genital hygiene symptoms, signs of DKA (even if glucose normal — measure ketones if symptomatic), urine/vaginal cultures if symptomatic.
ddi: Diuretics (additive volume depletion), insulin/sulfonylureas (additive hypoglycemia; reduce dose), lithium (diuresis may affect levels), rifampin (decreases SGLT2i levels).
pearls: Euglycemic DKA is the most dangerous and underrecognized SGLT2i adverse effect — patients may present with anion gap metabolic acidosis, ketonemia, but glucose can be normal or only mildly elevated. Risk factors: prolonged fasting, low-carb diet, surgery, insulin dose reduction, alcohol use, illness. PERIOPERATIVE HOLD: hold SGLT2 inhibitors 3–4 days before elective surgery. EMPEROR-Preserved: first drug to reduce CV outcomes in HFpEF. EMPA-KIDNEY: reduced CKD progression across eGFR spectrum.
trials: EMPA-REG OUTCOME (NEJM 2015), EMPEROR-Reduced, EMPEROR-Preserved, EMPA-KIDNEY (2023)
pk: t½ ~12.4 h. Bioavailability ~86%. Protein binding 86%. Metabolized by UGT1A3/UGT2B7 (glucuronidation; NOT CYP). Excretion: fecal ~41%, renal ~54%.
highyield:
• SGLT2 inhibitor — blocks glucose reabsorption in proximal tubule → glycosuria
• EMPA-REG OUTCOME: 38% reduction in CV death in T2DM with established CVD
• EMPEROR-Reduced/Preserved: 25% RRR in CV death/HF hospitalization in HFrEF and HFpEF
• Euglycemic DKA: monitor for DKA even with near-normal glucose (hold perioperatively)
• UTIs and genital mycotic infections common — counsel on hygiene
• Fournier gangrene: rare but serious — necrotizing fasciitis of perineum

name: Levothyroxine
moa: Exogenous T4 (thyroxine); converted peripherally to active T3 (triiodothyronine) by deiodinases. Binds thyroid hormone receptors (THRalpha, THRbeta) in nucleus → regulates gene transcription affecting metabolism, thermogenesis, protein synthesis, cardiac function, CNS development.
indications: Hypothyroidism (primary, secondary, tertiary); TSH suppression in thyroid cancer/goiter; myxedema coma (high IV doses); Hashimoto's thyroiditis; post-thyroidectomy replacement.
dosing: Adults: 1.6 mcg/kg/day PO (start lower in elderly/cardiac disease: 25–50 mcg/day, titrate slowly). Myxedema coma: 200–400 mcg IV loading, then 50–100 mcg IV daily. Elderly/cardiac: start 25 mcg/day, increase by 25 mcg q4–6 weeks. Administer on empty stomach 30–60 min before breakfast (absorption affected by food/coffee).
renal: No dose adjustment required.
hepatic: Reduced T4-to-T3 conversion in severe liver disease; may require T3 supplementation in some cases.
ades: Iatrogenic hyperthyroidism (overdose): tachycardia, palpitations, atrial fibrillation, tremor, anxiety, insomnia, heat intolerance, weight loss, bone loss (osteoporosis with suppressive therapy), increased metabolic rate. Symptoms of hypothyroidism if underdosed.
monitoring: TSH every 6–8 weeks during dose titration; once stable, q6–12 months. Free T4 (if central hypothyroidism — TSH unreliable). Heart rate, blood pressure, symptoms of hypo/hyperthyroidism. Bone density with long-term suppressive therapy. Lipid panel (hypothyroidism causes dyslipidemia).
ddi: Calcium carbonate, antacids, PPIs, iron, cholestyramine, sucralfate — all reduce absorption (separate by 4h). Warfarin (increased anticoagulant effect — T4 increases vitamin K factor catabolism; monitor INR). Digoxin toxicity risk (worsens at lower T4 levels — monitor at baseline). Rifampin/phenytoin/carbamazepine (increase T4 metabolism — increase dose).
pearls: Narrow therapeutic index — brand substitution can affect thyroid levels (bioequivalence standards allow 20% variation). Morning fasting administration critical for consistent absorption. Coffee can reduce absorption even before oral intake — advise strict fasting. Tirosint (liquid gel cap) has better absorption. TSH is the most sensitive monitoring parameter for primary hypothyroidism. Central hypothyroidism: use free T4 (not TSH) for monitoring. Myxedema coma: IV T4 + stress dose steroids (rule out adrenal insufficiency first).
trials: ATA/AACE guidelines for hypothyroidism management, TRUST trial (LT4 in subclinical hypothyroidism in elderly)
pk: t½ ~6–7 days. Bioavailability ~40–80% (PO) — variable; take fasting 30–60 min before food. Protein binding >99% (TBG, albumin, transthyretin). Converted to T3 (active) peripherally by deiodinases.
bbw: CARDIOVASCULAR TOXICITY in OSTEOPOROSIS MANAGEMENT: Not for weight reduction. Doses within normal reference range when used for weight reduction in euthyroid patients cause no benefit and can cause serious cardiovascular harm.
highyield:
• Take on empty stomach (absorption decreased by food, calcium, iron, antacids, PPIs)
• Separate administration from calcium by ≥4 hours; iron by ≥4 hours; cholestyramine by ≥4 hours
• Narrow therapeutic index — small dose changes cause significant TSH fluctuation
• Pregnancy increases LT4 requirements ~30–50% — check TSH immediately upon pregnancy
• Goal TSH: 0.5–2.5 mIU/L for most patients; lower for thyroid cancer suppression

name: Levetiracetam
moa: Binds synaptic vesicle protein 2A (SV2A) — unique mechanism; modulates synaptic neurotransmitter release (exact mechanism incompletely understood). May also inhibit voltage-gated N-type calcium channels and reduce GABA-A receptor current inhibition. NO sodium channel blockade.
indications: Focal (partial) seizures (adjunctive therapy); generalized tonic-clonic seizures; myoclonic seizures (juvenile myoclonic epilepsy); status epilepticus (IV; 2nd-line after benzodiazepines per guidelines); seizure prophylaxis (head trauma, post-surgical, SAH — institutional protocols vary).
dosing: Adults: 500–1500 mg BID PO/IV (max 3000 mg/day). Status epilepticus: 20–60 mg/kg IV (max 4500 mg) infused over 15 min. Pediatric dosing weight-based. No loading dose required for chronic use. Titrate gradually to improve tolerability.
renal: CrCl 50–80: 500–1000 mg q12h. CrCl 30–49: 250–750 mg q12h. CrCl <30: 250–500 mg q12h. ESRD/HD: 500–1000 mg q24h + 250–500 mg supplement post-dialysis.
hepatic: No dose adjustment for mild-moderate hepatic impairment. Caution in severe impairment.
ades: Behavioral/psychiatric effects: irritability, aggression, hostility, depression (Keppra rage — counsel patients/caregivers), somnolence, dizziness, weakness, anorexia. Serious (rare): anaphylaxis, Steven-Johnson syndrome.
monitoring: Serum levels (reference: 12–46 mcg/mL — though correlation with efficacy is weak; useful for adherence). CBC, BMP. Behavioral assessment. Renal function (CrCl-based dosing).
ddi: Minimal drug interactions (no CYP involvement, no protein binding interactions). Probenecid (inhibits renal clearance — increases levetiracetam levels by ~70% — avoid). Metformin (may compete for renal OCT1/OCT2 — minor interaction).
pearls: Major advantage: minimal drug interactions (no enzyme induction/inhibition) — preferred in poly-pharmacy patients. No protein binding. Renal excretion (dose-adjust for CKD). Psychiatric side effects (Keppra rage) are clinically significant — consider baseline psychiatric evaluation and pyridoxine (B6) supplementation which may mitigate behavioral effects. IV:PO bioavailability is 1:1 (100% oral bioavailability). ESTABLISHED protocol for status epilepticus: benzo first-line → levetiracetam/fosphenytoin/valproate equally second-line (ESETT trial).
trials: ESETT trial (Kapur NEJM 2019 — levetiracetam = fosphenytoin = valproate for benzodiazepine-refractory status epilepticus)
pk: t½ ~7 h. Bioavailability ~100% (PO). Protein binding <10%. NOT hepatically metabolized (esterase hydrolysis). Excretion: renal ~66% unchanged.
highyield:
• Broad-spectrum AED — focal and generalized seizures; also used in status epilepticus
• Psychiatric side effects: irritability, aggression, depression in ~10–20% ("Keppra rage")
• Renal dosing required (CrCl <80): dose intervals extended based on CrCl
• Minimal drug-drug interactions (no hepatic metabolism, no enzyme induction)
• IV and oral are bioequivalent — easy IV-to-PO switch with no dose change

name: Fosphenytoin
class: Hydantoin antiepileptic — sodium channel blocker (prodrug of phenytoin)
moa: Prodrug; rapidly converted to phenytoin by phosphatases. Phenytoin: blocks voltage-gated sodium channels (binds inactivated state) → stabilizes neuronal membranes → reduces neuronal excitability and propagation of seizure activity. Also suppresses ventricular automaticity (Class IB antiarrhythmic).
indications: Status epilepticus (2nd-line after benzodiazepines); seizure prophylaxis (perioperative neurosurgical); conversion from IV to PO phenytoin. Preferred over phenytoin IV (less hypotension, no propylene glycol vehicle, faster infusion rate, IM administration possible).
dosing: Dosed in phenytoin equivalents (PE): Status epilepticus: 15–20 mg PE/kg IV (max infusion rate: 150 mg PE/min). Seizure prophylaxis: 10–20 mg PE/kg IV load. Maintenance: 4–6 mg PE/kg/day divided q8–12h.
renal: No dose adjustment for fosphenytoin per se; phenytoin protein binding altered (reduced albumin binding in uremia — higher free phenytoin levels). Monitor free phenytoin levels in CKD/hypoalbuminemia.
hepatic: Reduced phenytoin metabolism in hepatic failure → higher free levels. Monitor free phenytoin.
ades: Cardiovascular (less than phenytoin IV but still risk): hypotension, bradycardia, arrhythmias (cardiac monitoring required during infusion). Neurologic: nystagmus, ataxia, dizziness, drowsiness (dose-related). Unique fosphenytoin: pruritus/paresthesias of face/groin during infusion (transient, phosphate vehicle). Long-term phenytoin: gingival hyperplasia, hirsutism, coarsening of facial features, peripheral neuropathy, osteoporosis (induces vitamin D catabolism), cognitive effects.
monitoring: Continuous cardiac monitoring during IV loading (ECG, BP, HR). Serum phenytoin levels: total (target 10–20 mcg/mL) or free (target 1–2 mcg/mL). In hypoalbuminemia/uremia: MUST check free phenytoin (or correct total: corrected = measured / [0.2 x albumin + 0.1]). CBC, LFTs, glucose.
ddi: Extensive CYP2C9/2C19 inducer and substrate: induces CYP3A4, 2C9, 2C19 → reduces levels of warfarin, statins, azoles, protease inhibitors, hormonal contraceptives, many others. Valproate displaces phenytoin from binding (↑ free phenytoin levels). Enteral nutrition reduces absorption significantly (hold tube feeds 1h before and after oral phenytoin).
pearls: ESETT trial: levetiracetam = fosphenytoin = valproate for benzodiazepine-refractory SE — no significant difference in efficacy. Saturable (zero-order) kinetics at higher doses: small dose increases → disproportionately large serum level increases (narrow therapeutic index). Purple glove syndrome (phenytoin IV extravasation): tissue necrosis — avoid peripheral IV; fosphenytoin far safer for IM/peripheral use. Check free phenytoin in hypoalbuminemia. NOT enzyme-inducing properties reduced vs. phenytoin.
trials: ESETT (Kapur NEJM 2019), DeToledo et al. fosphenytoin vs phenytoin tolerability
pk: Prodrug → phenytoin (by phosphatases). t½ ~15 min (fosphenytoin); phenytoin t½ ~22 h. Protein binding ~95–99% (fosphenytoin to albumin). Non-linear (saturable, Michaelis-Menten) PK.
bbw: CARDIAC DYSRHYTHMIAS and HYPOTENSION: IV administration must not exceed 150 mg PE/min. Cardiac monitoring required. Severe cardiac toxicity including fatal outcomes with rapid infusion.
highyield:
• Prodrug of phenytoin — dosed in phenytoin equivalents (PE); safer for IV than phenytoin (less cardiotoxicity)
• Non-linear PK: small dose changes → large concentration changes at high levels
• Purple glove syndrome (phenytoin) — tissue necrosis with extravasation; fosphenytoin IM-compatible
• Drug interactions extensive: induces CYP2C9, CYP3A4 (reduces warfarin, OCP, many others)
• Monitor free phenytoin (not total) in hypoalbuminemia: Sheiner-Tozer equation

name: Alteplase (tPA)
moa: Recombinant human tPA; binds fibrin in thrombus → converts plasminogen to plasmin → dissolves fibrin clot. Fibrin-selective (activated mainly in presence of clot-bound plasminogen). Dose-dependent systemic lytic state also occurs.
indications: Acute ischemic stroke (within 3–4.5h of symptom onset); acute massive PE (hemodynamically unstable); STEMI (when PCI unavailable within 120 min); catheter occlusion (Cathflo — lower dose); deep vein thrombosis (selected cases).
dosing: Ischemic stroke: 0.9 mg/kg IV (max 90 mg); 10% as IV bolus over 1 min, remaining 90% over 60 min. Begin within 3h of onset (standard); can extend to 4.5h in selected patients (no prior stroke+diabetes, age <80, not on anticoagulants, no NIHSS >25). Massive PE: 100 mg IV over 2h.
renal: No dose adjustment (hepatically cleared; minimal renal effect).
hepatic: Caution in severe hepatic impairment (coagulopathy risk).
ades: Intracranial hemorrhage (most feared — 6–7% symptomatic ICH in stroke trials), systemic bleeding (GI, urinary, access sites), angioedema (rare but can be severe), reperfusion injury, gingival bleeding.
monitoring: Neurologic assessment q15–30min during/after infusion; BP every 15 min (goal <180/105 during/24h after); hold antithrombotic x 24h after tPA; CT/MRI before tPA (rule out hemorrhage). NIHSS. Serial BP (hypertension management required — labetalol, nicardipine). Avoid Foley/NG/arterial lines for 30 min after infusion if possible.
ddi: Anticoagulants (additive bleeding risk — hold oral anticoagulants before use; check INR/PTT contraindications). Antiplatelet agents (increased ICH risk — aspirin ok, dual antiplatelet avoid in first 24h post-tPA). Heparin (contraindicated concurrently).
pearls: ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATIONS to tPA in stroke: recent surgery (<14d major), active internal bleeding, recent ICH, uncontrolled BP >185/110, blood glucose <50 or >400, platelet <100k, INR >1.7, prior stroke within 3 months. EXTENDED WINDOW (3–4.5h): exclude age >80 + prior stroke + diabetes combination, NIHSS >25, full anticoagulant doses, extensive early ischemic changes. TENECTEPLASE: emerging as simpler alternative to alteplase in stroke (single bolus, similar efficacy, NOR-TEST 2, TASTE-A trials). Mechanical thrombectomy for large vessel occlusion is adjunctive and complementary to tPA.
trials: NINDS tPA stroke trial (NEJM 1995), ECASS III (4.5h window), NOR-TEST 2 (tenecteplase vs alteplase)
pk: t½ ~4–5 min. IV infusion. Rapid hepatic clearance. Fibrin-selective thrombolytic.
highyield:
• IV alteplase: 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg) for ischemic stroke within 4.5 h of onset
• Extend window to 4.5 h: exclude >80 yo with severe stroke AND diabetes AND prior stroke
• BP must be ≤185/110 before initiating; ≤180/105 during/after for 24 h
• Absolute contraindication: intracranial surgery/serious head trauma/prior stroke within 3 months
• Hold antiplatelets and anticoagulants for 24 h post-tPA before resuming

name: Nimodipine
moa: Blocks L-type voltage-gated calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle. Preferential cerebral vasodilator due to high lipophilicity and CNS penetration. Prevents calcium-mediated neuronal cell death after SAH. Reduces delayed cerebral ischemia (DCI) from cerebral vasospasm by attenuating calcium-mediated smooth muscle constriction and neuroprotective mechanisms.
indications: Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage (aSAH): reduces risk of poor neurological outcomes from cerebral vasospasm/DCI (NOT proven to prevent vasospasm radiographically — provides neuroprotection and reduces DCI clinical effects). ONLY FDA indication: aSAH.
dosing: 60 mg PO/NGT q4h x 21 days (begin within 96h of aSAH onset). Must continue for full 21 days regardless of clinical response. If severe hypotension: 30 mg q2h (same total daily dose). ORAL ONLY — IV nimodipine has caused fatalities when given via IV line (catastrophic hypotension).
renal: No dose adjustment (hepatically metabolized).
hepatic: Child-Pugh C: reduce to 30 mg q4h (dramatically reduced metabolism). Child-Pugh A/B: standard dosing with monitoring.
ades: Hypotension (most important clinical concern — can worsen cerebral perfusion pressure in aSAH; use with caution, may need IV fluids/vasopressors), edema, bradycardia, flushing, headache, nausea. FATAL HYPOTENSION if given IV.
monitoring: BP every 4h (hold or reduce dose if SBP <90 mmHg; reassess cerebral perfusion goals — MAP targets per neurosurgery/neurocrit), neurologic exam, transcranial Doppler (vasospasm surveillance), CT/CTA, liver function.
ddi: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (azoles, macrolides, ritonavir) dramatically increase nimodipine levels (profound hypotension risk). CYP3A4 inducers (rifampin, carbamazepine, phenytoin) reduce efficacy. Other antihypertensives (additive BP lowering).
pearls: NEVER INJECT IV — oral liquid has been accidentally administered IV, causing catastrophic hypotension and death. The FDA issued a black box warning; Nymalize oral solution is preferred formulation (capsules should NOT be punctured and drawn up in syringe). Begin within 96h. Full 21-day course is mandatory — do not discontinue early even if patient looks well. Neuroprotective mechanism; may NOT prevent vasospasm on angiography but prevents DCI and improves functional outcomes (NICE-SAH guidelines).
trials: Allen et al. NEJM 1983 (original nimodipine SAH trial), British Aneurysm Nimodipine Trial, NEWTON trial (combination with clazosentan)
pk: t½ ~1–2 h. Bioavailability ~13% (extensive first-pass). Protein binding >95%. Metabolized by CYP3A4. Excretion: hepatic → fecal/renal.
highyield:
• Only calcium channel blocker proven to reduce neurological deficits after subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH)
• Mechanism: cerebral vasodilation reducing vasospasm risk (NOT arterial vasospasm reversal)
• FATAL ERRORS: IV administration instead of oral → cardiovascular collapse (oral-only drug)
• Must label oral solution clearly — multiple fatal IV misadministration cases documented
• Do not hold for hypotension — use cautiously; standard dose 60 mg PO q4h × 21 days

name: Apixaban
class: Direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) — direct Factor Xa inhibitor
moa: Selective, reversible, direct inhibitor of Factor Xa (free and prothrombinase-bound). Prevents conversion of prothrombin to thrombin → anticoagulation. Does NOT inhibit thrombin directly (unlike dabigatran). Predictable pharmacokinetics.
indications: Nonvalvular atrial fibrillation (stroke/systemic embolism prevention — ARISTOTLE); DVT/PE treatment and extended secondary prevention (AMPLIFY); VTE prophylaxis (post-orthopedic surgery); VTE prophylaxis in medically ill patients (APEX — once-daily apixaban).
dosing: NVAF: 5 mg BID; reduce to 2.5 mg BID if 2 of 3: age ≥80, weight ≤60 kg, SCr ≥1.5 mg/dL. DVT/PE treatment: 10 mg BID x 7 days → 5 mg BID. Extended VTE prophylaxis: 2.5 mg BID. Post-surgery: 2.5 mg BID.
renal: ESRD on dialysis: dosing is controversial — FDA-approved 5 mg BID (or 2.5 mg BID if age ≥80 + weight ≤60 kg) based on RENAL-AF, but some guidelines recommend caution. eGFR >25: standard dosing. Monitor renal function every 6–12 months.
hepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C). No dose adjustment for mild-moderate. Caution in moderate hepatic impairment.
ades: Bleeding (major GI bleeding, intracranial hemorrhage — GI bleeding rate similar to warfarin; ICH rate lower than warfarin). Anemia, bruising. No hepatotoxicity signal.
monitoring: Standard: clinical assessment for bleeding. No routine level monitoring required. Anti-Xa activity level for special situations (extremes of weight, renal impairment, suspicion of subtherapeutic dosing, perioperative decisions). CBC, SCr q6–12 months (renal function for dosing decisions).
ddi: P-gp AND CYP3A4 substrate (dual pathway — fewer interactions than some DOACs). P-gp + CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, ritonavir — increase apixaban levels significantly; reduce to 2.5 mg BID or avoid). P-gp + CYP3A4 inducers (rifampin, carbamazepine, phenytoin — decrease apixaban levels ≥50% — avoid combination). Avoid other anticoagulants/dual antiplatelet.
pearls: ARISTOTLE trial: apixaban superior to warfarin for NVAF (lower stroke, ICH, mortality, bleeding). Better safety profile than warfarin (especially ICH). REVERSAL: andexanet alfa (Andexxa) specifically reverses anti-Xa DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban). Andexanet alfa approved for life-threatening bleeding. PERIOPERATIVE MANAGEMENT: hold 1–2 days before low-bleed-risk procedures; 3–5 days for high-bleed-risk procedures depending on renal function. No bridging required for most cases. SPECIAL NOTE: apixaban 2.5 mg BID dose HALF DOSE — do not confuse with full 5 mg BID dose.
trials: ARISTOTLE (NVAF), AMPLIFY (VTE treatment), APEX (VTE prophylaxis medically ill)
pk: t½ ~12 h. Bioavailability ~50%. Protein binding 87%. Metabolized by CYP3A4/P-gp. Excretion: fecal ~56%, renal ~27%.
bbw: PREMATURE DISCONTINUATION: ↑ thrombotic risk in AFib. SPINAL/EPIDURAL HEMATOMA risk.
highyield:
• AMPLIFY: apixaban non-inferior to LMWH/warfarin for DVT/PE with significantly less major bleeding
• Cancer-associated VTE: apixaban now preferred over LMWH (Caravaggio trial, ADAM-VTE)
• Dose for VTE treatment: 10 mg BID × 7 days, then 5 mg BID
• Andexanet alfa (specific reversal); 4-factor PCC as alternative
• Antiphospholipid syndrome (triple positivity): warfarin preferred over apixaban

name: Enoxaparin
moa: Binds antithrombin III (AT-III) → markedly accelerates AT-III inhibition of Factor Xa (primarily) and IIa (thrombin, less so vs. UFH). Higher anti-Xa:anti-IIa ratio (~4:1) vs. UFH (~1:1). Predictable bioavailability (SC), no need for routine PTT monitoring.
indications: DVT/PE treatment; VTE prophylaxis (medical, surgical, orthopedic); NSTEMI/UA (with antiplatelet therapy); STEMI (as adjunct to thrombolytics); bridging anticoagulation for AF/mechanical valves; pregnancy anticoagulation.
dosing: Treatment DVT/PE: 1 mg/kg SC q12h OR 1.5 mg/kg SC q24h. VTE prophylaxis: 40 mg SC q24h (medical) or 30 mg SC q12h (orthopedic). NSTEMI: 1 mg/kg SC q12h. Renal adjustment essential.
renal: CrCl <30: treatment dose reduce to 1 mg/kg SC q24h; prophylaxis 30 mg SC q24h. Avoid or use UFH in ESRD/HD (anti-Xa monitoring required if used in severe CKD). ESRD: use UFH with PTT monitoring or UFH alternative (anti-Xa level if enoxaparin used).
hepatic: No specific adjustment; monitor for coagulopathy (thrombocytopenia, bleeding) in severe liver disease.
ades: Bleeding (major and minor), HIT (lower risk than UFH — ~0.1–1% vs. 3–5%), injection site bruising/hematoma, hyperkalemia (aldosterone suppression — all heparins), osteoporosis (prolonged use), elevated LFTs (transient).
monitoring: Anti-Xa levels (target 0.5–1.0 IU/mL for q12h treatment dosing — 4h post-dose; 1–2 IU/mL for q24h treatment dosing — 4h post-dose; 0.2–0.5 IU/mL for prophylaxis). Monitor platelet count (HIT — check platelet count at baseline and day 5 if receiving >4 days). CBC, SCr. 4T score for HIT.
ddi: Other anticoagulants/antiplatelets (additive bleeding). NSAIDs, SSRIs (bleeding risk). Antiplatelet agents (dual antiplatelet with LMWH — careful monitoring). CANNOT use protamine for full reversal (only reverses ~60% anti-Xa activity); andexanet alfa (off-label, used in practice).
pearls: Preferred over UFH for NSTEMI/UA (ESSENCE trial), VTE treatment, and prophylaxis outside ICU. Predictable PK — no routine monitoring except extremes of weight, pregnancy, renal impairment. Anti-Xa monitoring in: morbidly obese (>120 kg), underweight (<45 kg), CKD, pregnancy. HIT with LMWH is rare but cross-reactivity exists — switch to argatroban or fondaparinux if HIT confirmed. Fondaparinux: anti-Xa only, no anti-IIa, no HIT risk — alternative in HIT for prophylaxis.
trials: ESSENCE (NSTEMI), INTERACT/SYNERGY (NSTEMI/UA), PREVENT (VTE prophylaxis)
pk: t½ ~4.5–7 h (SC). Bioavailability ~92% (SC). Minimal protein binding. NOT significantly metabolized by CYP. Excretion: renal (primarily).
bbw: SPINAL/EPIDURAL HEMATOMA: Risk of paralysis with neuraxial procedures. Consider timing of last dose before epidural placement/removal.
highyield:
• Weight-based dosing: 1 mg/kg SC q12h (treatment) or 40 mg SC daily (prophylaxis)
• Monitor anti-Xa levels in extremes of weight, CKD (accumulation if CrCl <30)
• CrCl <30: reduce to 1 mg/kg daily for treatment (enoxaparin accumulates)
• Protamine partially reverses (~60%): 1 mg per 1 mg enoxaparin (given within 8 h)
• HIT (heparin-induced thrombocytopenia): cross-reacts with UFH antibodies; switch to argatroban/bivalirudin

name: Hydroxyurea
class: Ribonucleotide reductase inhibitor / fetal hemoglobin inducer
moa: Inhibits ribonucleotide reductase → decreases deoxyribonucleotide synthesis → DNA synthesis inhibition (S-phase specific cytotoxic). In sickle cell disease: induces fetal hemoglobin (HbF) production (mechanism unclear — epigenetic, HbF gamma-globin gene induction) → HbF dilutes HbS → reduces polymerization → fewer sickling events.
indications: Sickle cell disease (reduces painful crises, ACS, hospitalizations, transfusion requirements, mortality — BABY HUG, MSH trials); chronic myelogenous leukemia (CML — cytoreduction); essential thrombocythemia; polycythemia vera; head/neck cancer (radiosensitizer).
dosing: Sickle cell: 15 mg/kg/day PO initially; titrate by 5 mg/kg/day q8–12 weeks to maximum tolerated dose (up to 35 mg/kg/day); target HbF >15–20% and MCV increase as pharmacodynamic markers. Myeloproliferative: 15–30 mg/kg/day.
renal: CrCl 10–59: reduce dose by 50%. CrCl <10/dialysis: reduce by 75%, dose after HD session. Primarily renally excreted.
hepatic: No specific dose adjustment guideline; monitor CBC closely.
ades: Myelosuppression (dose-limiting — leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, anemia; monitor CBC), mucositis/GI ulcers (leg ulcers — particularly bothersome in sickle cell), teratogenicity (Category D — effective contraception mandatory), secondary malignancy (leukemogenic — risk low with standard doses for SCD), macrocytosis (MCV increase expected — marker of HbF induction effectiveness).
monitoring: CBC q2 weeks during titration, then q4–8 weeks when stable. Hold if ANC <2000, platelets <80,000, Hgb <4.5 g/dL, reticulocytes <80k (adjust based on baseline for SCD). HbF level every 3 months. SCr, pregnancy test before initiation and periodically.
ddi: Antiretrovirals (didanosine, stavudine — fatal pancreatitis/hepatotoxicity — avoid combination). Other myelosuppressants (additive bone marrow suppression). Radiation (additive toxicity).
pearls: MSH trial: landmark study showing hydroxyurea reduces SCD painful crisis rate by 44%, ACS risk, transfusions. Now standard of care for moderate-severe SCD (recurrent vaso-occlusive crises ≥3/year, ACS, stroke). Teratogenic — must counsel ALL reproductive-age patients. Leg ulcers are a significant quality of life issue — manage proactively. macrocytosis is expected and is a surrogate marker of HbF induction. HbF rise lags 2–4 months after initiation. L-glutamine (Endari) is alternative/adjunct for SCD that doesn't respond to hydroxyurea.
trials: MSH trial (Charache NEJM 1995), BABY HUG trial (Wang LANCET 2011)
pk: t½ ~3–4 h. Bioavailability ~80% (oral). Protein binding minimal. Metabolized to urea (non-enzymatic). Excretion: renal ~50–80% unchanged.
bbw: MYELOSUPPRESSION: Bone marrow suppression (neutropenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia). MUTAGENICITY/CARCINOGENICITY: Secondary leukemias reported with long-term use.
highyield:
• Sickle cell disease: increases fetal hemoglobin (HbF) → decreases sickling
• BABY HUG, MSH trials: reduces pain crises, ACS episodes, transfusion requirements
• Monitor CBC q4–8 weeks during initiation; hold if ANC <2000/mm³ or platelets <80,000
• Teratogenic — reliable contraception required for both partners
• Macrocytosis is expected and confirms adherence — NOT a sign of B12/folate deficiency here

name: Albuterol
moa: Selective beta-2 adrenergic receptor agonist → activates adenylyl cyclase → cAMP → PKA activation → phosphorylates myosin light chain kinase → bronchial smooth muscle relaxation → bronchodilation. Onset 5–15 min (inhaled), peak 30–60 min, duration 4–6h. Also mast cell stabilization.
indications: Acute bronchospasm (asthma, COPD exacerbation, exercise-induced bronchospasm); rescue reliever therapy; acute hyperkalemia (IV/nebulized — shifts K+ intracellularly); adjunct to ventilator management. First-line reliever for all asthma patients.
dosing: Inhaled MDI: 2 puffs (90 mcg/puff) q4–6h PRN; acute: up to q20 min x3 then q1–4h. Nebulization: 2.5–5 mg q20 min x3 doses, then 2.5–10 mg q1–4h. Continuous nebulization: 10–15 mg/h for severe exacerbation. IV/SC (rare): 0.4–0.5 mg q4h. Hyperkalemia: 10–20 mg nebuized over 15 min.
renal: No dose adjustment required.
hepatic: No dose adjustment required.
ades: Tachycardia, palpitations, tremor (skeletal muscle), hypokalemia (intracellular shift — monitor K+ with repeated doses/continuous nebulization), hypomagnesemia, hyperglycemia, worsening hypoxemia (V/Q mismatch — early paradoxical effect), paradoxical bronchospasm (rare).
monitoring: HR, oxygen saturation (SpO2), breath sounds, pulmonary function (PEF, FEV1 in acute setting), potassium and magnesium levels (with repeated/continuous dosing), blood glucose.
ddi: Non-selective beta-blockers (propranolol) — block bronchodilatory effect (use cardioselective BB with caution in asthma/COPD). MAO inhibitors/TCAs — potentiate cardiovascular effects. Diuretics — additive hypokalemia. Digoxin — hypokalemia increases toxicity risk.
pearls: Short-acting beta-agonists are rescue therapy only — scheduled use indicates poor asthma control (add controller therapy). Spacer/valved holding chamber significantly improves MDI lung deposition (especially in acute settings and children). Levalbuterol (R-isomer only) claims fewer side effects vs. racemic albuterol — evidence does not clearly support clinical superiority. For acute severe asthma in ICU: consider heliox-driven albuterol nebulization (improved lower airway delivery). IV magnesium sulfate 2g over 20 min is synergistic with beta-agonists in severe exacerbations.
trials: NAEPP/GINA asthma guidelines, NHLBI 2020 focused update
pk: t½ ~5–8 h (inhaled). Bioavailability ~10–20% inhaled. Protein binding 10%. Hepatic metabolism (SULT1A3). Excretion: renal ~28% unchanged.
highyield:
• Short-acting β2-agonist (SABA) — bronchodilator for acute asthma/COPD relief
• Hypokalemia (K⁺ intracellular shift via β2 stimulation) — useful for hyperkalemia treatment
• Tachycardia and tremor dose-dependent — common side effects
• Spacer/VHC recommended for MDI use (improves lung deposition 2–7×)
• SABA overuse (>2 canisters/month) = signal of uncontrolled asthma — reassess therapy

name: Tiotropium
moa: Blocks muscarinic M3 receptors on bronchial smooth muscle and submucosal glands → sustained bronchodilation (24h duration — kinetically selective for M3 over M2 receptors → fewer cardiac side effects) and reduced mucus secretion. Parasympathetic tone (cholinergic) is the dominant bronchoconstrictor mechanism in COPD.
indications: COPD (first-line LAMA for all symptomatic COPD patients per GOLD guidelines — reduces exacerbations, dyspnea, improves exercise capacity); asthma as add-on therapy to ICS (LAMA add-on to ICS for uncontrolled asthma). Tiotropium is the most studied LAMA in COPD.
dosing: COPD (HandiHaler): 18 mcg dry powder once daily. COPD/Asthma (Respimat): 5 mcg (2 inhalations of 2.5 mcg) once daily. Take at same time each day. Instruct in proper inhaler technique.
renal: CrCl <50: use with caution (renally eliminated — drug accumulates; monitor for anticholinergic side effects). No dose adjustment specified but anticipate higher levels.
hepatic: No dose adjustment required (hepatic metabolism minor).
ades: Dry mouth (most common — anticholinergic), constipation, urinary retention (caution with BPH — anticholinergic effect), blurred vision/narrow-angle glaucoma risk (do NOT get spray in eyes — Respimat), tachycardia, paradoxical bronchospasm. Paradoxical increased COPD mortality concern with higher doses (UPLIFT trial reassured at standard dose — no CV harm).
monitoring: Respiratory symptoms (dyspnea, exacerbation frequency), pulmonary function (FEV1, 6MWT), urinary symptoms, glaucoma, heart rate, inhaler technique assessment.
ddi: Other anticholinergics (additive — avoid concurrent SAMA/ipratropium use when LAMA prescribed). Avoid concurrent use with SAMA (additive anticholinergic but same receptor system — no additive benefit, additive toxicity). Dual bronchodilator: LABA + LAMA combination (Anoro Ellipta, Stiolto Respimat) preferred for moderate-severe COPD.
pearls: UPLIFT trial: tiotropium 18 mcg daily for 4 years in COPD — reduced exacerbations, improved QoL; did not significantly reduce FEV1 decline rate (primary endpoint). Reassured CV safety concerns. INSPIRE trial: tiotropium vs salmeterol/fluticasone — similar exacerbation rates but tiotropium better for some COPD subtypes. Dual bronchodilator (LABA + LAMA) superior to monotherapy in moderate-severe COPD. Inhaler technique is critical — HandiHaler vs. Respimat have different techniques; ensure patient education.
trials: UPLIFT trial (Tashkin NEJM 2008), INSPIRE, GOLD COPD guidelines 2024
pk: t½ ~5–6 days (terminal). Bioavailability ~19% (inhaled). Protein binding ~72%. Minimal hepatic metabolism. Excretion: renal ~74% (unchanged).
highyield:
• Long-acting muscarinic antagonist (LAMA) — once-daily COPD maintenance
• UPLIFT trial: significantly reduced exacerbations, improved lung function vs placebo
• Anticholinergic side effects: dry mouth (most common), urinary retention, constipation, acute narrow-angle glaucoma
• HandiHaler (DPI 18 mcg) vs Respimat (SMI 2.5 mcg daily) — both effective but Respimat has more cardiovascular safety data
• Rinse mouth after use to reduce dry mouth and GI absorption

name: Fluticasone/Salmeterol
class: Inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) + Long-acting beta-2 agonist (LABA) combination
moa: Fluticasone: activates glucocorticoid receptors → nuclear transactivation/transrepression → suppresses airway inflammation (cytokines, eicosanoids, mediators), reduces mucosal edema and mucus production. Salmeterol: selective beta-2 agonist (12–24h duration) → bronchodilation via cAMP pathway. Synergistic: ICS upregulates beta-2 receptors; LABA enhances GR nuclear translocation.
indications: Persistent asthma (uncontrolled on ICS alone — controller therapy); COPD (moderate-severe with exacerbation history — reduces exacerbation rate). GINA step 3–5 asthma. GOLD group B/D COPD (combined with LAMA = triple therapy).
dosing: Asthma: Advair 100/50, 250/50, 500/50 mcg (fluticasone/salmeterol) 1 inhalation BID via Diskus. HFA: Advair HFA 45/21, 115/21, 230/21 mcg 2 inhalations BID. COPD: 250/50 or 500/50 mcg Diskus 1 inhalation BID. Rinse mouth after each use.
renal: No dose adjustment required.
hepatic: Fluticasone hepatically metabolized (CYP3A4) — increased exposure with hepatic impairment; consider lower doses.
ades: ICS-related: oral candidiasis/thrush (prevent by rinsing/gargling after use + spacer), dysphonia (hoarseness — vocal cord myopathy), adrenal suppression (high dose, long duration — especially fluticasone vs. budesonide — assess HPA axis), pneumonia risk in COPD (TORCH trial), osteoporosis (high doses). LABA-related: tachycardia, palpitations, tremor. BLACK BOX WARNING (LABA): increased risk of asthma-related death when LABAs used without ICS — do not use salmeterol monotherapy in asthma.
monitoring: Pulmonary function tests (FEV1, PEF), asthma control questionnaire, exacerbation frequency, oral cavity inspection (candida), height in pediatric patients (ICS may affect growth — use lowest effective dose), bone density with prolonged high doses, HPA axis assessment.
ddi: CYP3A4 inhibitors (ritonavir, ketoconazole) — dramatically increase fluticasone levels → adrenal suppression (Cushing's syndrome) — CONTRAINDICATED (ritonavir) or use with extreme caution. Non-selective beta-blockers (antagonize salmeterol). Beta-2 agonist interactions.
pearls: SMART trial: salmeterol monotherapy (without ICS) doubled asthma-related deaths — BLACK BOX warning; LABAs should NEVER be used as monotherapy for asthma. TORCH trial: ICS/LABA in COPD reduced exacerbations but increased pneumonia risk (NNH 15 for additional pneumonia per year with fluticasone — monitor vigilantly). Step down therapy when asthma well-controlled >3 months. Budesonide/formoterol (Symbicort) alternative with potentially lower adrenal suppression. Rinse mouth after each use to prevent candida.
trials: SMART trial (FDA black box salmeterol), TORCH trial (ICS/LABA in COPD), GINA/GOLD guidelines
pk: Fluticasone propionate t½ ~7.8 h; bioavailability ~1–2% inhaled (first-pass). Salmeterol t½ ~5.5 h; bioavailability ~2–3% inhaled. Both: CYP3A4 metabolism.
bbw: LABA MONOTHERAPY WITHOUT ICS IN ASTHMA: Salmeterol monotherapy increases risk of asthma-related death (SMART trial). Use only in combination with ICS in asthma.
highyield:
• ICS/LABA combination — standard maintenance for persistent asthma and COPD
• LABA alone in asthma is contraindicated — must combine with ICS (FDA SMART trial safety signal)
• CYP3A4 inhibitors (ritonavir, ketoconazole): ↑ fluticasone → systemic corticosteroid effects (Cushing's)
• Rinse mouth and gargle after each use — prevent oropharyngeal candidiasis
• Step down ICS dose if well-controlled; do not abruptly discontinue ICS/LABA

name: Roflumilast
moa: Selectively inhibits PDE4 → prevents cAMP degradation in airway inflammatory cells (neutrophils, eosinophils, macrophages, T cells) → elevated intracellular cAMP → decreased inflammatory mediator release (TNF-alpha, IL-6, IL-8, LTB4) and smooth muscle effects → anti-inflammatory bronchodilation. Targets neutrophilic inflammation (COPD mechanism) rather than eosinophilic (unlike ICS).
indications: Severe COPD associated with chronic bronchitis and frequent exacerbations (GOLD D, exacerbation ≥2/year) as add-on to bronchodilator therapy — specifically for patients with chronic bronchitis phenotype and high exacerbation risk. Reduces rate of moderate/severe COPD exacerbations.
dosing: 500 mcg PO once daily WITH food (reduces GI side effects). Begin at 250 mcg daily x 4 weeks then titrate to 500 mcg (optional step-up to improve tolerance). Oral tablet only.
renal: No dose adjustment required (primarily non-renally eliminated).
hepatic: Contraindicated in moderate-severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh B or C — extensive hepatic metabolism, drug accumulation).
ades: GI: nausea, diarrhea, vomiting, abdominal pain (common, especially initial weeks — most resolve), weight loss (average 2 kg — intentional in some patients, concerning in cachectic COPD patients — monitor closely), psychiatric: depression, insomnia, anxiety, suicidal ideation (BLACK BOX WARNING), headache.
monitoring: Weight (baseline and monthly — discontinue if unexplained significant weight loss), mood/behavior assessment (depression, suicidal ideation — BLACK BOX), pulmonary function (FEV1), exacerbation frequency, GI tolerability.
ddi: CYP1A2/CYP3A4 substrate: CYP3A4 inhibitors/CYP1A2 inhibitors (fluvoxamine, enoxacin) — increase roflumilast levels. CYP inducers (rifampin, phenobarbital, carbamazepine) — reduce efficacy significantly. Theophylline (additive PDE inhibition — not recommended). Moderate CYP inhibitors: caution.
pearls: HERMES/M2-127/M2-128 trials: roflumilast 500 mcg reduced moderate/severe COPD exacerbations by ~17% in patients with chronic bronchitis and severe COPD. BEST clinical use: GOLD group D patients with chronic bronchitis (productive cough ≥3 months x 2 consecutive years), FEV1 <50%, ≥2 exacerbations/year who remain symptomatic despite triple inhaler therapy. BLACK BOX: psychiatric events — screen for depression/suicidal ideation before prescribing. Weight loss can be clinically significant in already malnourished COPD patients. Not a bronchodilator — does not provide acute symptom relief.
trials: M2-127, M2-128, HERMES trial (Calverley LANCET 2009 — COPD exacerbation reduction)
pk: t½ ~17 h (roflumilast), ~30 h (N-oxide active metabolite). Bioavailability ~80% (oral). Protein binding ~99% (roflumilast), ~97% (N-oxide). Metabolized by CYP3A4/CYP1A2. Excretion: renal ~70%.
highyield:
• PDE4 inhibitor — anti-inflammatory; reduces exacerbations in severe COPD with chronic bronchitis
• NOT a bronchodilator — does not acutely relieve dyspnea
• Psychiatric side effects: depression, insomnia, suicidal ideation — screen and monitor
• Significant weight loss (~2 kg average) — monitor weight; avoid in underweight patients
• Theophylline combination not recommended; CYP inducers (rifampin) reduce efficacy

name: Furosemide
moa: Inhibits Na-K-2Cl cotransporter (NKCC2) in thick ascending limb of loop of Henle → impairs urinary concentration → profound natriuresis and diuresis. Also inhibits carbonic anhydrase (minor). Onset IV: 5 min; PO: 30–60 min. Duration IV: 2h; PO: 6–8h.
indications: Edema (HF, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, renal failure); acute decompensated heart failure (ADHF); hypertension (adjunct); hypercalcemia; hyperkalemia (promote kaliuresis); oliguric AKI (controversially — does not improve outcomes, only promotes urine output).
dosing: IV ADHF: 1–2.5× oral dose IV (e.g., if on furosemide 40 mg PO → give 40–80 mg IV); DOSE trial: high-dose (2.5×) and continuous infusion both effective. Continuous infusion: 5–20 mg/h after bolus. PO: 20–600 mg/day in divided doses. Renal dosing: higher doses needed as GFR drops (drug needs to reach tubular lumen — secretion by OAT1/3 impaired in uremia; compete with uremic toxins).
renal: Higher doses required in CKD/ESRD (reduced tubular secretion, tubular insensitivity). ESRD: may require 160–600 mg doses. Monitor electrolytes closely.
hepatic: Use with caution in cirrhosis — electrolyte imbalances precipitate hepatic encephalopathy. Spironolactone + furosemide (5:2 ratio) preferred for cirrhotic ascites.
ades: Hypokalemia (potassium wasting — monitor K+, supplement), hyponatremia, hypomagnesemia, hypocalcemia (vs. thiazides which cause hypercalcemia), metabolic alkalosis (contraction alkalosis), ototoxicity (high doses IV, esp. with aminoglycosides), volume depletion/prerenal azotemia, gout (uricosuria decreased → hyperuricemia), photosensitivity. Sulfonamide-based — cross-reactivity consideration.
monitoring: BMP (Na+, K+, Mg2+, BUN/Cr) daily when IV or dose changes; urine output; daily weight; signs of volume depletion (orthostasis, dry mucous membranes). Spot urine Na+ can gauge adequacy of response.
ddi: Aminoglycosides/cisplatin (additive ototoxicity), NSAIDs (decreased diuretic effect + AKI risk), probenecid (blocks tubular secretion → decreased effect), lithium (increased toxicity — decreased clearance), digoxin (hypokalemia increases toxicity risk), corticosteroids (additive hypokalemia).
pearls: Loop diuretics work in loop of Henle — effective even when GFR is low (unlike thiazides, which need GFR >30). DOSE trial: continuous infusion vs. intermittent bolus — no significant difference in outcomes; intermittent bolus preferred for most. Furosemide resistance: consider adding thiazide (metolazone 2.5–5 mg 30 min before furosemide) for synergistic sequential nephron blockade. Acoustic nerve monitoring if prolonged high-dose IV use.
trials: DOSE trial (Felker NEJM 2011 — high vs. low dose, bolus vs. continuous), CARRESS-HF
pk: t½ ~2 h. Bioavailability 60–70% (oral, variable; IV 100%). Protein binding ~91–99%. Minimal hepatic metabolism. Excretion: renal ~66% unchanged.
bbw: EXCESSIVE DIURESIS: Profound water/electrolyte depletion, circulatory collapse, thrombosis, embolism. Associated with deafness when given IV rapidly or with aminoglycosides.
highyield:
• Ceiling effect: dose-response reaches plateau (max effective dose); continuous infusion > intermittent bolus for severe HF
• Tubular secretion (OAT pathway) — reduced efficacy in renal failure, NSAID use, or probenecid
• Hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, metabolic alkalosis — monitor and replete
• Ototoxicity with IV administration >4 mg/min or concurrent aminoglycosides/vancomycin
• Albumin binding: in hypoalbuminemia, less free drug delivered to tubule → diuretic resistance

name: Sodium Bicarbonate
moa: Bicarbonate (HCO3−) is the body's primary buffer: HCO3− + H+ → H2CO3 → H2O + CO2. IV administration raises serum pH. Also shifts K+ intracellularly (useful for acute hyperkalemia), alkalinizes urine (enhances excretion of weak acids), and replaces renal bicarbonate losses in RTA/CKD.
indications: Severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.1, bicarbonate <10 mEq/L — controversial); cardiac arrest (prolonged, with adequate ventilation); hyperkalemia (temporizing measure — shifts K+ intracellularly); TCA overdose (alkalinization for cardiac toxicity); urinary alkalinization (rhabdomyolysis protection, uric acid stones, salicylate overdose); RTA type I/II; CKD-associated metabolic acidosis (oral).
dosing: Cardiac arrest/severe acidosis: 1 mEq/kg IV bolus. Metabolic acidosis: calculated deficit = (24 - current HCO3) × 0.5 × weight (kg); give 50% over 4–6h. Hyperkalemia: 50 mEq IV over 5 min (temporizing). Oral CKD: 650 mg TID–QID titrated to HCO3 target 22–26 mEq/L.
renal: Monitor fluid balance (hyperosmolarity + volume load). Titrate to serum HCO3 levels. In CKD, oral supplementation reduces CKD progression (BAST trial evidence).
hepatic: No specific adjustment; avoid in patients with sodium-sensitive conditions (cirrhosis with ascites).
ades: Metabolic alkalosis (overshoot), hypernatremia (high sodium load — 50 mEq NaHCO3 = 50 mEq Na+), volume overload, hypocalcemia (alkalosis shifts Ca2+ to albumin-bound state → ionized Ca2+ drops → tetany, seizures), hypokalemia, paradoxical CSF acidosis (CO2 crosses BBB faster than HCO3), tissue necrosis if extravasation.
monitoring: Arterial blood gas (pH, pCO2, HCO3), serum electrolytes (Na+, K+, Ca2+), ionized calcium (especially if tetany), osmolality, fluid status.
ddi: Quinolones, tetracyclines, iron supplements (decreased absorption with oral NaHCO3 — separate by 2h). Lithium (alkalinization increases renal excretion — may decrease lithium levels). Aspirin (alkalinization increases salicylate excretion — use intentionally in overdose). Amphetamines (decreased excretion with alkaline urine — retain in body).
pearls: Controversial in cardiac arrest: routine use NOT recommended (ACC/AHA guidelines) — CO2 generated can worsen intracellular acidosis and impair hemodynamics; use after prolonged arrest or specific indications (TCA toxicity, hyperkalemia). In TCA overdose: target serum pH 7.45–7.55 (alkalemia narrows Na+ channel blockade — monitor QRS width). Urinary alkalinization: maintain urine pH >6.5 for rhabdomyolysis protection or uric acid nephrolithiasis. Monitor ionized calcium carefully — alkalosis drops ionized Ca2+.
trials: BICAR-ICU trial (Jaber LANCET 2018 — bicarbonate in severe metabolic acidosis in ICU)
pk: Endogenous buffer. IV infusion or oral tablets. Distributes to extracellular fluid. Rapidly dissociates to Na⁺ and HCO₃⁻. Renal regulation of bicarbonate.
highyield:
• Indications: metabolic acidosis (pH <7.1), hyperkalemia (temporizing), TCA overdose (wide QRS), urinary alkalinization
• Overshoot alkalosis risk — titrate carefully; goal HCO₃⁻ 15–20 mEq/L (not full correction)
• Hypokalemia and hypocalcemia worsen with rapid administration (K⁺ and Ca²⁺ shift into cells)
• Incompatible with calcium solutions (precipitates calcium carbonate)
• Urine alkalinization increases excretion of salicylates, phenobarbital, methotrexate

name: Sevelamer
moa: Polymer containing multiple amine groups that protonates in GI tract → positively charged → binds dietary phosphate anions in intestinal lumen via ion exchange and hydrogen bonding → prevents phosphate absorption → reduces serum phosphate. Also binds bile acids (LDL-lowering effect) and uric acid. NOT absorbed systemically.
indications: Hyperphosphatemia in CKD stages 3–5, dialysis-dependent patients. Preferred over calcium-based binders when serum Ca2+ is elevated or when cardiovascular calcification risk is a concern.
dosing: Initial: 800 mg TID with meals (based on serum phosphorus level: 3.5–5.5 mg/dL → 800 mg; 5.5–7.5 → 1200–1600 mg; >7.5 → 1600 mg TID). Titrate by 400–800 mg/meal q2 weeks to target phosphorus <5.5 mg/dL. Must be taken WITH meals.
renal: Used specifically in CKD/ESRD. No systemic absorption → no renal dose adjustment.
hepatic: No dose adjustment needed (not absorbed).
ades: GI (nausea, vomiting, constipation, diarrhea, dyspepsia — most common; causes poor adherence), dysphagia (large tablets — use powder form for swallowing difficulty), pill burden (large number of tablets/day), metabolic acidosis (Renagel HCl version delivers chloride load — use Renvela carbonate to avoid), bowel obstruction/perforation (rare, serious).
monitoring: Serum phosphorus (target CKD-MBD guidelines: dialysis patients <5.5 mg/dL), calcium, PTH (secondary hyperparathyroidism monitoring), pill burden assessment, lipid panel (sevelamer lowers LDL — monitor), CBC (anemia of CKD).
ddi: Multiple medications: sevelamer can bind certain drugs (mycophenolate, quinolones, levothyroxine, warfarin) → decreased absorption. Administer these drugs 1–3h before or 3h after sevelamer. Reduced bile acid absorption may affect fat-soluble vitamin absorption (A, D, E, K).
pearls: LANDMARK/DCOR trials: sevelamer vs. calcium-based binders — sevelamer does NOT reduce mortality but associated with fewer hypercalcemic episodes and possibly less vascular calcification progression. Calcium carbonate is cheaper and effective first-line if Ca2+ is normal. Pill burden is a major adherence barrier (often 9–12 tablets/day with meals). Renvela (carbonate) preferred over Renagel (HCl) to avoid exacerbating metabolic acidosis in CKD patients.
trials: DCOR trial (Suki NEJM 2007), LANDMARK trial, INDEPENDENT trial
pk: NOT absorbed systemically — acts locally in GI tract. Binds phosphate in intestinal lumen → fecal excretion. No systemic PK/PD of note.
highyield:
• Non-calcium phosphate binder — preferred in CKD patients with hypercalcemia or vascular calcification
• Sevelamer HCl (Renagel) can worsen metabolic acidosis; sevelamer carbonate (Renvela) is bicarbonate-buffered (preferred)
• Reduces LDL cholesterol as secondary benefit (bile acid binding)
• Take with meals — must be taken with food; never crush tablets (risk of expansion)
• Separate administration of levothyroxine, fluoroquinolones, and other drugs by ≥1–2 hours (binds drugs)

name: Pantoprazole
moa: Irreversibly inhibits H+/K+-ATPase (proton pump) on gastric parietal cell secretory canaliculus → >90% reduction of acid secretion. Prodrug: acid-activated in secretory canaliculus → sulfenamide derivative → covalent binding to cysteines on proton pump. New acid secretion requires synthesis of new proton pump proteins (48–72h for full resynthesis after stopping PPI).
indications: GERD/erosive esophagitis (first-line); peptic ulcer disease (treatment + H. pylori eradication triple/quad therapy); stress ulcer prophylaxis (SUP) in mechanically ventilated ICU patients, coagulopathic, or major head/burn trauma; upper GI bleed (IV — high-dose infusion post-endoscopy); NSAID gastropathy prevention; Zollinger-Ellison syndrome.
dosing: GERD: 40 mg PO/IV daily. Erosive esophagitis: 40 mg PO BID x 8 weeks. PUD (H. pylori): 40 mg BID x 10–14d + antibiotics. Upper GI bleed: 80 mg IV bolus then 8 mg/h continuous x 72h post-hemostasis. SUP ICU: 40 mg IV/PO daily (PEPTIC trial — IV vs. PO equivalent).
renal: No dose adjustment needed. Minimal renal elimination (primarily hepatic metabolism).
hepatic: Reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment (max 20 mg/day). CYP2C19 metabolism reduced in poor metabolizers — higher AUC.
ades: Generally well-tolerated. Long-term risks: hypomagnesemia (>1 year — monitor Mg2+ annually), C. difficile risk (↑gastric pH promotes C. diff germination), pneumonia risk (aspiration with reduced acid barrier), bone fractures/osteoporosis (reduced Ca2+ absorption), B12 deficiency (intrinsic factor needs acid for B12 absorption), CKD association (observational — not clearly causal), SIADH (rare), interstitial nephritis.
monitoring: Serum magnesium annually with long-term use; H. pylori confirmation of eradication (UBT or stool antigen 4 weeks post-treatment); reassess SUP indication regularly (de-escalate when ICU risk factors resolve — prevent "PPI creep"); B12 levels with prolonged use in elderly.
ddi: Clopidogrel (CYP2C19 inhibition — reduced clopidogrel activation → decreased antiplatelet effect; pantoprazole has weaker interaction than omeprazole). Methotrexate (high-dose — reduced renal clearance). Atazanavir/rilpivirine (requires acid for absorption — contraindicated with PPIs). Mycophenolate (reduced absorption). Iron, ketoconazole (reduced absorption in alkaline environment).
pearls: PEPTIC trial: IV vs. PO PPI in ICU upper GI bleeding — equivalent outcomes; oral preferred when gut works. SUP de-escalation critical: only appropriate for high-risk ICU patients (mechanical ventilation >48h, coagulopathy, major burns/head trauma — ACCP/SCCM criteria). Most "PPI-prescribed" patients in hospital do NOT have a valid indication. Pantoprazole preferred over omeprazole in clopidogrel patients (weaker CYP2C19 inhibition). Take 30–60 min before meals for optimal efficacy (need active proton pumps). Long-term PPI: lowest effective dose, reassess annually.
trials: PEPTIC trial (NEJM 2020 — IV vs. oral PPI), SUP-ICU trial, TRITON-TIMI 38 (PPI + clopidogrel)
pk: t½ ~1–2 h. Bioavailability ~77% (oral). Protein binding ~98%. Metabolized by CYP2C19 (major), CYP3A4 (minor). Excretion: renal ~71% (metabolites).
highyield:
• PPI — irreversible H⁺/K⁺-ATPase inhibitor; CYP2C19 genotype affects efficacy
• Hypomagnesemia with long-term use (>1 year) — check Mg²⁺ periodically
• Clopidogrel interaction: PPIs (especially omeprazole > pantoprazole) reduce active metabolite via CYP2C19
• C. difficile and community-acquired pneumonia risk with prolonged PPI therapy
• In ICU stress ulcer prophylaxis: PPIs superior to H2RAs for high-risk patients (mechanically ventilated, coagulopathy)

name: Rifaximin
moa: Inhibits bacterial RNA synthesis by binding beta-subunit of bacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase → broad-spectrum antimicrobial effect concentrated in GI tract (>99.6% remains in gut lumen — <0.4% systemic absorption). Reduces ammonia-producing bacteria in gut → lowers serum ammonia in hepatic encephalopathy. Also modulates gut microbiome.
indications: Hepatic encephalopathy (HE) — prevention of recurrence (FDA-approved with lactulose); traveler's diarrhea (non-invasive E. coli strains); IBS-D (irritable bowel syndrome with diarrhea — 2-week course); small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO — off-label).
dosing: HE prophylaxis: 550 mg PO BID (with or without lactulose). Traveler's diarrhea: 200 mg TID x 3 days. IBS-D: 550 mg TID x 14 days (can retreat for recurrence).
renal: No dose adjustment (not systemically absorbed, excreted unchanged in feces).
hepatic: Cirrhotic patients: modest increase in systemic exposure (up to 20-fold increase due to portosystemic shunting) — but still far below systemic antibiotic levels; no dose adjustment required based on current evidence.
ades: Generally excellent tolerability (minimal systemic effects due to non-absorption). GI: nausea, abdominal pain, bloating, constipation (less than antibiotics). Peripheral edema (rare). C. difficile risk is LOW (maintains normal gut flora better than systemic antibiotics). Hepatotoxicity (very rare).
