monitoring: Mental status/HE grade (for HE prevention), liver function tests, serum ammonia (clinical correlation — ammonia level doesn't always predict HE severity), number of HE episodes per year, INR/albumin (liver function markers).
ddi: P-glycoprotein substrate but negligible interactions due to non-absorption. Warfarin (some reports of increased INR — monitor). Cyclosporine (modest increase in rifaximin exposure in cirrhosis — monitor).
pearls: RFHE trial: rifaximin 550 mg BID reduced HE recurrence vs. placebo (NNT ~4) and reduced hospitalizations. Combination with lactulose more effective than either alone. Cost is significant ($1,500+/month) — access and adherence barriers. Unlike systemic antibiotics, rifaximin preserves gut microbiome diversity. For IBS-D: non-antibiotic mechanism proposed (microbiome modulation) — "gut-selective" antibiotic. SIBO: off-label but widely used (1200–1600 mg/day x 10–14 days).
trials: RFHE trial (Bass NEJM 2010 — rifaximin HE prevention), ILLUMINATE trial (IBS-D)
pk: t½ ~6 h. Bioavailability <0.4% (poorly absorbed — acts locally in GI). Protein binding ~68% (in plasma if absorbed). Metabolized minimally; primarily excreted fecally intact.
highyield:
• Minimally absorbed antibiotic — gut-selective; not for systemic infections
• FDA-approved: hepatic encephalopathy (HE) secondary prophylaxis (with or without lactulose), travelers' diarrhea, IBS-D
• RFHE trial: reduced HE recurrence by 58% vs placebo on top of lactulose
• No significant drug interactions (poor absorption); minimal systemic side effects
• Rifaximin + lactulose combination more effective than lactulose alone for HE

name: Lactulose
moa: Non-absorbed synthetic disaccharide (galactose + fructose). Colonic bacteria ferment lactulose → lactic acid, acetic acid, formic acid → acidifies colonic contents (pH 5–5.5) → traps NH3 as NH4+ (ionized, non-absorbable) → reduces ammonia absorption. Osmotic effect draws water into colon → laxative effect (target 2–3 soft stools/day). Reduces blood ammonia.
indications: Hepatic encephalopathy — acute treatment and prophylaxis (standard first-line agent); constipation (occasional use); portal-systemic encephalopathy.
dosing: Acute HE: 25–30 g (30–45 mL) PO/NGT q1–2h until bowel movement; then 3–4x/day to target 2–3 soft stools/day. Maintenance: 20–30 g TID–QID (adjust to bowel habits). Rectal enema (if unable to take PO): 300 mL in 700 mL water, retained 30 min q4–6h. AVOID over-laxation (→ dehydration, electrolyte disturbances worsens HE).
renal: No dose adjustment (not absorbed).
hepatic: Primary use is in hepatic patients — no dose adjustment for hepatic impairment specifically; dose titrated by stool output.
ades: Abdominal cramping, bloating, flatulence, diarrhea (excessive dosing → hyponatremia, dehydration, aspiration risk). Nausea/vomiting. Over-laxation can worsen HE (hyponatremia and prerenal azotemia worsen encephalopathy). Hypernatremia (with concurrent electrolyte abnormalities).
monitoring: Stool frequency/consistency (target 2–3 soft stools/day), mental status/orientation, serum electrolytes (especially Na+, K+), volume status (beware dehydration). Ammonia levels (clinical correlation — absolute value less important than trend + mental status).
ddi: Non-absorbable antibiotics (neomycin — additive NH3 reduction; historical combination now replaced by rifaximin). Antacids (may reduce lactulose bacterial fermentation). Other laxatives (additive diarrhea risk).
pearls: First-line agent for hepatic encephalopathy; rifaximin is adjunctive add-on (not monotherapy replacement). Patient adherence is a major issue (taste is very sweet/unpleasant, causes significant bloating). Individual dose titration to stool frequency is more important than fixed dosing. Over-treatment (>4 watery stools/day) causes dehydration and electrolyte disturbances that can worsen encephalopathy — educate patients/caregivers. For patients who cannot take PO (coma), rectal administration is appropriate.
trials: SONIC trial, combined rifaximin+lactulose HE studies (Bass NEJM 2010)
pk: NOT absorbed (poorly hydrolyzed by intestinal disaccharidases). Acts as osmotic laxative in colon. Fermented by colonic bacteria to acidic metabolites.
highyield:
• Hepatic encephalopathy: titrate dose to 2–4 soft stools/day (NOT watery diarrhea)
• Mechanism in HE: colonic acidification traps NH₃ as NH₄⁺ (non-absorbable); cathartic effect
• Hypernatremia risk with excessive use (osmotic diarrhea → free water loss)
• Diabetes: contains some galactose — monitor glucose in diabetic patients
• Palatability issues common — mix with juice; alternative formulations available

name: Sertraline
moa: Selectively inhibits serotonin transporter (SERT) → blocks reuptake of serotonin (5-HT) from synaptic cleft → increased synaptic serotonin concentrations → downregulation of postsynaptic 5-HT1A/2A receptors over 2–4 weeks (explains therapeutic delay). Minimal effect on norepinephrine and dopamine reuptake. Some sigma-1 receptor activity.
indications: Major depressive disorder (MDD); panic disorder; OCD; PTSD; social anxiety disorder; premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD). First-line SSRI for most psychiatric indications. Safe in cardiac disease (SADHART trial).
dosing: MDD/anxiety: Start 25–50 mg/day PO; target 50–200 mg/day; max 200 mg/day. PMDD: 50 mg/day (cycle days 14–28 or continuously). Titrate slowly in elderly (start 25 mg, max 100 mg) — more sensitive to side effects. At least 4 weeks before dose adjustment (adequate trial 8–12 weeks).
renal: No dose adjustment needed (minimal renal elimination).
hepatic: Reduce dose by 50% in hepatic impairment (decreased metabolism — increased exposure).
ades: GI (N/V/D — most common early, often resolve in 2–4 weeks), sexual dysfunction (decreased libido, delayed orgasm/ejaculation — common and often persistent; use as switching consideration), weight gain (modest, long-term), insomnia or somnolence, headache, dry mouth. QTc prolongation (modest, less than TCAs). Hyponatremia (SIADH — elderly, women, low body weight). Bruising/bleeding (platelet serotonin depletion). Activation/agitation early in treatment (monitor in anxiety/suicidal patients). Serotonin syndrome (with serotonergic combinations).
monitoring: Mental status (suicidal ideation — black box warning for pediatric/young adults ≤24; assess at 1, 2, 4 weeks initially), therapeutic response assessment at 4–8 weeks, metabolic monitoring (weight, glucose), sexual function, sleep, QTc at baseline if cardiac risk factors, sodium if elderly.
ddi: MAO inhibitors (serotonin syndrome — 14-day washout required; 5 weeks for fluoxetine). Serotonergic agents (triptans, tramadol, fentanyl, linezolid — serotonin syndrome risk). NSAIDs/anticoagulants (platelet dysfunction + GI bleed risk). Cimetidine (doubles sertraline levels — avoid). CYP2D6 inhibitor (moderate) — increases levels of TCAs, antipsychotics, some beta-blockers.
pearls: Most commonly prescribed antidepressant globally. 4–6 week onset for full antidepressant effect — counsel patients about delayed response and not to discontinue prematurely. SADHART trial: sertraline safe in post-MI patients (no adverse cardiac outcomes). STAR*D trial: step-wise approach — switch or augment if inadequate response after 8–12 weeks. Discontinuation syndrome: taper gradually to avoid dizziness, paresthesias ("brain zaps"), irritability. Sertraline among the preferred SSRIs in pregnancy (risk category — some data in first trimester). SSRI choice often driven by drug interactions, patient history, and side effect profile.
trials: SADHART (post-MI depression), STAR*D (depression treatment sequence), IMPACT (late-life depression)
pk: t½ ~26 h. Bioavailability ~44% (oral; food ↑ absorption ~30%). Protein binding ~99%. Metabolized by CYP2C19 (major), CYP2D6, CYP3A4. Active metabolite: desmethylsertraline.
bbw: SUICIDALITY: Increased risk of suicidal thinking and behavior in children, adolescents, and young adults (≤24 years) with MDD and other psychiatric disorders. Monitor closely during initial treatment.
highyield:
• Most commonly prescribed SSRI — first-line for MDD, GAD, PTSD, OCD, panic disorder
• Serotonin syndrome: risk with MAOIs (contraindicated, 14-day washout), linezolid, triptans, tramadol, fentanyl
• Sexual dysfunction (decreased libido, anorgasmia) in 30–40% — counsel patients
• QTc prolongation (mild) — relevant in patients on other QT-prolonging drugs
• Avoid abrupt discontinuation: serotonin discontinuation syndrome (FINISH mnemonic)

name: Quetiapine
moa: Antagonizes multiple receptors: D2 (dopamine), 5-HT2A (serotonin), H1 (histamine — sedation), alpha-1 adrenergic (orthostatic hypotension), muscarinic (less than typical). D2 antagonism → antipsychotic effect; 5-HT2A antagonism → mood stabilization, fewer EPS; H1 antagonism → sedation (significant — often used intentionally for insomnia/anxiety). Active metabolite norquetiapine inhibits NET (antidepressant mechanism).
indications: Schizophrenia; bipolar disorder (manic, depressive, mixed episodes + maintenance); adjunctive MDD (Seroquel XR); acute agitation (IM formulation). Off-label: insomnia (low-dose 25–50 mg — widespread but controversial), dementia-related agitation (black box warning — use with caution).
dosing: Schizophrenia: start 25 mg BID, titrate to 300–800 mg/day. Bipolar mania: 50 mg BID day 1 → target 400–800 mg/day by day 4. Bipolar depression: 50 mg QHS → 300 mg QHS. Adjunctive MDD: 50–300 mg/day. XR (extended-release): once daily dosing.
renal: No dose adjustment needed (minimal renal excretion).
hepatic: Start with lower doses (25 mg/day), titrate slowly — CYP3A4 metabolism reduced; increase exposure.
ades: Sedation (H1 — significant, dose-related), metabolic syndrome (weight gain, glucose dysregulation/diabetes, dyslipidemia — second worst after olanzapine among atypicals), orthostatic hypotension (alpha-1), dry mouth, constipation, QTc prolongation (moderate), tardive dyskinesia (lower risk than typical antipsychotics), cataracts (FDA monitoring recommendation — slit-lamp exam at initiation and 6-month intervals), agranulocytosis (rare). BLACK BOX: increased mortality in elderly with dementia-related psychosis.
monitoring: Metabolic: weight/BMI, fasting glucose/A1c, fasting lipids at baseline then quarterly (first year), then annually. CBC if signs of infection. QTc baseline and PRN. Neurological: AIMS exam annually for tardive dyskinesia. Ophthalmology: slit-lamp lens exam at baseline and q6 months. Mental status.
ddi: CYP3A4 substrate (major): azole antifungals, macrolides, protease inhibitors dramatically increase quetiapine levels (risperidone/quetiapine → CNS depression, QTc, sudden death — avoid strong CYP3A4 inhibitors or reduce dose 1/6 of normal). CYP3A4 inducers (carbamazepine, phenytoin, rifampin) reduce levels 5-fold → increased psychosis. CNS depressants (additive sedation). QTc-prolonging agents. Antihypertensives (additive hypotension).
pearls: Off-label insomnia: widely prescribed at low doses (25–50 mg) but lacks rigorous trial evidence; sedation is purely H1-mediated at low doses (not therapeutic for psychosis); risk of metabolic side effects even at low doses. Metabolic monitoring is mandatory. Seroquel is the brand (generic widely available). CATIE trial: olanzapine was most effective antipsychotic but worst metabolic profile; quetiapine in the middle. For bipolar depression: quetiapine monotherapy (300 mg QHS) is FDA-approved and effective (QUINTi trial — NNT ~7 for response).
trials: CATIE trial (Lieberman NEJM 2005), BOLDER I and II (bipolar depression), QUINTi trial
pk: t½ ~6–7 h. Bioavailability ~100% (oral, food increases Cmax ~25%). Protein binding ~83%. Metabolized by CYP3A4 (primary). Active metabolite: norquetiapine (also antidepressant active).
bbw: ELDERLY DEMENTIA-RELATED PSYCHOSIS: Increased mortality with antipsychotics in elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis. Not FDA-approved for this indication. SUICIDALITY (as adjunct for MDD): See all antidepressant BBW.
highyield:
• Atypical antipsychotic — used for schizophrenia, bipolar I & II, MDD adjunct
• Metabolic syndrome: weight gain, dyslipidemia, hyperglycemia (monitor fasting glucose, lipids, waist circumference)
• Sedation + QTc prolongation are dose-dependent side effects
• CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole): require dose reduction; inducers (phenytoin, rifampin): require dose increase ×5
• Low doses (25–100 mg) often used off-label for insomnia — evidence limited, metabolic risks persist

name: Lithium
moa: Exact mechanism incompletely understood. Proposed: inhibits inositol monophosphatase (IP1) and inositol polyphosphate 1-phosphatase → depletes inositol → reduces PKC signaling cascade; inhibits GSK-3 beta (glycogen synthase kinase) → neuroprotective; modulates G-protein signaling; increases neurotrophin BDNF. Interferes with Na+/K+ pumps and replaces Na+ in cells. Narrow therapeutic index.
indications: Bipolar disorder (gold standard for maintenance — most evidence for suicide reduction); acute mania; bipolar depression (augmentation); unipolar MDD (augmentation, treatment-resistant); schizoaffective disorder. Only drug with strong evidence for reducing suicide in bipolar and unipolar depression.
dosing: Immediate-release: 300 mg TID initially, titrate to therapeutic level. Extended-release (Lithobid): 450–900 mg BID. Target serum level: 0.6–1.2 mEq/L (maintenance); 0.8–1.2 mEq/L (acute mania). Draw trough level 12h after last dose. Very weight-based and renally cleared — adjust carefully.
renal: Entirely renally cleared (competes with Na+ for tubular reabsorption — hyponatremia → lithium retention → toxicity). CrCl 10–50: reduce dose by 25–75%; avoid in ESRD/CrCl <10. NSAIDs, ACEi, ARBs, diuretics → dramatically increase lithium levels. Dehydration major toxicity risk.
hepatic: No hepatic metabolism — no hepatic adjustment needed. All clearance is renal.
ades: At therapeutic levels: fine tremor, polyuria/polydipsia (nephrogenic diabetes insipidus — vasopressin-resistant), hypothyroidism (5–35% — monitor TSH), weight gain, GI upset (take with food), cognitive dulling, acne/psoriasis exacerbation, ECG changes (T-wave flattening). TOXICITY signs (levels >1.5): coarse tremor, ataxia, confusion, lethargy → >2.0: nystagmus, seizures, cardiac arrhythmias, irreversible neurologic damage → >2.5: renal failure, coma, death.
monitoring: Lithium level (draw 12h trough): at steady state (5 days after initiation/dose change) then q3–6 months when stable. Renal function (BUN/Cr, GFR) and electrolytes (especially Na+) q6 months. TSH q6–12 months. Calcium q6 months (lithium → hyperparathyroidism/hypercalcemia with chronic use). Weight. BP. Pregnancy test (teratogen — Ebstein's anomaly risk, especially first trimester).
ddi: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen → reduce renal Li+ clearance by 12–60% → toxicity). ACEi/ARBs (reduce GFR → Li+ accumulates → toxicity — AVOID; if used, reduce Li+ dose and monitor intensely). Thiazide diuretics (Na+ depletion → increased Li+ reabsorption → toxicity). Loop diuretics (safer than thiazides). Metronidazole (reduces Li+ clearance). Serotonin syndrome risk with serotoninergic drugs.
pearls: Only medication proven to reduce suicide risk in both bipolar AND unipolar depression (meta-analysis). Narrow TI — toxicity is life-threatening and can be irreversible (permanent cerebellar damage). Salt intake must be consistent — low-Na+ diet increases Li+ retention. Dehydration (vomiting/diarrhea/fever/exercise) → Li+ toxicity → HOLD lithium and aggressively hydrate. ESRD: lithium is dialyzable — HD used in severe toxicity. Lithium-induced NDI: treat with amiloride (potassium-sparing) or thiazide diuretic (paradoxical antidiuretic in NDI).
trials: Cipriani et al. Lancet 2013 (meta-analysis — lithium suicide reduction), EMBOLDEN I/II (bipolar)
pk: t½ ~18–24 h. Bioavailability ~100% (oral). NOT protein-bound. NOT metabolized. Excretion: renal 95% unchanged (parallels sodium handling).
bbw: TOXICITY: Narrow therapeutic index. Lithium toxicity is closely related to serum concentrations and can occur at doses near therapeutic levels. Monitor serum levels, renal function, thyroid function.
highyield:
• Narrow TI: therapeutic range 0.6–1.2 mEq/L (acute mania); 0.8–1.0 mEq/L maintenance; >1.5 mEq/L = toxicity risk
• Toxicity signs: coarse tremor, confusion, ataxia, seizures, cardiac arrhythmias (fine tremor = therapeutic)
• NSAIDs, ACEi/ARBs, thiazides: all increase lithium levels (reduce renal clearance) → toxicity
• Sodium depletion (low-Na diet, excessive sweating, diarrhea) increases lithium reabsorption → toxicity
• Thyroid and renal function monitoring at baseline, q6 months: hypothyroidism (~20%), nephrogenic DI

name: Ondansetron
moa: Selective competitive antagonist of serotonin 5-HT3 receptors. Chemotherapy/radiation → enterochromaffin cells in GI tract release serotonin → activates 5-HT3 receptors on vagal afferent neurons → emesis. Ondansetron blocks 5-HT3 in GI tract AND area postrema (chemoreceptor trigger zone) → prevents acute CINV. Limited effect on delayed CINV (different mechanism — substance P/NK1).
indications: Chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting (CINV) — acute phase (first 24h); radiation-induced nausea and vomiting; postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV); refractory nausea in various clinical settings. First-line antiemetic for highly/moderately emetogenic chemotherapy (combined with dexamethasone ± NK1 antagonist for highly emetogenic regimens).
dosing: CINV: 8 mg IV/PO q8h or 24 mg PO x1 before chemotherapy. PONV: 4 mg IV at end of surgery. General N/V: 4–8 mg PO/IV q4–6h PRN. ODT (orally disintegrating) useful for nauseous patients. Maximum single dose: 16 mg IV (QTc risk).
renal: No dose adjustment required (hepatically eliminated).
hepatic: Severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C): max 8 mg/day IV (reduced clearance — increased QTc risk with higher doses).
ades: Headache (most common), constipation (serotonin blockage in GI → reduced motility), dizziness, fatigue. QTc prolongation (dose-dependent — FDA safety communication 2011: 32 mg IV single dose removed from market; maximum IV single dose now 16 mg; IV preferred to run over ≥15 min). Serotonin syndrome (if combined with other serotonergic agents — rare but possible). Masking of progressive ileus (constipation concern).
monitoring: QTc at baseline (if high-risk: electrolyte abnormalities, cardiac history, concurrent QTc-prolonging drugs). Electrolytes (hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia worsen QTc risk). Bowel function (constipation). Nausea/vomiting control assessment.
ddi: QTc-prolonging agents (additive risk — cardiac monitoring). Serotonin syndrome risk with linezolid, MAOIs, triptans, tramadol. Apomorphine (severe hypotension — contraindicated). CYP3A4/2D6 enzymes involved; few major clinical interactions.
pearls: 5-HT3 antagonists are the most important antiemetics for acute CINV. For highly emetogenic chemotherapy (cisplatin, doxorubicin-cyclophosphamide): triplet therapy = 5-HT3 antagonist + dexamethasone + NK1 antagonist (aprepitant/fosaprepitant). Delayed CINV (>24h after chemo): NK1 antagonists more important (substance P/NK1 receptor-mediated). QTc 2011 FDA warning: avoid IV ondansetron in patients with congenital long QT syndrome; ensure electrolytes (K+, Mg2+) are normal. ODT dissolves on tongue — useful when patient cannot swallow. Palonosetron (Aloxi): second-gen 5-HT3 antagonist with stronger/longer binding and better delayed CINV coverage.
trials: MASCC antiemesis guideline 2023, ASCO antiemesis guidelines, Hesketh classification (emetogenicity)
pk: t½ ~3–5 h. Bioavailability ~60% (oral). Protein binding 70–76%. Metabolized by CYP3A4, CYP1A2, CYP2D6. Excretion: renal ~44% (metabolites), fecal ~25%.
highyield:
• 5-HT3 receptor antagonist — first-line antiemetic for chemotherapy-induced N/V (CINV)
• QTc prolongation: dose-dependent; IV 32 mg single dose removed from market; max IV single dose now 16 mg
• Serotonin syndrome: rare but possible with other serotonergic agents (tramadol, SSRIs)
• Constipation most common ADE (5-HT3 blockade in GI tract)
• CYP3A4 metabolized — aprepitant/fosaprepitant combination enhances antiemetic coverage for highly emetogenic chemo

name: Filgrastim
class: G-CSF (granulocyte colony-stimulating factor) / myeloid growth factor
moa: Recombinant human G-CSF. Binds G-CSF receptor on neutrophil precursors → stimulates proliferation, differentiation, and survival of neutrophil progenitor cells in bone marrow → increases neutrophil production and release → raises ANC. Also activates mature neutrophils (enhances phagocytosis, antibody-dependent cytotoxicity). Shortens duration of severe neutropenia after chemotherapy.
indications: Febrile neutropenia prevention (primary/secondary prophylaxis in high-risk chemotherapy regimens); treatment of established severe neutropenia; mobilization of stem cells for HSCT (with apheresis); congenital/cyclic/idiopathic neutropenia; AML induction (post-chemo neutrophil recovery).
dosing: Prophylaxis: 5 mcg/kg SC daily starting 24–72h after chemotherapy completion; continue until ANC ≥10,000/mcL post-nadir OR ≥2,000–3,000/mcL during 2 consecutive days post-nadir. Stem cell mobilization: 10 mcg/kg SC daily x 4 days, then apheresis on day 5. Do NOT administer within 14 days before or 24h after cytotoxic chemotherapy.
renal: No dose adjustment required.
hepatic: No dose adjustment required.
ades: Bone pain (medullary pain from marrow expansion — 10–30%; treat with acetaminophen or antihistamines; NSAIDs also effective), fever, splenomegaly (rarely splenic rupture — RARE but serious: educate patients on left upper quadrant/shoulder pain → immediate evaluation), injection site reactions, leukocytosis (if ANC >10,000 → consider dose reduction/hold), thrombocytopenia, ARDS (pulmonary infiltrates with G-CSF in sepsis — caution in critically ill).
monitoring: ANC and platelets 2x/week during therapy; spleen size assessment (history of splenomegaly — periodic US); CBC prior to each dose in stem cell mobilization; vital signs.
ddi: Chemotherapy (avoid within 24h of cytotoxic chemotherapy — stimulating proliferating marrow cells during chemo increases myelosuppression). Bleomycin (pulmonary toxicity enhanced). Lithium (additive neutrophil-releasing effect — may amplify G-CSF effect).
pearls: ASCO guidelines: primary G-CSF prophylaxis recommended when febrile neutropenia risk >20% with planned regimen; consider at 10–20% with individual patient risk factors. Do NOT start during active infection or with cytotoxic chemo. Pegfilgrastim (Neulasta, Fulphila): long-acting G-CSF (pegylated) — single dose per chemotherapy cycle (24h after chemo); equivalent efficacy to daily filgrastim; preferred for convenience. Biosimilars (Zarxio, others) are FDA-approved and therapeutically equivalent. Bone pain management: loratadine 10 mg/day during G-CSF reduces bone pain (ASCO evidence-based recommendation).
trials: ASCO/NCCN G-CSF guidelines, multiple RCTs establishing febrile neutropenia risk reduction
pk: t½ ~3.5 h (SC). Bioavailability ~60–70% (SC). Not protein-bound significantly. Eliminated by receptor-mediated clearance (neutrophil-dependent) and renal clearance.
highyield:
• G-CSF — stimulates neutrophil production; used for febrile neutropenia prophylaxis and treatment
• ASCO/NCCN guidelines: primary prophylaxis if chemotherapy regimen has ≥20% febrile neutropenia risk
• Do NOT give 24 h before or 24 h after myelosuppressive chemotherapy (bone marrow sensitization)
• Bone pain most common side effect (marrow expansion) — ibuprofen or loratadine may help
• Zarxio (filgrastim-sndz): FDA-approved biosimilar; not automatically interchangeable at all pharmacies without prescriber approval

name: Tacrolimus
moa: Binds FK506-binding protein 12 (FKBP12) → tacrolimus-FKBP12 complex inhibits calcineurin phosphatase → prevents dephosphorylation of NFAT (nuclear factor of activated T cells) → blocks IL-2 gene transcription → impairs T-cell activation and proliferation. 100x more potent than cyclosporine per unit mass.
indications: Solid organ transplant rejection prophylaxis (kidney, liver, heart, lung, pancreas — first-line CNI); treatment of acute cellular rejection; graft-vs-host disease (GVHD) prophylaxis in HSCT; topical use (Protopic) for atopic dermatitis.
dosing: Highly variable — individualized by TDM. Kidney transplant: 0.1–0.2 mg/kg/day PO in 2 divided doses (IR) initially; target trough: 10–15 ng/mL (first 3 months), 5–10 ng/mL (maintenance). Liver transplant: similar. Highly variable absorption — TDM is MANDATORY. Astagraf XL (once daily, extended-release): bioequivalent to IR with better adherence.
renal: Nephrotoxic — careful TDM essential. In impaired renal function from tacrolimus toxicity, dose reduction and level monitoring required. CKD itself doesn't change dose requirements; monitor trough levels.
hepatic: Severe hepatic impairment: reduced metabolism → higher levels. Start at lower end, increase TDM frequency. CYP3A4/5 metabolized.
ades: Nephrotoxicity (dose-dependent, chronic — major long-term concern; both acute functional and chronic fibrotic nephropathy), neurotoxicity (tremor, headache, paresthesias, seizures, PRES — posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome), hypertension, hyperkalemia, hypomagnesemia, new-onset diabetes after transplant (NODAT — worse than cyclosporine), infections (BK virus nephropathy, CMV, fungal — immunosuppression), hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, GI disturbance, alopecia. No gingival hyperplasia/hirsutism (advantage over cyclosporine).
monitoring: MANDATORY trough levels (draw 12h post-dose IR, 24h post-dose XL) at each clinic visit initially, then q1–3 months when stable. SCr/GFR, electrolytes (K+, Mg2+), glucose/A1c (NODAT screening), CBC, BP, magnesium (hypoMg common, requires supplementation). BK virus PCR in kidney transplant. CMV PCR in high-risk patients.
ddi: Major CYP3A4/P-gp substrate — EXTENSIVE interactions. Strong inhibitors (azoles, diltiazem, verapamil, macrolides, ritonavir) → 2–10x increase in tacrolimus levels → toxicity. Strong inducers (rifampin, carbamazepine, phenytoin, St. John's Wort) → dramatic reduction in levels → rejection risk. Grapefruit juice (CYP3A4 inhibition) — AVOID. NSAIDs (additive nephrotoxicity). Potassium-sparing diuretics (additive hyperkalemia). Sirolimus (additive nephrotoxicity — avoid concurrent use in most protocols).
pearls: Narrow therapeutic index — TDM mandatory for all patients. Generic tacrolimus substitution is controversial — FDA considers bioequivalent but transplant societies recommend AGAINST switching formulations without dose adjustment and increased monitoring (risk of rejection or toxicity). NODAT (new-onset diabetes after transplant) is significantly more common with tacrolimus than cyclosporine. PRES is a serious CNS complication — headache, seizures, visual disturbance, altered mental status + hypertension → brain MRI → tacrolimus reduction/discontinuation. BK nephropathy: surveillance PCR + reduce immunosuppression is treatment.
trials: US Renal Transplant Study (tacrolimus vs. cyclosporine), SYMPHONY trial, ELITE-Symphony
pk: t½ ~12 h (variable). Bioavailability ~20–25% (oral, highly variable); food reduces absorption. Protein binding >99% (RBC, albumin). Metabolized by CYP3A4/3A5 (major). Excretion: fecal ~92%.
bbw: SERIOUS INFECTIONS and MALIGNANCIES (lymphoma, skin cancer): Immunosuppression increases susceptibility. NEW FORMULATIONS: Only for transplant. Astagraf XL/Envarsus XR NOT interchangeable with Prograf or each other (different PK profiles).
highyield:
• Calcineurin inhibitor — inhibits IL-2 production → T-cell suppression
• Narrow TI: TDM critical; troughs vary by indication (kidney 5–15 ng/mL early; liver 5–20 ng/mL)
• CYP3A4 and P-gp: extensive DDIs — azole antifungals, macrolides, diltiazem ↑ levels; rifampin, phenytoin ↓ levels
• Nephrotoxicity (acute and chronic), neurotoxicity (tremor, PRES), hyperglycemia (NODAT), hyperkalemia, hypertension
• CYP3A5 polymorphism: extensive metabolizers require higher doses; genotyping can guide initial dosing

name: Mycophenolate Mofetil / Mycophenolic Acid
moa: Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) is a prodrug hydrolyzed to mycophenolic acid (MPA). MPA selectively inhibits inosine monophosphate dehydrogenase (IMPDH) — rate-limiting enzyme in de novo purine (guanosine) synthesis. Lymphocytes rely predominantly on de novo pathway (unlike other cells that use salvage pathway) → selective antiproliferative effect on T and B lymphocytes → reduced antibody production and cell-mediated immunity.
indications: Solid organ transplant rejection prophylaxis (kidney, liver, heart — first-line antiproliferative agent replacing azathioprine); lupus nephritis; IgA nephropathy; ANCA-associated vasculitis; myasthenia gravis. Combined with tacrolimus and corticosteroids as standard triple therapy in renal transplant.
dosing: Kidney transplant: 1000–1500 mg PO BID (MMF) or 720 mg BID (enteric-coated MPA — Myfortic). Adjust for GI intolerance (reduce dose). Doses given on empty stomach for MPA (or with food if GI intolerant for MMF). NOT interchangeable mg-for-mg (Myfortic 720 mg = CellCept 1000 mg).
renal: Dose adjustment in severe renal impairment post-transplant (max 1g BID in kidney transplant patients with early post-op period until graft function established). MPA acyl glucuronide metabolite accumulates in CKD — may explain GI side effects.
hepatic: No dose adjustment for hepatic impairment alone.
ades: GI dominant: diarrhea (most common — dose-dependent, use Myfortic EC for better GI tolerability), nausea/vomiting/cramping, GI ulceration/bleeding. Myelosuppression: leukopenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia (CBC monitoring). Infections (increased risk — CMV, BK virus, PCP, fungal). Teratogenicity (Category D — absolute contraindication in pregnancy; effective contraception MANDATORY; REMS program for women of childbearing potential). Lymphoma/malignancy risk with long-term immunosuppression.
monitoring: CBC with differential q2 weeks x 3 months, then monthly for 9 months, then q3 months (myelosuppression monitoring). CMV PCR in at-risk patients. Pregnancy tests + contraception counseling (REMS). Diarrhea severity (consider dose reduction or switch to enteric-coated Myfortic). MPA levels (optional — target AUC 30–60 mg·h/L; not universally performed but used in rejection/toxicity situations).
ddi: Antacids/PPIs (reduce MMF absorption — separate by 2h from antacids). Cholestyramine/colestipol (interrupt enterohepatic recirculation → reduce MPA levels by 40%). Azathioprine (do NOT combine — additive myelosuppression). Rifampin (reduces MPA levels via enterohepatic recirculation disruption). Sevelamer (reduces MPA levels — separate administration). Oral contraceptives (slightly reduced efficacy — use additional contraceptive method).
pearls: Myfortic (enteric-coated MPA) causes less GI side effects vs. CellCept — switch if diarrhea is problematic (NOT bioequivalent mg-for-mg; Myfortic 720 = CellCept 1000). REMS program (Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy) for MMF due to teratogenicity — mandatory counseling. Stop mycophenolate 3 months before planned conception. MMF replaced azathioprine as antiproliferative in most transplant regimens due to superior rejection prevention. MPA levels: TDM available but limited evidence that AUC-guided dosing improves outcomes vs. fixed dosing.
trials: Sollinger NEJM 1995 (MMF vs. azathioprine), Symphony trial, MYSS trial
pk: MMF (CellCept) → MPA (active) by esterases; MPA t½ ~18 h. Bioavailability MMF ~94%; MPA (Myfortic) ~72%. Protein binding >97%. Enterohepatic recycling (UGT1A9). Excretion: renal ~93% (MPAG).
bbw: EMBRYO-FETAL TOXICITY: Do not use in pregnancy; causes miscarriage and birth defects. REMS program required. Malignancy and serious infections.
highyield:
• Inhibits IMPDH → blocks purine synthesis → antiproliferative for T and B lymphocytes
• MMF and MPA (sodium) are NOT interchangeable (different salt forms, different bioavailability) — DO NOT substitute without dose adjustment
• GI toxicity (diarrhea, N/V) dose-limiting — enteric-coated MPA (Myfortic) may reduce GI effects
• REMS program required (iPLEDGE-like — 2-form contraception required in females of reproductive potential)
• Azathioprine alternative: less effective but fewer GI effects; XO inhibitor allopurinol dramatically ↑ azathioprine toxicity (AVOID combination)

name: Morphine
moa: Binds mu-opioid receptors (MOR) predominantly → G-protein (Gi/o) coupling → decreased cAMP, increased K+ conductance (hyperpolarization), decreased Ca2+ entry → reduced neuronal excitability → analgesic, euphoric, sedating effects. Also delta and kappa receptor activity. Central and peripheral (dorsal horn) action. Morphine-6-glucuronide (M6G) is active, potent metabolite — accumulates in renal failure.
indications: Moderate-to-severe pain (acute, chronic, cancer, procedural); dyspnea in advanced illness (palliative — "air hunger"); acute MI pain (IV morphine — but CULPRIT-SHOCK and ATLANTIC data suggest possible harm with ACS/P2Y12 drugs); pulmonary edema (reduces preload/anxiety).
dosing: Opioid-naive adults: 2.5–5 mg IV q4h PRN; 15–30 mg PO q4h (IR). Chronic pain: titrate ER formulation (MS Contin) with IR breakthrough dosing (10–15% of total daily dose for breakthrough). PCA: 1–2 mg demand dose with 6–10 min lockout. Elderly/renal: reduce dose, increase interval. Equianalgesic table: morphine 10 mg IV = 30 mg PO (3:1 oral:IV ratio).
renal: Active metabolite M6G accumulates in CKD/ESRD → prolonged sedation, respiratory depression. Use with extreme caution in GFR <30; prefer alternative opioids (hydromorphone, fentanyl) in ESRD.
hepatic: Hepatic metabolism (glucuronidation — relatively preserved vs. CYP-dependent drugs). Reduce dose in severe cirrhosis (reduced first-pass metabolism → increased bioavailability PO, decreased clearance).
ades: Respiratory depression (most feared — dose-dependent; reversal with naloxone 0.4 mg IV q2–3 min up to 10 mg), constipation (most common long-term — tolerance does NOT develop; always prescribe bowel regimen), N/V (common early, tolerance develops), sedation, pruritus (histamine release — treat with hydroxyzine not diphenhydramine), urinary retention (alpha tone in sphincter), physical dependence/withdrawal, tolerance, addiction risk, hyperalgesia (paradoxical — chronic opioids can worsen pain sensitivity), immunosuppression (chronic).
monitoring: Pain score (NRS 0–10), respiratory rate/depth, sedation scale (RASS, Pasero), constipation, PDMP (prescription drug monitoring program) check before prescribing, ORT (Opioid Risk Tool) for abuse potential, tolerance signs, COWS (clinical opioid withdrawal scale if tapering).
ddi: CNS depressants (benzodiazepines — additive respiratory depression: BLACK BOX warning FDA 2016; this combination accounts for majority of overdose deaths), alcohol, muscle relaxants. MAOIs (contraindicated — severe, unpredictable reactions). Serotonergic agents (serotonin syndrome). CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers affect related opioids.
pearls: Morphine is the prototype opioid — all others are compared to it (equianalgesic table). AVOID in ESRD (M6G accumulation). Respiratory depression reversal: naloxone 0.04–0.4 mg IV (titrate low in opioid-tolerant patients to avoid precipitating withdrawal). Constipation: prescribe osmotic laxative (PEG/lactulose) or peripheral mu-opioid receptor antagonist (methylnaltrexone, naloxegol) routinely — tolerance never develops to constipation. CDC 2022 guideline: prescribe lowest effective dose, limit to ≤3-day supply for acute pain (wherever possible), use non-opioid analgesia as first-line. Risk mitigation: naloxone co-prescribing for all patients on long-term opioids or high doses.
trials: CDC Opioid Prescribing Guidelines 2022, SPACE trial (opioids vs. non-opioids for chronic musculoskeletal pain)
pk: t½ ~2–4 h. Bioavailability PO ~20–40% (first-pass — significant). Protein binding ~35%. Metabolized by UGT2B7 → M6G (active, potent), M3G (neuroexcitatory). Excretion: renal (90% M3G + M6G).
bbw: ADDICTION, ABUSE, and MISUSE: High abuse potential. LIFE-THREATENING RESPIRATORY DEPRESSION. NEONATAL OPIOID WITHDRAWAL SYNDROME. CYTOCHROME P450 INTERACTIONS. RISKS FROM CONCOMITANT USE WITH BENZODIAZEPINES (hypnotics, alcohol).
highyield:
• Active metabolite M6G accumulates in renal failure → prolonged respiratory depression
• Renal impairment: avoid extended use or use alternative opioids (hydromorphone or fentanyl preferred)
• Equianalgesic dosing: morphine 30 mg PO = 10 mg IV/SC; use when rotating opioids
• Naloxone reverses respiratory depression: 0.4–2 mg IV q2–3 min; repeat q20–60 min (shorter t½ than morphine)
• 3-in-1 rule: morphine requires 3 doses escalating 25–50% to achieve analgesic effect

name: Naloxone
moa: Competitive antagonist at mu, delta, and kappa opioid receptors with high affinity (higher than most agonists) → rapidly displaces opioids → reverses opioid-induced respiratory depression, sedation, analgesia, and miosis within 1–2 min IV. Short duration (30–90 min IV) — shorter than most opioids → RE-SEDATION IS A MAJOR RISK.
indications: Opioid overdose (life-threatening respiratory depression) — IV/IM/intranasal/SC. Community distribution (Narcan nasal spray) for layperson use. Intraoperative opioid reversal. Pruritus reversal (low doses). Opioid-induced constipation (peripherally-acting oral formulations — methylnaltrexone).
dosing: Opioid overdose: 0.4–2 mg IV/IM/SC q2–3 min up to 10 mg (if no response after 10 mg, reconsider opioid diagnosis). Intranasal (Narcan): 4 mg per nostril (spray); repeat q2–3 min in alternating nostrils. POST-REVERSAL: duration shorter than most opioids → may need repeat doses, continuous infusion (2/3 of effective bolus dose per hour), or prolonged monitoring (4–6h for short-acting; 12–24h for long-acting opioids/methadone). Opioid-tolerant patients: use lowest effective dose (0.04 mg titrated) to avoid precipitating acute withdrawal.
renal: No dose adjustment needed.
hepatic: No dose adjustment needed; primarily hepatically metabolized (glucuronidation).
ades: In opioid-naive: no significant effects. In opioid-tolerant/dependent: precipitates ACUTE OPIOID WITHDRAWAL — agitation, tachycardia, hypertension, diaphoresis, N/V/D, pain crisis. Pulmonary edema (rare — likely mechanism sympathetic surge after reversal). Ventricular arrhythmias (tachycardia, VF — rare with rapid large doses).
monitoring: Respiratory rate (primary endpoint — target >8–10 breaths/min, adequate depth), oxygen saturation (SpO2), level of consciousness, heart rate and BP. Watch for RE-NARCOTIZATION (return of respiratory depression as naloxone wears off): typically 30–90 min. Duration of monitoring depends on opioid duration/formulation.
ddi: No significant drug interactions with non-opioid drugs. Buprenorphine: high receptor affinity — requires larger naloxone doses (often 2–10 mg) to displace. Methadone: very long duration → extended naloxone monitoring/infusion needed.
pearls: Single most important concept: naloxone duration is SHORTER than most opioids — re-narcotization is common. For methadone or extended-release opioid overdose: MUST monitor for ≥12–24h and likely need repeat doses or infusion. Community naloxone access (Narcan nasal spray, Kloxxado): OTC availability since 2023; should be co-prescribed with ALL patients on chronic opioid therapy per FDA guidance and CDC 2022 guidelines. Intranasal naloxone for laypersons: 4 mg per nostril → repeat if no response → call 911. Buprenorphine/naloxone (Suboxone) is a treatment for opioid use disorder — the naloxone component prevents IV misuse (precipitates withdrawal if injected IV, inactive when taken sublingually).
trials: CONSORT study (naloxone community distribution), FDA OTC approval 2023, CDC Opioid Guidelines 2022
pk: t½ ~1–2 h (IV). Bioavailability intranasal ~44–46%; IM ~65–75%. NOT orally bioavailable (extensive first-pass). Hepatic metabolism. Excretion: renal.
highyield:
• Competitive opioid receptor antagonist — reverses respiratory depression, sedation, miosis
• Duration of action (1–2 h) often SHORTER than opioid being reversed — re-sedation can occur; repeat dosing or infusion may be needed
• Naloxone IV onset ~2 min; intranasal (Narcan) onset ~8–13 min; IM onset ~5 min
• Precipitates acute withdrawal in opioid-dependent patients (agitation, HTN, tachycardia, seizures)
• Community naloxone: dispensed OTC (Narcan spray); pharmacists can dispense without Rx in most US states

name: Sertraline
moa: Inhibits presynaptic serotonin (5-HT) reuptake, increasing synaptic 5-HT concentration.
indications: Major depressive disorder, PTSD, OCD, panic disorder, social anxiety disorder, premenstrual dysphoric disorder.
pk: Well absorbed orally; significant first-pass. t½ = 26 hours (shortest washout period of common SSRIs). Metabolized by CYP2C19/2D6 to active desmethylsertraline. Steady state in 7 days.
dosing: MDD: 50mg/day, titrate to 50-200mg/day. PTSD/OCD: up to 200mg/day. Start 25mg if sensitive.
renal: No significant adjustment needed.
hepatic: Use with caution; reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment.
ades: GI (nausea, diarrhea, most common early), sexual dysfunction, insomnia, serotonin syndrome (with other serotonergic agents), hyponatremia (elderly), weight gain (long-term).
monitoring: Suicide risk (especially first 4 weeks and with dose changes in <25yr). Response at 4-8 weeks.
ddi: MAOIs (serotonin syndrome—14d washout), linezolid, tramadol, pimozide (QT), warfarin (mild CYP effect).
bbw: Increased risk of suicidal thinking and behavior in children, adolescents, and young adults (aged <25) with MDD and other psychiatric disorders.
pearls: Preferred SSRI in cardiovascular disease (safest cardiac profile). Minimal CYP2D6 inhibition vs. fluoxetine. Safest SSRI in pregnancy (most data). PTSD first-line.
highyield:
• First-line for PTSD (both sertraline and paroxetine FDA approved)
• Shortest t½ of common SSRIs (26h) → fastest washout
• Safest SSRI in cardiac disease and pregnancy
• MAOi washout: 14 days after sertraline (vs. 5 weeks for fluoxetine)

name: Lithium
moa: Multiple mechanisms: inhibits inositol monophosphatase (depletes phosphoinositol), modulates second messenger systems (PKC), may increase serotonin synthesis.
indications: Bipolar I disorder (acute mania and maintenance), augmentation for refractory depression, schizoaffective disorder.
pk: Complete oral absorption. NOT protein bound. NOT metabolized (excreted unchanged). t½ = 18-36h. Renal elimination (competing with Na+ reabsorption—sodium depletion increases lithium retention).
dosing: Acute mania: target level 0.8-1.2 mEq/L. Maintenance: 0.6-0.8 mEq/L. Check level 12h post-dose. Adjust for renal function.
renal: Reduce dose proportionally to CrCl reduction. Contraindicated in severe renal impairment. Dialyzable.
hepatic: No hepatic metabolism — hepatic impairment has no effect.
ades: Fine tremor (most common), polyuria/polydipsia (nephrogenic DI), hypothyroidism, weight gain, cognitive blunting, leukocytosis, acne, psoriasis exacerbation.
monitoring: Serum lithium level (12h post-dose) at initiation and every 3-6 months. TSH every 6 months. SCr/BMP every 6-12 months. ECG in cardiac patients.
ddi: NSAIDs and COX-2 inhibitors ↑ lithium (reduce GFR). Thiazide diuretics ↑ lithium (Na+ depletion). ACEi/ARBs ↑ lithium. Loop diuretics: less interaction than thiazides.
pearls: Only mood stabilizer with proven antisuicidal effect. Nephrotoxic long-term (chronic interstitial nephropathy). Cardiac: T-wave flattening common; rare QTc prolongation. NOT metabolized by liver—true renal drug.
highyield:
• Narrow TI: 0.6-1.2 mEq/L. Toxicity >1.5 mEq/L
• Na+ depletion (NSAIDs, thiazides, ACEi, sweating, low Na+ diet) → lithium toxicity
• Hypothyroidism in 20-40% long-term users → check TSH q6 months
• Only antisuicidal mood stabilizer (NEJM 2003)

name: Mesalamine (5-ASA)
moa: Topical anti-inflammatory in colonic mucosa. Inhibits prostaglandin synthesis, leukotriene production, and NF-κB activation locally.
indications: Mild-moderate ulcerative colitis (induction and maintenance). Crohn disease colitis (limited evidence).
pk: Oral formulations vary by release mechanism (pH-dependent, time-release). Local action in colon (systemic absorption minimized). Rectal forms (suppositories, enemas) for distal UC.
dosing: Induction: 2.4-4.8g/day oral. Maintenance: 1.6-2.4g/day. Rectal: mesalamine enema 4g/60mL nightly (left-sided) or suppository 1g/day (proctitis).
renal: Reduce dose or avoid in significant renal impairment (nephrotoxicity risk at high doses).
hepatic: No dose adjustment needed.
ades: GI (nausea, diarrhea), headache, rash. Rare: interstitial nephritis (dose-dependent, check SCr/UA), myocarditis, pancreatitis. Paradoxical flare possible.
monitoring: SCr/BMP at baseline and annually. UA for nephrotoxicity monitoring.
ddi: Azathioprine: may increase myelotoxicity. Warfarin: minor interaction.
pearls: First-line for mild-moderate UC. Rectal formulations superior for distal disease/proctitis. Check SCr/UA annually for nephrotoxicity. Not effective for luminal Crohn disease. Sulfasalazine = sulfapyridine (absorbed, causes ADRs) + 5-ASA (active).
highyield:
• First-line mild-moderate UC (induction + maintenance)
• Monitor renal function annually (interstitial nephritis)
• Rectal route preferred for distal UC/proctitis
• Sulfasalazine: sulfa-related ADRs (hemolysis in G6PD, folic acid antagonism)

name: Azathioprine / 6-Mercaptopurine
moa: Prodrug converted to 6-thioguanine nucleotides (6-TGN) by TPMT/HPRT. 6-TGN inhibits purine synthesis → lymphocyte apoptosis. Onset 3-6 months.
indications: IBD (Crohn, UC) maintenance; prevention of rejection in organ transplant; autoimmune hepatitis; rheumatoid arthritis.
pk: AZA → 6-MP (intestinal wall and liver). 6-MP → 6-TGN (active) via HPRT or → 6-MMPR (hepatotoxic, inactive) via TPMT. TPMT polymorphism determines toxicity risk.
dosing: AZA: 2-3 mg/kg/day for IBD. 6-MP: 1-1.5 mg/kg/day. Start low, titrate over months.
renal: Reduce dose with renal impairment.
hepatic: Elevated 6-MMPR metabolites in liver injury → hepatotoxicity risk.
ades: Myelosuppression (dose-dependent), nausea, hepatotoxicity, pancreatitis (early, usually within 30 days), increased infection risk, lymphoma risk (long-term, especially with TPMT low activity).
monitoring: CBC (weekly initially → monthly), LFTs. TPMT genotype or phenotype before initiation. 6-TGN/6-MMPR levels guide dosing optimization.
ddi: Allopurinol: inhibits xanthine oxidase → dramatic increase in 6-MP toxicity (reduce AZA/6-MP dose by 75%). Aminosalicylates: inhibit TPMT → increase 6-TGN (myelotoxicity).
bbw: Increased risk of malignancy, particularly lymphoma. Mutagenic in animals. Serious infections.
pearls: TPMT testing before initiation. Normal TPMT: standard dose. Intermediate: reduce 30-50%. Poor metabolizer (absent TPMT): avoid or use <10% normal dose (severe myelosuppression). Allopurinol interaction is life-threatening.
highyield:
• TPMT phenotype MUST be checked before starting (poor metabolizer → fatal myelosuppression)
• Allopurinol + AZA/6-MP = severe toxicity (75% dose reduction required)
• Onset of IBD effect: 3-6 months (not for acute flares)
• BBW: malignancy (lymphoma), serious infections

name: Ondansetron
moa: Selective 5-HT3 antagonist at peripheral vagal nerve terminals and in chemoreceptor trigger zone (CTZ) of CNS. Blocks afferent signals causing nausea/vomiting.
indications: Chemotherapy-induced nausea/vomiting (CINV) prevention; post-operative nausea/vomiting (PONV); radiation-induced N/V; acute nausea (off-label).
pk: Well absorbed orally. t½ = 3-6h. Hepatic metabolism (CYP3A4/1A2/2D6). Significant first-pass; oral bioavailability ~60%. IV and oral doses differ.
dosing: CINV: 8mg IV over 15 min before chemo; 8mg PO q8h. PONV: 4mg IV at end of surgery. Opioid-induced N/V: 4mg IV PRN.
renal: No dose adjustment needed.
hepatic: Severe hepatic impairment: max 8mg/day (reduced clearance).
ades: Headache, constipation, dizziness, QTc prolongation (dose-dependent), serotonin syndrome (rare, high doses + other serotonergic agents).
monitoring: ECG for QTc baseline in patients with cardiac disease or on other QT-prolonging agents. Avoid IV doses >32mg.
ddi: QT-prolonging drugs (additive). MAOIs, tramadol, fentanyl (serotonin syndrome risk at high doses).
pearls: IV dose: single dose >32mg removed from market (QTc). Oral bioavailability 60% (not same as IV). Does NOT work well for anticipatory N/V (conditioned response) — benzodiazepine needed. Constipation is more common than with first-generation antiemetics.
highyield:
• QTc prolongation: dose-dependent; avoid >32mg IV single dose
• Does not prevent anticipatory N/V (benzo preferred for that)
• Not effective for vomiting of vestibular origin (motion sickness — use scopolamine/antihistamines)
• 5-HT3 antagonists are first-line for CINV

name: Methylprednisolone
moa: Binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptor → translocates to nucleus → modulates gene transcription. Suppresses NF-κB, reduces inflammatory cytokines, inhibits phospholipase A2.
indications: Acute asthma/COPD exacerbation, allergic reactions, anaphylaxis, MS relapse, transplant rejection, septic shock (low-dose, controversial), spinal cord injury (no longer recommended), IBD flare.
pk: IV/oral/IM. Oral bioavailability ~88%. t½ plasma 3.5h; biologic t½ 18-36h. Extensive hepatic metabolism (CYP3A4). Lower protein binding than prednisone.
dosing: Acute asthma: 40-80mg IV/oral daily. MS relapse: 1g IV daily x3-5 days. Transplant rejection: 500-1000mg IV. Dose equivalent: methylprednisolone 4mg = prednisone 5mg = hydrocortisone 20mg.
renal: No dose adjustment required.
hepatic: Reduced metabolism in severe liver disease; monitor for increased effects.
ades: Hyperglycemia, hypertension, fluid retention, insomnia, mood changes, HPA suppression (>3 weeks use), osteoporosis, avascular necrosis, cataracts, immunosuppression, Cushing syndrome (chronic).
monitoring: Blood glucose (especially diabetics), blood pressure, electrolytes. Bone density if long-term.
ddi: CYP3A4 inhibitors (azole antifungals, macrolides) increase levels. CYP3A4 inducers (rifampin) decrease levels. Warfarin: variable effect. NSAIDs: additive GI risk.
pearls: No HPA suppression with <3 weeks use at most doses. For septic shock: hydrocortisone 200mg/day IV (not methylprednisolone per ADRENAL/APROCCHSS trials). Pulse steroids (1g) for transplant rejection, MS relapse. Not evidence-based for ARDS (DEXA-ARDS shows dexamethasone benefit in severe ARDS).
highyield:
• Dose equivalences: 4mg methyl = 5mg pred = 20mg hydrocort = 0.75mg dexameth
• Septic shock: hydrocortisone 200mg/day preferred (not methylprednisolone)
• HPA suppression: avoid abrupt discontinuation after >3 weeks use
• No longer recommended for acute spinal cord injury (no benefit in NASCIS trials)

name: Montelukast
moa: Selectively antagonizes cysteinyl leukotriene receptor CysLT1, blocking LTC4, LTD4, LTE4. Reduces bronchoconstriction, eosinophilic inflammation, and mucus secretion.
indications: Asthma prophylaxis (mild persistent or add-on), exercise-induced bronchospasm, allergic rhinitis, aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD).
pk: Rapid oral absorption; peak 3-4h. t½ = 2.7-5.5h. Extensive hepatic metabolism (CYP2C8/3A4). >99% protein bound. No renal adjustment.
dosing: Adults: 10mg once daily (evening preferred). 6-14y: 5mg. 2-5y: 4mg chewable.
renal: No dose adjustment needed.
hepatic: Use with caution in severe hepatic impairment.
ades: Generally well tolerated. Neuropsychiatric events (depression, suicidal ideation, behavioral changes — FDA BBW 2020), headache, upper respiratory infections, abdominal pain.
monitoring: Monitor for neuropsychiatric symptoms; discontinue if behavioral changes occur.
ddi: CYP inducers (rifampin, phenobarbital) may decrease montelukast levels. Generally minimal DDI profile.
bbw: Serious neuropsychiatric events including agitation, aggression, anxiety, depression, hallucinations, suicidal thinking and behavior. Weigh benefits vs risks; avoid in patients with psychiatric disorders.
pearls: FDA 2020 BBW added for neuropsychiatric events. Now: restrict use to patients where other therapies inadequate for allergic rhinitis (asthma: still acceptable add-on). Most effective for AERD and aspirin-exacerbated disease. Not for acute bronchospasm.
highyield:
• FDA 2020 BBW: neuropsychiatric events (depression, suicidality)
• Drug of choice for aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD)
• Not for acute bronchospasm relief (no beta-agonist activity)
• Added-on value in eosinophilic asthma and exercise-induced bronchospasm

name: Acetazolamide
moa: Inhibits carbonic anhydrase in renal proximal tubule → decreased HCO3 reabsorption → bicarbonate diuresis → metabolic acidosis → compensatory increase in respiratory drive.
indications: Altitude sickness prevention/treatment (stimulates ventilation by inducing metabolic acidosis), open-angle glaucoma (reduces aqueous humor production), metabolic alkalosis (hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis), idiopathic intracranial hypertension, partial seizures.
pk: Well absorbed orally; t½ = 4-8h (extended release 10-15h). Renal elimination unchanged.
dosing: Altitude sickness: 125-250mg BID-TID starting 1-2 days before ascent. Glaucoma: 250mg QID or 500mg SR BID. Metabolic alkalosis: 250-500mg q6-12h IV.
renal: Avoid if CrCl <10 mL/min (ineffective + hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis risk).
hepatic: Hepatic encephalopathy risk due to ammonium accumulation; use caution.
ades: Metabolic acidosis, hypokalemia, paresthesias (tingling of extremities — sulfona-mide effect), nephrolithiasis (urine alkalinization), sulfonamide hypersensitivity (cross-reactivity).
monitoring: Serum electrolytes (K+, HCO3), renal function.
ddi: Phenytoin: variable (some alkalinization of urine reduces renal elimination). Lithium: decreased tubular reabsorption (lower lithium levels). Aspirin: can cause aspirin toxicity (reduces excretion).
pearls: Contraindicated with hepatic failure (ammonia accumulation). Paresthesias are common and benign. Cross-reactivity with sulfonamide allergy (caution). Best evidence for altitude sickness prophylaxis (starts 24-48h before ascent).
highyield:
• Works for altitude sickness by causing mild metabolic acidosis → increased ventilation
• Paresthesias (tingling) are common, benign side effect from CA inhibition
• Avoid in hepatic failure (ammonia accumulation → encephalopathy)
• Sulfonamide cross-reactivity: use with caution in sulfa allergy

name: Erlotinib
moa: Reversibly inhibits EGFR (HER1) tyrosine kinase, blocking downstream MAPK/PI3K/Akt signaling, reducing cell proliferation.
indications: NSCLC with EGFR exon 19 deletion or exon 21 L858R mutation. Pancreatic cancer (in combination with gemcitabine).
pk: Oral bioavailability 60% with food (100% with food vs. fasted). t½ = 36h. Hepatic metabolism via CYP3A4/1A2. Take on empty stomach (or food consistently—per label).
dosing: NSCLC: 150mg once daily on empty stomach. Pancreatic cancer: 100mg/day with gemcitabine.
renal: No formal adjustment; use caution with severe renal impairment.
hepatic: Reduce dose in moderate-severe hepatic impairment; closely monitor LFTs.
ades: Acneiform/papulopustular rash (70-80% — correlates with response!), diarrhea, mucositis, fatigue. Interstitial lung disease (ILD, rare, 3-4% — can be fatal). Hypomagnesemia.
monitoring: LFTs (hepatotoxicity risk), monitor for ILD (new dyspnea, cough, fever). Monitor skin rash as predictor of response.
ddi: CYP3A4 inducers (rifampin) reduce erlotinib levels significantly. PPI and H2-blockers reduce absorption (pH-dependent). Warfarin: INR changes (CYP2C9).
pearls: Now largely replaced by osimertinib (3rd gen EGFR TKI) for EGFR-mutated NSCLC. Rash severity correlates with treatment response (paradoxically, worse rash = better outcome). ILD requires immediate discontinuation. Avoid PPIs if possible.
highyield:
• EGFR exon 19 del or L858R = required biomarker for use
• Acneiform rash in ~75% (correlates with response—do not dose-reduce for rash alone)
• ILD (3-4%): discontinue immediately if suspected
• Take on EMPTY stomach; PPIs reduce absorption

name: Levonorgestrel (Emergency Contraception)
moa: Primarily inhibits or delays ovulation. May also prevent fertilization. Does NOT prevent implantation of a fertilized egg (no abortifacient effect if already pregnant).
indications: Emergency contraception: reduces pregnancy risk after unprotected intercourse or contraceptive failure.
pk: Rapid oral absorption. t½ = 24-32h. Hepatic metabolism. No significant DDI at single dose.
dosing: Plan B: 1.5mg single oral dose as soon as possible, within 72 hours (effective up to 120 hours, decreasing efficacy with time). Take any time in menstrual cycle.
renal: No adjustment needed for single dose.
hepatic: Single dose well tolerated even with hepatic impairment.
ades: Nausea/vomiting (most common), irregular bleeding, headache, dizziness, breast tenderness. Menstrual cycle may be early or late.
monitoring: Pregnancy test if next menses >1 week late. No lab monitoring needed.
ddi: Enzyme inducers (rifampin, phenytoin, St. Johns wort) reduce efficacy — consider copper IUD as alternative.
pearls: Most effective within 72h (89% effective); 120h still effective (57-63%). Weight limitation: less effective in patients >165-175 lbs (BMI ≥26-30); copper IUD (>99% effective up to 5 days) preferred for higher BMI. Does NOT affect existing pregnancy. OTC in all states.
highyield:
• Does NOT cause abortion — does not affect implanted embryo
• Efficacy decreases with time: 95% <24h, 85% 25-48h, 58% 49-72h
• Enzyme inducers (rifampin, carbamazepine) reduce efficacy → recommend copper IUD
• Reduced efficacy at high BMI (>165-175 lb) → copper IUD preferred
